Apply chain rule:

$$\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = 2a \cdot (-y^{-2})\frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{2a}{y^2}\cdot\frac{dy}{dx}$$

Substitute $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{2a}{y}$:

$$\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -\frac{2a}{y^2}\cdot \frac{2a}{y} = -\frac{4a^2}{y^3}$$

Using the constraint $y^2 = 4ax$, we can write $a = \dfrac{y^2}{4x}$, so:

$$4a^2 = 4 \cdot \frac{y^4}{16x^2} = \frac{y^4}{4x^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -\frac{y^4}{4x^2 y^3} = -\frac{y}{4x^2}$$

Or leaving in terms of $a$ and $x$, using $y^2 = 4ax \Rightarrow y = 2\sqrt{ax}$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -\frac{4a^2}{y^3} = -\frac{1}{4a}\cdot \frac{4a^2}{x^{3/2}} \cdot \text{...} = -\frac{y}{4x^2}\;}$$

> *(Award 2 marks for first derivative, 3 marks for differentiating the second time, 2 marks for substitution and final form.)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in GAMAT101 Implicit Differentiation Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the chain rule on $y^n$** ŌĆö Writing $\dfrac{d}{dx}(y^2) = 2y$ instead of $2y\,\dfrac{dy}{dx}$. This single error costs 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 2. **Dropping the IFT condition $F_y \neq 0$** ŌĆö When asked to state the IFT, students write the formula but forget the *hypothesis*. The condition is worth 1 mark by itself.
> 3. **Premature substitution of $y$ in the second derivative** ŌĆö If the problem gives $F(x,y)=0$ and asks for $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$, do **not** try to express $y$ as a function of $x$. Leave $y$ symbolic.
> 4. **Sign errors during factoring** ŌĆö When moving $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ terms across the equals sign, students often forget to flip the sign. Double-check by substituting a test point.
> 5. **Forgetting the product rule on $xy$** ŌĆö $\dfrac{d}{dx}(xy) = y + x\,\dfrac{dy}{dx}$, **not** $y$.
> 6. **Not simplifying with the original constraint** ŌĆö When asked for $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ in *closed form*, you **must** use $F(x,y)=0$ to eliminate terms. Failure to do so loses the final 1ŌĆō2 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Implicit differentiation is mandatory** when $y$ cannot be isolated as $y = f(x)$.
- [x] **Always apply the chain rule** to terms containing $y$: multiply by $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$.
- [x] **Use the product rule** on any $x \cdot y$ term: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(xy) = y + x\,\dfrac{dy}{dx}$.
- [x] **The IFT formula** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -\dfrac{F_x}{F_y}$ requires $F_y \neq 0$ at the point.
- [x] **Differentiate $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ again** to get $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ ŌĆö treat $y$ as $y(x)$ throughout.
- [x] **Substitute the original constraint** $F(x,y) = 0$ at the *end* to simplify second derivatives.
- [x] **Common derivatives to memorize:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(y^n) = ny^{n-1}\dfrac{dy}{dx}$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(e^y) = e^y\dfrac{dy}{dx}$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\ln y) = \dfrac{1}{y}\dfrac{dy}{dx}$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin y) = \cos y\,\dfrac{dy}{dx}$.
- [x] **Trig chain rule quick reference:** derivative of $\sin y$ is $\cos y \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx}$, derivative of $\cos y$ is $-\sin y \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx}$.
- [x] **Engineering relevance:** Implicit differentiation is the foundation of **gradient computation, curve rendering, and constrained optimization** in information science and engineering.
- [x] **Exam mantra:** *Differentiate everything w.r.t. $x$, chain on every $y$, collect, factor, solve.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tangents and Normal Lines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE ŌĆō 1
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tangents and Normal Lines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In Differential Calculus, given a smooth continuous curve $y = f(x)$ at a point $P(x_1, y_1)$ on the curve, two fundamental geometric constructs are defined as follows:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tangent Line (KTU Definition):** The tangent to a curve $y = f(x)$ at a point $P(x_1, y_1)$ is the straight line that touches the curve exactly at $P$ and has the **same instantaneous rate of change** (slope) as the curve at that point. Its slope is mathematically given by $m_{tan} = \left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{(x_1, y_1)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normal Line (KTU Definition):** The normal to a curve $y = f(x)$ at a point $P(x_1, y_1)$ is the straight line that passes through $P$ and is **perpendicular to the tangent** at that point. Its slope is given by $m_{norm} = -\dfrac{1}{m_{tan}} = -\dfrac{1}{\left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{(x_1, y_1)}}$, provided the tangent is not horizontal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car along a curved hilly road. At every instant, the steering wheel of your car aligns with a particular direction ŌĆö that direction is the **tangent direction**. If you draw a chalk line on the ground that just grazes the road at that exact spot, that is the **tangent line**. The **normal line** is like a flag pole planted vertically at that spot, standing perpendicular to the road surface, indicating the direction "straight up from the curve".

- **Slope of Tangent** = the *steepness* of the road at that instant.
- **Slope of Normal** = the *steepness of a line at right angles* to the road.

### 1.3 The Four Standard Lengths at a Point on a Curve

> [!NOTE]
> At any point $P(x_1, y_1)$ on $y = f(x)$, four standard directed lengths are studied:

| Geometric Length | Standard Notation | Intuitive Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Length of Tangent** | $LT$ or $\vert PT \vert$ | Distance from $P$ to the point $T$ where the tangent meets the $x$-axis. |
| **Length of Normal** | $LN$ or $\vert PN \vert$ | Distance from $P$ to the point $N$ where the normal meets the $x$-axis. |
| **Sub-tangent** | $ST$ or $\vert TM \vert$ | Projection of $PT$ on the $x$-axis (i.e., $\vert x_1 - x_T \vert$). |
| **Sub-normal** | $SN$ or $\vert MN \vert$ | Projection of $PN$ on the $x$-axis (i.e., $\vert x_1 - x_N \vert$). |

These lengths have a constant engineering metric in Cartesian coordinates, with no additional physical constants required (assumed **units: coordinate units**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Theory ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

The construction of tangent and normal relies on the following chain of reasoning:

1. **Step 1 ŌĆö Existence Check:** Confirm that the function $f(x)$ is *differentiable* at $x = x_1$. If $\left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x_1}$ exists finitely, the tangent exists. If the derivative is infinite, the tangent is **vertical** (parallel to $y$-axis).
2. **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Slope of Tangent:** Evaluate the derivative at the point of contact.
3. **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Slope of Normal:** Take the negative reciprocal of the tangent's slope.
4. **Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Point-Slope Form:** Substitute $(x_1, y_1)$ and the slope into $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$.
5. **Step 5 ŌĆö Derive Lengths:** Use the geometric relationship between similar triangles formed by the tangent/normal with the coordinate axes.

### 2.2 Standard KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas assume $y = f(x)$ differentiable at $P(x_1, y_1)$ and $y_1 \neq 0$ where required.**

| # | Quantity | Formula (LaTeX) | Conditions / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slope of Tangent | $m_{tan} = \dfrac{dy}{dx} \bigg\vert_{(x_1, y_1)}$ | Finite value required |
| 2 | Slope of Normal | $m_{norm} = -\dfrac{1}{m_{tan}}$ | Valid only if $m_{tan} \neq 0$ |
| 3 | Equation of Tangent | $y - y_1 = m_{tan}(x - x_1)$ | Point-slope form |
| 4 | Equation of Normal | $y - y_1 = -\dfrac{1}{m_{tan}}(x - x_1)$ | Vertical if $m_{tan} = 0$ |
| 5 | Sub-tangent | $ST = \dfrac{y_1}{dy/dx} \bigg\vert_{P}$ | Signed length on $x$-axis |
| 6 | Sub-normal | $SN = y_1 \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} \bigg\vert_{P}$ | Signed length on $x$-axis |
| 7 | Length of Tangent | $LT = \dfrac{y_1 \sqrt{1 + (dy/dx)^2}}{\,dy/dx\,} \bigg\vert_{P} = \dfrac{\vert y_1 \vert \sqrt{1 + (dy/dx)^2}}{\vert dy/dx \vert}$ | Always positive |
| 8 | Length of Normal | $LN = \vert y_1 \vert \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2} \bigg\vert_{P}$ | Always positive |

### 2.3 Tangent and Normal for Parametric and Polar Curves

For **parametric curves** $x = x(t), \, y = y(t)$:

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}$$

For **polar curves** $r = f(\theta)$:

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{\dfrac{dr}{d\theta}\sin\theta + r\cos\theta}{\dfrac{dr}{d\theta}\cos\theta - r\sin\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises the Cartesian form. Parametric form is included for higher-order Part B problems.

### 2.4 Angle of Intersection of Two Curves

If two curves $C_1$ and $C_2$ intersect at a common point, and their respective tangent slopes at that point are $m_1$ and $m_2$, the **acute angle of intersection** $\theta$ is given by:

$$\tan\theta = \left\vert \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert$$

**Special Case ŌĆö Orthogonal Curves:** Two curves are *orthogonal* (intersect at $90^\circ$) iff $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$, i.e., $1 + m_1 m_2 = 0$, making the denominator zero in the formula above (the numerator giving a non-zero value).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer-Aided Design (CAD):** Tangent vectors are essential for spline interpolation, Bezier curves, and smooth path generation in graphics.
- **Robotics:** The path of a robotic arm requires continuous tangent vectors for collision-free motion planning.
- **Machine Learning (Gradient Descent):** The negative gradient (normal to the level set) points in the direction of steepest descent ŌĆö directly analogous to the normal to a level curve.
- **Civil Engineering:** Road design and railway curves require tangent and curvature calculations for safe vehicle dynamics.
- **Signal Processing:** The instantaneous frequency of a signal corresponds to the tangent slope of its phase plot.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Equation of Tangent Line (Cartesian Form)

**Given:** A curve $y = f(x)$ and a point $P(x_1, y_1)$ on it, where $f$ is differentiable at $x_1$.

**Step 1:** By definition of the derivative (limit of secant slopes), the slope of the tangent at $P$ is:

$$m_{tan} = \lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(x_1 + h) - f(x_1)}{h} = \left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{(x_1, y_1)}$$

**Step 2:** Using the standard point-slope form of a straight line, where a line through $(x_1, y_1)$ with slope $m$ has equation $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$:

$$y - y_1 = m_{tan}(x - x_1)$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $m_{tan} = \left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{(x_1, y_1)}$:

$$y - y_1 = \left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{(x_1, y_1)} \cdot (x - x_1)$$

This is the **standard form of the equation of the tangent** at $P(x_1, y_1)$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Equation of Normal Line

**Step 1:** The normal is perpendicular to the tangent. The product of slopes of two perpendicular lines equals $-1$:

$$m_{tan} \cdot m_{norm} = -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{norm} = -\dfrac{1}{m_{tan}}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the point-slope form with $P(x_1, y_1)$:

$$y - y_1 = m_{norm}(x - x_1)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $m_{norm}$:

$$y - y_1 = -\dfrac{1}{\left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{(x_1, y_1)}} \cdot (x - x_1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $\left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{P} = 0$, then $m_{norm}$ is undefined (vertical line), and the normal is the line $x = x_1$ (parallel to $y$-axis).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Length of Sub-tangent ($ST$)

**Geometric Setup:** Let $T$ be the foot of the tangent on the $x$-axis. Then the triangle $\triangle PTM$ (where $M$ is the projection of $P$ on the $x$-axis) is a right-angled triangle at $M$, with:

- Vertical leg: $PM = y_1$
- Horizontal leg: $TM = ST$ (sub-tangent)
- Hypotenuse: $PT = LT$ (length of tangent)
- Angle at $T$: $\theta = \angle PTM$ where $\tan\theta = m_{tan}$

**Step 1:** From the right triangle:

$$\tan\theta = \dfrac{PM}{TM} = \dfrac{y_1}{ST}$$

**Step 2:** But $\tan\theta$ also equals the slope of the tangent:

$$\tan\theta = m_{tan} = \dfrac{dy}{dx} \bigg\vert_{P}$$

**Step 3:** Equating both expressions:

$$m_{tan} = \dfrac{y_1}{ST} \quad \Rightarrow \quad ST = \dfrac{y_1}{m_{tan}} = \dfrac{y_1}{\left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{P}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Length of Sub-normal ($SN$)

**Step 1:** From the same right triangle, the angle at $M$ (between $PM$ and $MT$ extended) satisfies:

$$\tan(\angle PMN) = \dfrac{SN}{PM} = \dfrac{SN}{y_1}$$

**Step 2:** But $\angle PMN = \theta$ (alternate angles, since tangent is transversal to parallel $y$-axis projection), so:

$$\tan\theta = \dfrac{SN}{y_1}$$

**Step 3:** Equating with $m_{tan}$:

$$m_{tan} = \dfrac{SN}{y_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad SN = y_1 \cdot m_{tan} = y_1 \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} \bigg\vert_{P}$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 ŌĆö Length of Tangent ($LT$) and Length of Normal ($LN$)

**Length of Tangent (LT):** Using the right triangle $PTM$ with hypotenuse $PT$:

$$(PT)^2 = (PM)^2 + (TM)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad LT^2 = y_1^2 + ST^2$$

Substituting $ST = y_1 / m_{tan}$:

$$LT^2 = y_1^2 + \dfrac{y_1^2}{m_{tan}^2} = y_1^2 \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{m_{tan}^2}\right) = \dfrac{y_1^2 (1 + m_{tan}^2)}{m_{tan}^2}$$

Taking the positive square root:

$$LT = \dfrac{\vert y_1 \vert \sqrt{1 + m_{tan}^2}}{\vert m_{tan} \vert} = \dfrac{\vert y_1 \vert \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}}{\left\vert \dfrac{dy}{dx} \right\vert} \bigg\vert_{P}$$

**Length of Normal (LN):** From the same triangle with hypotenuse $PN$:

$$(PN)^2 = (PM)^2 + (MN)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad LN^2 = y_1^2 + SN^2$$

Substituting $SN = y_1 \cdot m_{tan}$:

$$LN^2 = y_1^2 + y_1^2 m_{tan}^2 = y_1^2 (1 + m_{tan}^2)$$

Taking the positive square root:

$$LN = \vert y_1 \vert \sqrt{1 + m_{tan}^2} = \vert y_1 \vert \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2} \bigg\vert_{P}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Check ŌĆö Reciprocal Relationship:** Notice that $LT \cdot \vert m_{tan} \vert = LN$. This identity can be used as a verification step in KTU valuation: $LT \cdot (dy/dx) = LN \cdot \text{sign}(dy/dx)$, so $\vert LT \cdot m_{tan} \vert = LN$.

### 3.6 Derivation 6 ŌĆö Angle of Intersection of Two Curves

**Given:** Two curves $C_1: y = f_1(x)$ and $C_2: y = f_2(x)$ intersect at $P(x_1, y_1)$.

**Step 1:** Tangent slopes at $P$:

$$m_1 = \left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{C_1, P}, \quad m_2 = \left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{C_2, P}$$

**Step 2:** If $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ are the angles made by the two tangents with the positive $x$-axis, then $\tan\alpha_1 = m_1$ and $\tan\alpha_2 = m_2$.

**Step 3:** The angle of intersection $\theta$ is the difference $\theta = \alpha_1 - \alpha_2$ (or its supplement; we take the acute angle).

**Step 4:** Using the tangent subtraction identity:

$$\tan(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) = \dfrac{\tan\alpha_1 - \tan\alpha_2}{1 + \tan\alpha_1 \tan\alpha_2} = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2}$$

**Step 5:** Taking absolute value to ensure $\theta$ is acute:

$$\tan\theta = \left\vert \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Edge Case:** If $1 + m_1 m_2 = 0$ and $m_1 \neq m_2$, then the curves are **orthogonal**, and $\theta = 90^\circ$. The formula gives an undefined result, which is the indicator.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Geometric Block Diagram ŌĆö Tangent and Normal at Point P

The following Mermaid block represents the complete functional architecture of tangent/normal construction from a curve to its geometric lengths, mapped as a flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Curve y is f of x] --> B[Select Point P with coordinates x1, y1]
    B --> C[Compute derivative dy by dx at P]
    C --> D{Is derivative finite?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Slope of tangent m equals dy by dx at P]
    D -- No --> F[Vertical tangent parallel to y axis]
    E --> G[Equation of tangent y minus y1 equals m times x minus x1]
    E --> H[Slope of normal equals negative 1 over m]
    H --> I[Equation of normal y minus y1 equals negative 1 over m times x minus x1]
    E --> J[Subtangent ST equals y1 over m]
    E --> K[Subnormal SN equals y1 times m]
    E --> L[Length of tangent LT equals y1 times sqrt of 1 plus m squared over m]
    E --> M[Length of normal LN equals y1 times sqrt of 1 plus m squared]
    G --> N[Output: Tangent Line]
    I --> O[Output: Normal Line]
    J --> P[Output: Four Lengths]
    K --> P
    L --> P
    M --> P
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Two-Curve Intersection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Curve C1: y equals f1 of x] --> P[Common Point P: x1, y1]
    C2[Curve C2: y equals f2 of x] --> P
    P --> S1[Slope m1 of tangent to C1 at P]
    P --> S2[Slope m2 of tangent to C2 at P]
    S1 --> F[Compute tan theta equals mod m1 minus m2 over 1 plus m1 m2]
    S2 --> F
    F --> Q{Is 1 plus m1 m2 equals 0?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Curves are Orthogonal theta equals 90 degrees]
    Q -- No --> S[Curves intersect at angle theta]
```

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic ŌĆö Visualizing $ST$, $SN$, $LT$, $LN$ on a Coordinate Plane

```
            y
            |
         N  |
         |  |
         |  |         T (Tangent meets x-axis)
         |  |        /
         |  |       /
         |  |      /  (Tangent Line)
         |  |     /
         |  |    /
         |  |   /
         |  |  /
         |  | / 
         |  |/__________ x
         |  M     T    N
         | (x1, 0) (xT, 0)  (xN, 0)
         |
         |  P (x1, y1) lies on the curve
         |
         |
       (Origin)
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- $P$ is the point of contact on the curve.
- $M$ is the foot of the perpendicular from $P$ to the $x$-axis (so $M = (x_1, 0)$).
- $T$ is where the tangent line meets the $x$-axis. Hence $\overline{MT} = ST$ (sub-tangent).
- $N$ is where the normal line meets the $x$-axis. Hence $\overline{MN} = SN$ (sub-normal).
- $\overline{PT} = LT$ (length of tangent) ŌĆö hypotenuse of right triangle $\triangle PMT$.
- $\overline{PN} = LN$ (length of normal) ŌĆö hypotenuse of right triangle $\triangle PMN$.

### 4.4 Flowchart ŌĆö Decision Logic for Tangent Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given curve y is f of x and point P] --> B[Evaluate dy by dx at P]
    B --> C{Value of derivative}
    C -- Finite non-zero --> D[Standard tangent: y minus y1 equals m times x minus x1]
    C -- Zero --> E[Horizontal tangent: y equals y1]
    C -- Infinite undefined --> F[Vertical tangent: x equals x1]
    C -- Does not exist --> G[Tangent does not exist at P]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]**
*Define the terms "tangent" and "normal" to a curve $y = f(x)$ at a point $P(x_1, y_1)$. Write down the equations of both lines in terms of the derivative $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$.*

**Model Answer:**

The **tangent** to a curve $y = f(x)$ at a point $P(x_1, y_1)$ is the straight line that touches the curve at $P$ and has the same slope as the curve at that point.

The **normal** to the curve at $P$ is the straight line passing through $P$ and perpendicular to the tangent at $P$.

The slope of the tangent is $m_{tan} = \left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{(x_1, y_1)}$, and the slope of the normal is $m_{norm} = -\dfrac{1}{\left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{(x_1, y_1)}}$.

Therefore, the equations are:

$$y - y_1 = \left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{(x_1, y_1)} (x - x_1) \quad \text{(Tangent)}$$

$$y - y_1 = -\dfrac{1}{\left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{(x_1, y_1)}} (x - x_1) \quad \text{(Normal)}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**
*What is the sub-tangent and sub-normal of a curve? State their formulas at a point $P(x_1, y_1)$.*

**Model Answer:**

The **sub-tangent** at $P(x_1, y_1)$ is the projection of the length of the tangent $PT$ onto the $x$-axis, denoted by $ST$ or $\vert TM \vert$.

The **sub-normal** at $P(x_1, y_1)$ is the projection of the length of the normal $PN$ onto the $x$-axis, denoted by $SN$ or $\vert MN \vert$.

The formulas are:

$$ST = \dfrac{y_1}{\left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{P}}, \quad SN = y_1 \cdot \left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_{P}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes:**
> 1. **Sign Errors in $ST$ and $SN$:** Students often forget that $ST$ involves $1/m_{tan}$ in the denominator while $SN$ involves $m_{tan}$ in the numerator. This swap costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Absolute Value Confusion:** Lengths must always be positive. The final answer for $LT$ and $LN$ requires $\vert \cdot \vert$ (modulus). Omitting this loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Point-Slope Form Substitution:** Substituting $x_1$ and $y_1$ in the *wrong slot* (e.g., using $x$ instead of $x_1$) costs **1ŌĆō2 marks**.
> 4. **Angle of Intersection ŌĆö Acute Angle:** Forgetting the modulus on the $\tan\theta$ formula leads to a *negative* or *obtuse* answer, which is marked wrong. Always take the acute angle.
> 5. **Vertical/Horizontal Tangent Edge Cases:** Not checking whether $\left.\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right|_P = 0$ or $\infty$ before applying the normal formula.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, May 2024 Style]*

**(a)** Find the equation of the tangent and normal to the curve $y = x^2 - 4x + 7$ at the point where $x = 2$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Find the lengths of the sub-tangent, sub-normal, tangent, and normal at the point $P(1, 2)$ on the curve $y^2 = 4x$. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Solution:**

The curve is $y = x^2 - 4x + 7$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find $y_1$ at $x = 2$:**

$$y_1 = (2)^2 - 4(2) + 7 = 4 - 8 + 7 = 3$$

So the point of contact is $P(2, 3)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the derivative:**

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 2x - 4$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate the slope of the tangent at $P$:**

$$m_{tan} = 2(2) - 4 = 4 - 4 = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Equation of the tangent (horizontal line, since $m_{tan} = 0$):**

$$y - 3 = 0 \cdot (x - 2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{y = 3}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Slope of the normal:** Since $m_{tan} = 0$, the normal is **vertical** (parallel to $y$-axis):

$$\boxed{x = 2}$$

**(b) Solution:**

The curve is $y^2 = 4x$ at $P(1, 2)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Differentiate implicitly:**

$$2y \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{4}{2y} = \dfrac{2}{y}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Slope of tangent at $P(1, 2)$:**

$$m_{tan} = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the four lengths:**

- **Sub-tangent:**

$$ST = \dfrac{y_1}{m_{tan}} = \dfrac{2}{1} = \boxed{2 \text{ units}}$$

- **Sub-normal:**

$$SN = y_1 \cdot m_{tan} = 2 \cdot 1 = \boxed{2 \text{ units}}$$

- **Length of Tangent:**

$$LT = \dfrac{\vert y_1 \vert \sqrt{1 + m_{tan}^2}}{\vert m_{tan} \vert} = \dfrac{2 \sqrt{1 + 1}}{1} = 2\sqrt{2} \text{ units}$$

- **Length of Normal:**

$$LN = \vert y_1 \vert \sqrt{1 + m_{tan}^2} = 2 \sqrt{1 + 1} = \boxed{2\sqrt{2} \text{ units}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question, May 2024 Style ŌĆö Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Find the angle of intersection between the curves $y^2 = 4x$ and $x^2 = 4y$ at their common point(s) other than the origin. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Show that the curves $xy = a^2$ and $x^2 + y^2 = 2a^2$ intersect orthogonally at their common points. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find common points of intersection (other than origin):**

From $y^2 = 4x$, we have $x = y^2 / 4$. Substituting into $x^2 = 4y$:

$$\left(\dfrac{y^2}{4}\right)^2 = 4y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{y^4}{16} = 4y \quad \Rightarrow \quad y^4 = 64 y \quad \Rightarrow \quad y(y^3 - 64) = 0$$

So $y = 0$ (origin) or $y^3 = 64 \Rightarrow y = 4$. For $y = 4$, $x = y^2/4 = 16/4 = 4$.

Common point: $P(4, 4)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Slopes at $P(4, 4)$:**

For $y^2 = 4x$:

$$2y \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_1 = \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{2}{y} = \dfrac{2}{4} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

For $x^2 = 4y$:

$$2x = 4 \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_2 = \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{x}{2} = \dfrac{4}{2} = 2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the angle of intersection:**

$$\tan\theta = \left\vert \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert = \left\vert \dfrac{\frac{1}{2} - 2}{1 + \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2} \right\vert = \left\vert \dfrac{-\frac{3}{2}}{1 + 1} \right\vert = \left\vert \dfrac{-\frac{3}{2}}{2} \right\vert = \dfrac{3}{4}$$

Therefore:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right) \approx 36.87^\circ$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right) \approx 36^\circ \, 52'}$$

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find the common points of intersection:**

The two curves are $C_1: xy = a^2$ and $C_2: x^2 + y^2 = 2a^2$.

Multiply $C_1$ by $2$: $2xy = 2a^2$. Add to $C_2$:

$$x^2 + 2xy + y^2 = 2a^2 + 2a^2 = 4a^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (x + y)^2 = 4a^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x + y = \pm 2a$$

From $xy = a^2$, the values of $x$ and $y$ are the roots of $t^2 \mp 2at + a^2 = 0$, i.e., $(t \mp a)^2 = 0$, giving $x = y = a$ or $x = y = -a$.

**Common points:** $P_1(a, a)$ and $P_2(-a, -a)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Slopes of tangents at $P_1(a, a)$:**

For $C_1: xy = a^2$:

$$y + x \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_1 = -\dfrac{y}{x} = -\dfrac{a}{a} = -1$$

For $C_2: x^2 + y^2 = 2a^2$:

$$2x + 2y \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_2 = -\dfrac{x}{y} = -\dfrac{a}{a} = -1$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verify orthogonality:**

$$m_1 \cdot m_2 = (-1)(-1) = 1 \neq -1$$

Hmm, this gives $1$, not $-1$. Let us check the other point $P_2(-a, -a)$:

For $C_1$ at $P_2$: $m_1 = -\dfrac{y}{x} = -\dfrac{-a}{-a} = -1$

For $C_2$ at $P_2$: $m_2 = -\dfrac{x}{y} = -\dfrac{-a}{-a} = -1$

So $m_1 m_2 = 1$ at both points, which means the curves are **tangent** (coincident slope) at these points ŌĆö they touch, not cross. The orthogonality claim requires a re-examination of the problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction (Examiner's Note):** The curves $xy = a^2$ and $x^2 + y^2 = 2a^2$ do not intersect orthogonally at $(a, a)$ or $(-a, -a)$ since both tangents have slope $-1$, making them **tangent to each other** (not orthogonal). The standard KTU problem uses $x^2 + y^2 = 2a^2$ replaced with a circle that genuinely intersects the hyperbola orthogonally. The corrected problem should use $x^2 - y^2 = a^2$ which gives $m_2 = x/y = a/a = 1$, so $m_1 m_2 = -1 \cdot 1 = -1$, confirming orthogonality. **End of correction.**

**Corrected Part (b) ŌĆö Verification of orthogonality with $C_2: x^2 - y^2 = a^2$:**

Common points remain $P_1(a, a)$ and $P_2(-a, -a)$.

Slope on $C_1$ at $P_1$: $m_1 = -y/x = -1$ (as above).

Slope on $C_2: x^2 - y^2 = a^2$:

$$2x - 2y \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_2 = \dfrac{x}{y} = \dfrac{a}{a} = 1$$

**Product of slopes:**

$$m_1 \cdot m_2 = (-1)(1) = -1$$

Hence the curves are **orthogonal** at $P_1(a, a)$.

By symmetry, the same holds at $P_2(-a, -a)$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision ŌĆö Tangents and Normal Lines**

- Ō£ģ **Tangent slope** = $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\bigg\vert_P$ ŌĆö must be evaluated *at* the point of contact, not in general.
- Ō£ģ **Normal slope** = $-\dfrac{1}{(dy/dx)\vert_P}$ ŌĆö take the **negative reciprocal**, not the reciprocal.
- Ō£ģ **Sub-tangent** $ST = \dfrac{y_1}{(dy/dx)\vert_P}$ ŌĆö the derivative is in the *denominator*.
- Ō£ģ **Sub-normal** $SN = y_1 \cdot (dy/dx)\vert_P$ ŌĆö the derivative is in the *numerator*.
- Ō£ģ **Reciprocal identity:** $ST \cdot SN = y_1^2$ (useful verification).
- Ō£ģ **Length of Normal** $LN = \vert y_1 \vert \sqrt{1 + (dy/dx)^2}$ ŌĆö always non-negative.
- Ō£ģ **Length of Tangent** $LT = \dfrac{LN}{\vert dy/dx \vert} = \dfrac{\vert y_1 \vert \sqrt{1 + (dy/dx)^2}}{\vert dy/dx \vert}$.
- Ō£ģ **Angle of intersection:** $\tan\theta = \left\vert \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{1 + m_1 m_2} \right\vert$ ŌĆö always take the **absolute value** for the acute angle.
- Ō£ģ **Orthogonal curves:** $m_1 m_2 = -1$ (denominator becomes $0$).
- Ō£ģ **Vertical tangent:** when $dy/dx = \infty$ (e.g., at the vertex of a sideways parabola).
- Ō£ģ **Horizontal tangent:** when $dy/dx = 0$ (stationary point); the normal is then vertical $x = x_1$.
- Ō£ģ **Parametric form:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}$.
- Ō£ģ **Polar form:** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{(dr/d\theta)\sin\theta + r\cos\theta}{(dr/d\theta)\cos\theta - r\sin\theta}$.
- Ō£ģ Always **state the units** (coordinate units) for lengths $LT$, $LN$, $ST$, $SN$.
- Ō£ģ For KTU 2024 ESE: write $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ with explicit *evaluation* notation (e.g., "at $P(1, 1)$") to earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linearization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE ŌĆō 1
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linearization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Linearization

In the calculus framework prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Linearization** is the process of approximating a differentiable function $f(x)$ near a point $x = a$ by a linear function (the equation of the tangent line) whose value and slope match those of $f$ at $x = a$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Linearization** of a function $f(x)$ at a point $x = a$ is the linear function:
> $$L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$$
> provided $f$ is differentiable at $x = a$. The function $L(x)$ is called the **Linear Approximation** or **Tangent Line Approximation** of $f$ near $a$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Perspective)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö The "Flat Earth" Assumption:**
> Imagine you are standing at the top of a curved hill at point $A$. A surveyor wishes to measure the height of a point $B$ located a few meters away from $A$. Instead of doing complex surveying to find the true curved distance, the surveyor simply looks at the hill as if it were **locally flat** near $A$. The height of $B$ is then estimated using the *slope* of the hill at $A$. This "flat patch" is exactly what **linearization** does mathematically ŌĆö it replaces a curved function with a flat (linear) tangent line for values of $x$ close to $a$.

## 1.3 Why Linearization Matters in Information Science

In information science, many **non-linear functions** (such as $\sqrt{x}$, $\sin x$, $e^x$, $\ln x$) are computationally expensive. Linearization allows:

- **Fast Approximations** in machine learning algorithms (e.g., Taylor-based optimization).
- **Error Estimation** in numerical methods.
- **Stability Analysis** in control systems and signal processing.

The standard **metric of accuracy** used in KTU examinations is the remainder term:
$$R_n(x) = f(x) - L(x)$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ŌĆö The Why and How

Linearization is derived directly from the **geometric definition of the derivative**. Recall that the slope of the tangent line to $y = f(x)$ at $x = a$ is $f'(a)$. The equation of a line with slope $m$ passing through $(a, f(a))$ is given by point-slope form.

**Logical Steps:**

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Base Point Selection:** Choose a base point $x = a$ close to the point of interest $x$. The closer $a$ is to $x$, the more accurate the approximation.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Match Function Value:** The linear function must pass through $(a, f(a))$. So $L(a) = f(a)$.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Match Derivative:** The slope of $L$ at $a$ must equal the slope of $f$ at $a$. So $L'(a) = f'(a)$.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Construct the Line:** A linear function has the form $L(x) = mx + c$. Using the two conditions above:
  * $L(a) = ma + c = f(a)$
  * $L'(a) = m = f'(a)$
  * Therefore: $c = f(a) - f'(a) \cdot a$.
  * Hence: $L(x) = f'(a) \cdot x + f(a) - f'(a) \cdot a = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$.

## 2.2 The Role of Differentials in Linearization

The **differential** of $f$ at $x = a$ is defined as:
$$dy = f'(a) \, dx$$
The change in $y$ is approximated as:
$$\Delta y \approx dy = f'(a) \, \Delta x$$
This is the **practical workhorse** of linearization in numerical computation.

## 2.3 Linearization Using the Differential Form

For small increments $\Delta x = x - a$:
$$f(x) \approx f(a) + dy = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$$

## 2.4 Error Bound for Linear Approximation

If $f''(x)$ is bounded on an interval containing $a$, the **maximum error** is given by:
$$E(x) = \vert f(x) - L(x) \vert \leq \frac{M}{2} \vert x - a \vert^2$$
where $M = \max \vert f''(t) \vert$ for $t$ between $a$ and $x$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Use of Linearization |
|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Signal Processing** | Linearizing non-linear amplifiers for small-signal analysis |
| **Computer Graphics** | Approximating curves near a point for fast rendering |
| **Machine Learning** | First-order Taylor approximation in gradient descent |
| **Control Systems** | Small-signal linearization of non-linear plant models |
| **Physics/Optics** | Approximating $\sin \theta \approx \theta$ for small angles |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Conditions |
|---------|---------|------------|
| Linearization at $a$ | $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$ | $f$ differentiable at $a$ |
| Differential form | $dy = f'(x) \, dx$ | $x$ is any point in domain |
| Change approximation | $\Delta y \approx f'(a) \, \Delta x$ | $\Delta x$ is small |
| Relative error | $\dfrac{\Delta y}{y} \approx \dfrac{f'(a)}{f(a)} \Delta x$ | $f(a) \neq 0$ |
| Error bound | $\vert f(x) - L(x) \vert \leq \dfrac{M}{2} \vert x - a \vert^2$ | $\vert f''(t) \vert \leq M$ |
| Square root approx. | $\sqrt{x} \approx \sqrt{a} + \dfrac{x - a}{2\sqrt{a}}$ | $a > 0$, $x$ near $a$ |
| Cube root approx. | $\sqrt[3]{x} \approx \sqrt[3]{a} + \dfrac{x - a}{3\sqrt[3]{a^2}}$ | $a \neq 0$ |
| Trigonometric approx. | $\sin x \approx x$ | $x$ in radians, $x \approx 0$ |
| Exponential approx. | $e^x \approx 1 + x$ | $x$ near $0$ |
| Logarithmic approx. | $\ln x \approx (x - 1)$ | $x$ near $1$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Derivation of the Linearization Formula

**Goal:** Show that $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$.

**Step 1:** Let $L(x)$ be a linear function of the form $L(x) = mx + c$, where $m$ and $c$ are constants to be determined.

**Step 2:** The condition $L(a) = f(a)$ (linearization matches the function value at $a$):
$$L(a) = ma + c = f(a)$$

**Step 3:** The condition $L'(a) = f'(a)$ (linearization matches the slope at $a$):
$$L'(a) = m = f'(a)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $m = f'(a)$ into the equation from Step 2:
$$f'(a) \cdot a + c = f(a)$$
$$c = f(a) - f'(a) \cdot a$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $m$ and $c$ into $L(x) = mx + c$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L(x) &= f'(a) \cdot x + f(a) - f'(a) \cdot a \\
&= f(a) + f'(a)\bigl(x - a\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The linearization of $f$ at $a$ is $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Error Bound

**Step 1:** By the **Mean Value Theorem**, since $f$ is continuous on $[a, x]$ and differentiable on $(a, x)$, there exists a point $c$ between $a$ and $x$ such that:
$$f(x) - f(a) = f'(c)(x - a)$$

**Step 2:** Subtract the linearization $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$ from both sides:
$$f(x) - L(x) = \bigl[f'(c) - f'(a)\bigr](x - a)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **Mean Value Theorem** to $f'$ on the interval between $a$ and $c$. There exists $\xi$ between $a$ and $c$ (and hence between $a$ and $x$) such that:
$$f'(c) - f'(a) = f''(\xi)(c - a)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) - L(x) &= f''(\xi)(c - a)(x - a)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Take absolute values. Since $\vert c - a \vert \leq \vert x - a \vert$:
$$\vert f(x) - L(x) \vert = \vert f''(\xi) \vert \cdot \vert c - a \vert \cdot \vert x - a \vert \leq M \vert x - a \vert^2$$

This gives the **error bound** used in KTU problems. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Examples (KTU Standard)

### Example 1 ŌĆö Linearization of $\sqrt{x}$ at $a = 4$

**Step 1:** Define $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ and $a = 4$.

**Step 2:** Compute $f(4) = \sqrt{4} = 2$.

**Step 3:** Compute $f'(x) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$, so $f'(4) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{4}} = \dfrac{1}{4}$.

**Step 4:** Apply the linearization formula:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L(x) &= f(4) + f'(4)(x - 4) \\
&= 2 + \tfrac{1}{4}(x - 4) \\
&= 2 + \tfrac{x}{4} - 1 \\
&= 1 + \tfrac{x}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Approximate $\sqrt{4.1}$:
$$L(4.1) = 1 + \frac{4.1}{4} = 1 + 1.025 = 2.025$$
The true value is $\sqrt{4.1} \approx 2.0248$. The error is roughly $0.0002$, which is excellent.

### Example 2 ŌĆö Linearization of $\sin x$ at $a = 0$

**Step 1:** $f(x) = \sin x$, $a = 0$.

**Step 2:** $f(0) = \sin 0 = 0$.

**Step 3:** $f'(x) = \cos x$, so $f'(0) = \cos 0 = 1$.

**Step 4:** Linearization:
$$L(x) = 0 + 1 \cdot (x - 0) = x$$
This yields the famous approximation $\sin x \approx x$ for $x$ near $0$.

### Example 3 ŌĆö Error in Approximating $(1.98)^3$ Using $a = 2$

**Step 1:** Let $f(x) = x^3$, $a = 2$, $x = 1.98$. Note $\Delta x = -0.02$.

**Step 2:** $f(2) = 8$, $f'(x) = 3x^2$, so $f'(2) = 12$.

**Step 3:** Linear approximation:
$$L(1.98) = 8 + 12(1.98 - 2) = 8 + 12(-0.02) = 8 - 0.24 = 7.76$$

**Step 4:** True value: $(1.98)^3 = 7.762392$. Error $\approx 0.002392$.

**Step 5:** Bound check: $f''(x) = 6x$, maximum on $[1.98, 2]$ is $M = 12$.
$$E \leq \frac{12}{2}(0.02)^2 = 6 \cdot 0.0004 = 0.0024$$
The actual error $0.002392$ is indeed $\leq 0.0024$. Ō£ō

### Example 4 ŌĆö Estimating Percentage Error

The radius of a sphere is measured as $r = 5$ cm with a possible error of $\pm 0.05$ cm. Estimate the maximum error in volume.

**Step 1:** Volume $V(r) = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$.

**Step 2:** Differential: $dV = V'(r) \, dr = 4\pi r^2 \, dr$.

**Step 3:** At $r = 5$, $dr = 0.05$:
$$dV = 4\pi (25)(0.05) = 5\pi \approx 15.708 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 4:** Percentage error:
$$\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{4\pi r^2 \, dr}{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3} = \frac{3 \, dr}{r} = \frac{3(0.05)}{5} = 0.03 = 3\%$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Flow: The Linearization Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete operational flow of constructing a linearization, from input function to error analysis. This represents the conceptual architecture used in KTU valuation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Function f of x] --> B{Is f differentiable at point a?}
    B -- No --> C[STOP: Linearization not possible at a]
    B -- Yes --> D[Compute f at a]
    D --> E[Compute f prime at a]
    E --> F[Compute slope m = f prime of a]
    D --> G[Compute intercept c = f of a minus m times a]
    F --> H[Construct L of x = m times x plus c]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Form: L of x = f of a + f prime of a times x minus a]
    I --> J{Is point of interest x close to a?}
    J -- No --> K[Apply higher order Taylor expansion]
    J -- Yes --> L[Evaluate L at x to get approximation]
    L --> M[Compute error E = f of x minus L of x]
    M --> N{Bound required?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Compute M = max of f double prime]
    O --> P[Apply error bound E less than or equal to M over 2 times delta x squared]
    N -- No --> Q[Return Approximation Value]
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map: Relationship Between Linearization, Differentials, and Taylor Series

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Differentiation] --> B[Definition of Derivative]
    B --> C[Slope at Point a]
    C --> D[Tangent Line Equation]
    D --> E[Linearization L of x]
    E --> F[Approximation f of x approx L of x]
    F --> G[Differential dy = f prime dx]
    G --> H[Error Analysis]
    H --> I[Error Bound Using f double prime]
    E --> J[Generalization]
    J --> K[Taylor Series: Higher Order Terms]
    K --> L[Quadratic Approximation]
    K --> M[Cubic Approximation]
```

## 4.3 Geometric Interpretation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Curve Region
        P1[Point A on curve f of x]:::curve
        P2[Point X near A]:::curve
    end
    subgraph Tangent Region
        T1[Tangent Line L of x at A]:::line
        T2[Approximated Point L of X]:::line
    end
    P1 -- equals --> T1
    P2 -- approximated by --> T2
    T2 -- error E --> P2
    classDef curve fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef line fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The diagram visually represents that the true curve value $f(X)$ is *approximated* by the tangent line value $L(X)$, with the **error $E$** being the small vertical gap between them. As $X \to A$, the error shrinks to zero quadratically.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar]`
**Define linearization of a function $f(x)$ at a point $x = a$. State the formula and the condition under which it is valid.**

**Model Answer:**

Linearization is the process of approximating a differentiable function $f(x)$ near a point $x = a$ by a linear function $L(x)$ whose value and slope match those of $f$ at $a$.

**Formula:** $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$.

**Condition:** $f$ must be **differentiable** at $x = a$, ensuring $f'(a)$ exists and is finite. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar]`
**Use linearization to approximate $\sqrt{25.2}$.**

**Model Answer:**

Let $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$, $a = 25$, $x = 25.2$. Then $f(25) = 5$, $f'(x) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$, so $f'(25) = \dfrac{1}{10}$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
L(25.2) &= 5 + \tfrac{1}{10}(25.2 - 25) \\
&= 5 + 0.02 \\
&= 5.02
\end{aligned}
$$
*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Find the linearization of $f(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$ at $a = 8$. Use it to approximate $\sqrt[3]{7.9}$ and estimate the error bound. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The side of a cube is measured as $12$ cm with a possible error of $\pm 0.02$ cm. Use differentials to estimate the **maximum error** and **percentage error** in the calculated volume. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** $f(x) = x^{1/3}$, $a = 8$, so $f(8) = 2$.

**Step 2:** $f'(x) = \dfrac{1}{3}x^{-2/3} = \dfrac{1}{3\sqrt[3]{x^2}}$, so $f'(8) = \dfrac{1}{3(4)} = \dfrac{1}{12}$.

**Step 3:** Linearization:
$$
L(x) = 2 + \tfrac{1}{12}(x - 8)
$$

**Step 4:** Approximate $\sqrt[3]{7.9}$:
$$L(7.9) = 2 + \tfrac{1}{12}(-0.1) = 2 - 0.00833 = 1.99167$$

**Step 5:** Error bound. Compute $f''(x) = -\dfrac{2}{9}x^{-5/3}$. On $[7.9, 8]$, maximum absolute value occurs at $x = 7.9$:
$$M = \frac{2}{9(7.9)^{5/3}} \approx \frac{2}{9 \cdot 41.85} \approx 0.00531$$
$$E \leq \frac{0.00531}{2}(0.1)^2 \approx 2.65 \times 10^{-5}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Volume $V = s^3$. Differential: $dV = 3s^2 \, ds$.

**Step 2:** At $s = 12$, $ds = 0.02$:
$$dV = 3(144)(0.02) = 8.64 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3:** Percentage error:
$$\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{3s^2 \, ds}{s^3} = \frac{3 \, ds}{s} = \frac{3(0.02)}{12} = 0.005 = 0.5\%$$

**Maximum error** $= 8.64$ cm┬│, **Percentage error** $= 0.5\%$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the linearization formula $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$ from first principles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the linearization of $f(x) = e^{0.3x}\cos(2x)$ at $a = 0$. Use it to estimate $f(0.05)$ and compare with the true value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

*(See full derivation in SECTION 3.1 above. The student is expected to:)*

- Assume $L(x) = mx + c$ (linear form). *(1 mark)*
- Impose $L(a) = f(a) \Rightarrow ma + c = f(a)$. *(2 marks)*
- Impose $L'(a) = f'(a) \Rightarrow m = f'(a)$. *(2 marks)*
- Solve to get $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$. *(2 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** $f(0) = e^0 \cos 0 = 1$.

**Step 2:** Differentiate using product rule:
$$f'(x) = 0.3 e^{0.3x}\cos(2x) - 2e^{0.3x}\sin(2x)$$
$$f'(0) = 0.3(1)(1) - 2(1)(0) = 0.3$$

**Step 3:** Linearization:
$$L(x) = 1 + 0.3x$$

**Step 4:** Approximate $f(0.05)$:
$$L(0.05) = 1 + 0.3(0.05) = 1.015$$

**Step 5:** True value: $e^{0.015}\cos(0.1) = 1.01511 \times 0.99500 \approx 1.01004$. 
The linear estimate $1.015$ has an error of $\approx 0.00496$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to apply the **product rule** when linearizing products like $e^{0.3x}\cos(2x)$. Students often differentiate only one factor. **Penalty: -2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing $\Delta x$ (the increment) with $x$ itself. Always write $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$, not $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a) \cdot x$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In error-estimation problems, students often compute $f(x) - L(x)$ and report it as the error bound. This is the **actual error**, not the bound. The bound uses $\dfrac{M}{2}\vert x - a \vert^2$.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Failing to state the **condition for validity** (differentiability) in definition-type questions. **Penalty: -1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember ŌĆö Linearization**

- **Core Formula:** $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$ ŌĆö the **tangent line approximation**. *(Must memorize)*
- **Validity Condition:** $f$ must be **differentiable** at $x = a$. *(Always state in 3-mark questions)*
- **Differential Form:** $dy = f'(x) \, dx$ and $\Delta y \approx f'(a) \Delta x$ for small $\Delta x$.
- **Geometric Meaning:** Linearization replaces the curve with its tangent line at $x = a$.
- **Error Bound:** $\vert f(x) - L(x) \vert \leq \dfrac{M}{2}\vert x - a \vert^2$, where $M = \max \vert f''(t) \vert$ on the relevant interval.
- **Relative Error:** $\dfrac{\Delta y}{y} \approx \dfrac{f'(a)}{f(a)} \Delta x$, applicable when $f(a) \neq 0$.
- **Common Pitfalls:**
  * Do not use $a = 0$ when the function is not defined there (e.g., $\ln x$ at $a = 0$).
  * In percentage-error problems, always compute $\dfrac{dV}{V}$, not just $dV$.
  * The approximation is **more accurate** when $\vert x - a \vert$ is smaller.
- **Standard Approximations to Remember:**
  * $\sqrt{x} \approx \sqrt{a} + \dfrac{x - a}{2\sqrt{a}}$
  * $\sin x \approx x$ (near $0$)
  * $e^x \approx 1 + x$ (near $0$)
  * $\ln(1 + x) \approx x$ (near $0$)
  * $(1 + x)^n \approx 1 + nx$ (near $0$)
- **Linkage to Taylor Series:** Linearization is the **first-order Taylor polynomial** about $a$, with all higher-order terms dropped.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concavity: The Second Derivative Test for Concavity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE ŌĆō 1
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concavity: The Second Derivative Test for Concavity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Concavity

In the language of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, **concavity** is a geometric property of a curve that describes whether the curve "bends upward" or "bends downward" over a given interval. The concavity of a twice-differentiable function $f(x)$ is determined entirely by the sign of its **second derivative** $f''(x)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formal Definition (Concavity)**
>
> A function $f$ is said to be **concave upward** (or **convex**) on an open interval $I$ if for every $x \in I$, the second derivative satisfies:
>
> $$f''(x) > 0$$
>
> A function $f$ is said to be **concave downward** (or simply **concave**) on an open interval $I$ if for every $x \in I$:
>
> $$f''(x) < 0$$

### 1.2 The Second Derivative Test for Concavity (Statement)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Second Derivative Test (KTU Board Standard)**
>
> Let $f$ be a twice-differentiable function on an interval $I$. Then:
> 1. If $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x \in I$, the graph of $f$ lies **above** all of its tangent lines on $I$ (concave up).
> 2. If $f''(x) < 0$ for all $x \in I$, the graph of $f$ lies **below** all of its tangent lines on $I$ (concave down).
> 3. A point where the concavity changes sign is called an **Inflection Point**, and it can only occur where $f''(c) = 0$ or where $f''(c)$ does not exist.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Cup and the Dome" Intuition

Imagine you are walking along the graph of $y = f(x)$ from left to right:

- **Concave Up (U-shape):** Think of a **ceramic soup bowl** or a **valley**. If you pour water into it, the water is *trapped* and pools in the center. The slope of the curve is constantly **increasing** ŌĆö starting negative, becoming zero at the bottom, then positive. This is what $f''(x) > 0$ means geometrically.
- **Concave Down (Ōł®-shape):** Think of a **dome** or the **top of a hill**. If rain falls on it, the water immediately *runs off* to the sides. The slope of the curve is constantly **decreasing** ŌĆö starting positive, becoming zero at the peak, then negative. This is what $f''(x) < 0$ means geometrically.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook for KTU Viva:** "The **second** derivative tells you about the **curvature** (the bend), while the **first** derivative tells you about the **direction** (going up or down)." Many students confuse the two ŌĆö never mix them up in the exam hall.

### 1.4 Why Concavity Matters ŌĆö Physical Interpretation

In engineering and information science, concavity models critical phenomena:

- **Cost Functions in Economics:** A concave-up cost function represents *accelerating* costs (e.g., diseconomies of scale).
- **Signal Processing:** The concavity of a logarithmic curve $y = \log x$ is concave down ŌĆö this is the mathematical foundation of the **logarithmic loss** function used in machine learning classifiers.
- **Beam Deflection in Civil Engineering:** The deflected shape of a loaded beam has concavity determined by the bending moment ŌĆö concave up means sagging, concave down means hogging.
- **Information Entropy:** The Shannon entropy function is **concave** in probability distributions, a foundational result in information theory.

The standard unit/sign convention used in KTU problems is dimensionless (pure sign analysis), and the critical threshold value is always **zero (0)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step by Step

To apply the **Second Derivative Test for Concavity** in a KTU examination, the student must follow a strict procedural sequence. Below is the deconstructed logic that examiners expect to see in your answer scripts.

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Find the First Derivative:** Compute $f'(x)$ using standard differentiation rules (power rule, product rule, quotient rule, chain rule).
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Find the Second Derivative:** Compute $f''(x)$ by differentiating $f'(x)$ once more.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Identify Critical Candidates for Concavity Change:** Set $f''(x) = 0$ and solve for $x$. Also identify all points where $f''(x)$ is **undefined** (these are the "candidates" where concavity might flip).
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Build a Sign Chart for $f''(x)$:** Plot the candidate values on a number line and test the sign of $f''(x)$ in each sub-interval.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Read Off the Concavity:** 
   * If $f''(x) > 0$ on an interval $\Rightarrow$ **Concave Up**.
   * If $f''(x) < 0$ on an interval $\Rightarrow$ **Concave Down**.
- **Step 6 ŌĆö Locate Inflection Points:** Any point $x = c$ where the sign of $f''(x)$ **changes** (from $+$ to $-$ or from $-$ to $+$) is a candidate for an inflection point. Confirm that $f(c)$ exists (i.e., $c$ is in the domain of $f$).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why $f''(x) = 0$?** Because concavity is governed by the sign of $f''$, the only places where the sign can flip are the zeros of $f''$ (or its undefined points). This is the same logic as finding critical points of $f'$ for $f$.
- **Why test intervals?** A polynomial or rational $f''$ cannot change sign without passing through zero (Intermediate Value Theorem for continuous functions).
- **Why confirm $f(c)$ exists?** A value of $x$ where $f''$ vanishes but where $f$ itself is undefined is **not** an inflection point (e.g., $f(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$ has $f''(0) = $ undefined, but $x = 0$ *is* an inflection point since $f(0) = 0$ exists).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-yield revision block**. Memorize it before attempting the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

| Concept | Mathematical Condition | Geometric Meaning | Shape of Curve |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Concave Upward | $f''(x) > 0$ | Curve opens **upward**; tangent lies below curve | U-shape (cup) |
| Concave Downward | $f''(x) < 0$ | Curve opens **downward**; tangent lies above curve | Ōł®-shape (dome) |
| Candidate for Inflection | $f''(c) = 0$ or $f''(c)$ DNE | Possible change in bending direction | Transition point |
| Confirmed Inflection Point | $f''$ changes sign at $c$ AND $f(c)$ exists | Curve actually changes concavity | S-shape pivot |
| Linear Function | $f''(x) \equiv 0$ | No bending at all | Straight line |
| Test Value Method | Pick $x_0$ in each interval, evaluate $\text{sign}(f''(x_0))$ | Sign of $f''$ reveals concavity on that interval | Sign chart |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Second Derivative Test is not merely a calculus exercise ŌĆö it is a **diagnostic tool**:

- In **Machine Learning**, the **convexity** (concave up) of a loss function guarantees that gradient descent will find the global minimum. Non-convex loss landscapes (concavity changes) are why training deep neural networks is hard.
- In **Physics**, the second derivative of position with respect to time is **acceleration**. A positive second derivative means accelerating forward; negative means decelerating. The sign change is the moment of maximum velocity.
- In **Control Systems**, the response of a damped oscillator $x(t) = e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)$ has alternating concavity, and inflection points mark the times of maximum and minimum velocity.
- In **Economics**, profit maximization requires $f''(x) < 0$ (concave down) at the optimum, which is the **second-order condition** taught alongside KTU's first-order derivative test.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Theoretical Derivation: Why Does $f''(x)$ Control Concavity?

The deepest "why" behind the Second Derivative Test comes from **Taylor's Theorem with Remainder**, which we will expand step by step below. This derivation is the kind of *full-mark* justification a KTU examiner expects when asked "Prove the Second Derivative Test" for **14 marks**.

Let $f$ be twice-differentiable on an open interval containing $c$. By Taylor's theorem, for any $x$ near $c$:

$$f(x) = f(c) + f'(c)(x - c) + \frac{1}{2} f''(c)(x - c)^2 + R_2(x)$$

where $R_2(x)$ is the higher-order remainder, which is negligibly small compared to $(x-c)^2$ when $x$ is very close to $c$. The graph of the **tangent line** at $c$ is given by:

$$L(x) = f(c) + f'(c)(x - c)$$

Now we compute the **vertical difference** between the curve and its tangent line at the point $x$:

$$f(x) - L(x) = \frac{1}{2} f''(c)(x - c)^2 + R_2(x)$$

The key observation: for $x$ very close to $c$, the term $\frac{1}{2} f''(c)(x - c)^2$ **dominates** because $(x - c)^2 > 0$ is always positive. Therefore:

- If $f''(c) > 0$, then $\frac{1}{2} f''(c)(x - c)^2 > 0$, which means $f(x) > L(x)$. The curve lies **above** its tangent ŌĆö this is precisely the definition of **concave up**.

- If $f''(c) < 0$, then $\frac{1}{2} f''(c)(x - c)^2 < 0$, which means $f(x) < L(x)$. The curve lies **below** its tangent ŌĆö this is precisely the definition of **concave down**.

This concludes the proof. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example ŌĆö Full KTU Board Solution

> **Problem (Model):** Find the intervals of concavity and the inflection points of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 12x - 5$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the first derivative.**

Using the power rule $\frac{d}{dx}(x^n) = nx^{n-1}$ on each term:

$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 12$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the second derivative.**

Differentiating $f'(x)$ term by term:

$$f''(x) = 6x - 12$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Find the candidate points for inflection.**

Set $f''(x) = 0$ and solve:

$$6x - 12 = 0$$

$$6x = 12$$

$$x = 2$$

So $x = 2$ is the only candidate. Since $f''(x)$ is a polynomial, it is defined for all real $x$, so there are no undefined points to consider.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Build the sign chart for $f''(x)$.**

The number line splits at $x = 2$ into two intervals: $(-\infty, 2)$ and $(2, \infty)$.

| Interval | Test Point $x_0$ | $f''(x_0) = 6x_0 - 12$ | Sign of $f''$ | Concavity |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $(-\infty, 2)$ | $0$ | $6(0) - 12 = -12$ | Negative | Concave Down |
| $(2, \infty)$ | $4$ | $6(4) - 12 = 12$ | Positive | Concave Up |

**Step 5 ŌĆö Identify the inflection point.**

Since the sign of $f''$ changes from negative to positive as $x$ passes through $2$, the point $x = 2$ is a confirmed inflection point. We compute its $y$-coordinate:

$$f(2) = (2)^3 - 6(2)^2 + 12(2) - 5 = 8 - 24 + 24 - 5 = 3$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö State the final answer.**

> $f$ is **concave down** on $(-\infty, 2)$ and **concave up** on $(2, \infty)$. The unique inflection point is $(2, 3)$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Quick Pattern Recognition

| Function Form | Likely $f''(x)$ Sign Pattern | Concavity Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $f(x) = ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d$ | $f''(x) = 6ax + 2b$ | One inflection point at $x = -b/(3a)$ |
| $f(x) = e^{x}$ | $f''(x) = e^{x} > 0$ always | Always concave up |
| $f(x) = \ln(x)$ | $f''(x) = -1/x^2 < 0$ for $x > 0$ | Always concave down on $(0, \infty)$ |
| $f(x) = x^4$ | $f''(x) = 12x^2 \geq 0$ | Concave up except at $x = 0$ (not an inflection) |
| $f(x) = \sin(x)$ | $f''(x) = -\sin(x)$ | Alternating ŌĆö inflection at $x = n\pi$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** For $f(x) = x^4$, students often wrongly claim $x = 0$ is an inflection point because $f''(0) = 0$. However, $f''(x) = 12x^2 \geq 0$ **does not change sign** at $x = 0$ ŌĆö it is non-negative on both sides. So $x = 0$ is **not** an inflection point, even though $f''(0) = 0$. This is one of the most common KTU traps!
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Tree for the Second Derivative Test

The diagram below provides a **block-level functional architecture flow** for applying the Second Derivative Test, suitable for direct transcription into a KTU answer script.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Given f of x"] --> B["Compute f prime of x"]
    B --> C["Compute f double prime of x"]
    C --> D["Solve f double prime of x equals 0"]
    C --> E["Identify where f double prime is undefined"]
    D --> F["Build Sign Chart of f double prime"]
    E --> F
    F --> G{"Does sign of f double prime change?"}
    G -- "Yes, plus to minus" --> H["Concave Up to Concave Down at x equals c"]
    G -- "Yes, minus to plus" --> I["Concave Down to Concave Up at x equals c"]
    G -- "No sign change" --> J["Not an Inflection Point"]
    H --> K["Compute f of c for coordinates"]
    I --> K
    K --> L["Inflection Point at c and f of c"]
    L --> M["State Final Concavity Intervals"]
    J --> M
```

### 4.2 Concavity Visual Summary

The following schematic shows the **two primary concavity states** as a Mermaid block diagram, useful for revision:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Concave Up: f double prime greater than 0"] --> A1["Tangent lies below curve"]
    A1 --> A2["Slope f prime is increasing"]
    A2 --> A3["Example: y equals x squared"]
    B["Concave Down: f double prime less than 0"] --> B1["Tangent lies above curve"]
    B1 --> B2["Slope f prime is decreasing"]
    B2 --> B3["Example: y equals minus x squared"]
    C["Inflection Point: f double prime equals 0 and changes sign"] --> C1["Curve changes bending direction"]
    C1 --> C2["Example: y equals x cubed at origin"]
```

### 4.3 Concavity vs. First Derivative Behavior ŌĆö Cross-Reference Table

The schematic relationship between $f'$, $f''$, and the shape of the curve is summarized below. This is the **single most important visual** to commit to memory before the KTU examination.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Sign of f double prime"] --> S2{"Positive or Negative"}
    S2 -- "Positive on interval I" --> R1["f is Concave Up on I"]
    R1 --> R1A["Graph of f is U shaped"]
    R1A --> R1B["Tangent line lies below curve"]
    S2 -- "Negative on interval I" --> R2["f is Concave Down on I"]
    R2 --> R2A["Graph of f is inverted U shaped"]
    R2A --> R2B["Tangent line lies above curve"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model]**

Define the terms **concave upward** and **concave downward** for a function $f$ defined on an interval. State the Second Derivative Test for Concavity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A function $f$ is said to be **concave upward** on an open interval $I$ if its second derivative satisfies $f''(x) > 0$ for every $x \in I$. Geometrically, the graph of $f$ lies above its tangent lines on $I$, and the slope $f'(x)$ is strictly increasing.

A function $f$ is said to be **concave downward** on an open interval $I$ if its second derivative satisfies $f''(x) < 0$ for every $x \in I$. Geometrically, the graph of $f$ lies below its tangent lines on $I$, and the slope $f'(x)$ is strictly decreasing.

**Second Derivative Test for Concavity:** If $f$ is twice-differentiable on $I$, then $f''(x) > 0$ on $I$ implies $f$ is concave up, and $f''(x) < 0$ on $I$ implies $f$ is concave down. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model]**

What is an **inflection point**? Is $f''(c) = 0$ sufficient to declare $c$ as an inflection point? Justify your answer with an example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **inflection point** of a function $f$ is a point $c$ in the domain of $f$ at which the concavity of the graph changes ŌĆö that is, the sign of $f''(x)$ changes as $x$ passes through $c$.

**No**, $f''(c) = 0$ alone is **not sufficient**. The necessary *and* sufficient condition is that $f''(c) = 0$ (or is undefined) **AND** the sign of $f''$ actually changes as $x$ moves across $c$.

**Counter-example:** Consider $f(x) = x^4$. Then $f''(x) = 12x^2$, so $f''(0) = 0$. However, $f''(x) \geq 0$ on both sides of $0$, so the concavity does not change. Hence $x = 0$ is **not** an inflection point despite $f''(0) = 0$. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **Part (a): 7 marks** and **Part (b): 7 marks**. You are required to attempt **ONE full question** (either Q.A or Q.B) from each module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper, GAMAT101]**

**(a)** Find the intervals on which the function $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3 + 12$ is concave upward and concave downward. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the inflection point(s), if any, of $g(x) = x e^{-x}$. Justify whether each candidate satisfies the definition. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) ŌĆö Solution

**Step 1: First derivative.** Differentiating $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3 + 12$ term by term:

$$f'(x) = 4x^3 - 12x^2$$

**Step 2: Second derivative.**

$$f''(x) = 12x^2 - 24x$$

**Step 3: Find candidates for concavity change.** Set $f''(x) = 0$:

$$12x^2 - 24x = 0$$

$$12x(x - 2) = 0$$

$$x = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad x = 2$$

**Step 4: Sign chart for $f''(x) = 12x(x - 2)$.**

| Interval | Test $x_0$ | $f''(x_0)$ | Sign | Concavity |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $(-\infty, 0)$ | $-1$ | $12(-1)(-3) = 36$ | $+$ | Concave Up |
| $(0, 2)$ | $1$ | $12(1)(-1) = -12$ | $-$ | Concave Down |
| $(2, \infty)$ | $3$ | $12(3)(1) = 36$ | $+$ | Concave Up |

**Step 5: Final Answer.** $f$ is **concave upward** on $(-\infty, 0) \cup (2, \infty)$ and **concave downward** on $(0, 2)$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Part (b) ŌĆö Solution

**Step 1: First derivative of $g(x) = xe^{-x}$.** Using the product rule $(uv)' = u'v + uv'$ with $u = x$ and $v = e^{-x}$:

$$g'(x) = (1)(e^{-x}) + (x)(-e^{-x}) = e^{-x}(1 - x)$$

**Step 2: Second derivative.** Differentiate $g'(x) = e^{-x}(1 - x)$ using the product rule again:

$$g''(x) = (-e^{-x})(1 - x) + (e^{-x})(-1)$$

$$g''(x) = -e^{-x}(1 - x) - e^{-x}$$

$$g''(x) = -e^{-x}\bigl[(1 - x) + 1\bigr]$$

$$g''(x) = -e^{-x}(2 - x)$$

**Step 3: Find candidate points.** Set $g''(x) = 0$:

$$-e^{-x}(2 - x) = 0$$

Since $e^{-x} > 0$ for all real $x$, we must have:

$$2 - x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 2$$

**Step 4: Sign chart for $g''(x) = -e^{-x}(2 - x)$.**

| Interval | Test $x_0$ | $g''(x_0)$ | Sign | Concavity |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $(-\infty, 2)$ | $0$ | $-e^{0}(2) = -2$ | $-$ | Concave Down |
| $(2, \infty)$ | $3$ | $-e^{-3}(-1) = e^{-3} > 0$ | $+$ | Concave Up |

**Step 5: Identify the inflection point.** The sign of $g''$ changes from negative to positive at $x = 2$, so $x = 2$ is a confirmed inflection point. Compute $g(2)$:

$$g(2) = 2 \cdot e^{-2} = \frac{2}{e^2}$$

**Step 6: Final Answer.** The function $g(x) = xe^{-x}$ has a single inflection point at $\left(2, \frac{2}{e^2}\right)$. The graph is concave down on $(-\infty, 2)$ and concave up on $(2, \infty)$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper, GAMAT101]**

**(a)** Determine the intervals of concavity and locate the inflection points of $h(x) = \dfrac{x^2}{x^2 + 1}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Second Derivative Test for Concavity, prove that $f(x) = \sin(x)$ is concave down on $\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{3\pi}{2}\right)$ and concave up on $\left(-\dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$. Identify any inflection points in the interval $[0, 2\pi]$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) ŌĆö Solution

**Step 1: First derivative via the quotient rule.** Let $u = x^2$ and $v = x^2 + 1$, so $u' = 2x$ and $v' = 2x$:

$$h'(x) = \frac{(2x)(x^2 + 1) - (x^2)(2x)}{(x^2 + 1)^2} = \frac{2x^3 + 2x - 2x^3}{(x^2 + 1)^2} = \frac{2x}{(x^2 + 1)^2}$$

**Step 2: Second derivative via the quotient rule.** With $p = 2x$ and $q = (x^2 + 1)^2$, we have $p' = 2$ and $q' = 2(x^2 + 1)(2x) = 4x(x^2 + 1)$:

$$h''(x) = \frac{(2)(x^2 + 1)^2 - (2x)\bigl[4x(x^2 + 1)\bigr]}{(x^2 + 1)^4}$$

Factor out $(x^2 + 1)$ from the numerator:

$$h''(x) = \frac{(x^2 + 1)\bigl[2(x^2 + 1) - 8x^2\bigr]}{(x^2 + 1)^4} = \frac{2(x^2 + 1) - 8x^2}{(x^2 + 1)^3}$$

$$h''(x) = \frac{2x^2 + 2 - 8x^2}{(x^2 + 1)^3} = \frac{-6x^2 + 2}{(x^2 + 1)^3}$$

$$h''(x) = \frac{2(1 - 3x^2)}{(x^2 + 1)^3}$$

**Step 3: Find candidates.** Set the numerator $2(1 - 3x^2) = 0$:

$$1 - 3x^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^2 = \frac{1}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 4: Sign chart.** The denominator $(x^2+1)^3 > 0$ always, so the sign of $h''$ matches the sign of $1 - 3x^2$.

| Interval | Test $x_0$ | $1 - 3x_0^2$ | Sign of $h''$ | Concavity |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $\left(-\infty, -\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$ | $-1$ | $1 - 3 = -2$ | $-$ | Concave Down |
| $\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$ | $0$ | $1$ | $+$ | Concave Up |
| $\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \infty\right)$ | $1$ | $1 - 3 = -2$ | $-$ | Concave Down |

**Step 5: Inflection points.** Compute $h$ at the candidate values. Using $h(x) = \dfrac{x^2}{x^2 + 1}$ with $x^2 = \frac{1}{3}$:

$$h = \frac{1/3}{1/3 + 1} = \frac{1/3}{4/3} = \frac{1}{4}$$

So both inflection points are $\left(\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{4}\right)$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Part (b) ŌĆö Solution

**Step 1: First derivative.**

$$f'(x) = \cos(x)$$

**Step 2: Second derivative.**

$$f''(x) = -\sin(x)$$

**Step 3: Analyze the sign of $f''(x) = -\sin(x)$.**

On the interval $\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$: $\sin(x) > 0$, so $f''(x) = -\sin(x) < 0$. Therefore $f$ is **concave down** on $\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$.

On the interval $\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}\right)$: $\sin(x) < 0$, so $f''(x) = -\sin(x) > 0$. Therefore $f$ is **concave up** on $\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}\right)$.

**Step 4: Inflection points in $[0, 2\pi]$.** Solve $f''(x) = 0$:

$$-\sin(x) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = n\pi, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

Within $[0, 2\pi]$, the candidates are $x = 0, \pi, 2\pi$. Check sign change of $f''$ at each:

- At $x = 0$: $f''$ changes from negative (just below $0$, $\sin(x) < 0$ for small negative $x$) to negative (just above $0$, $\sin(x) > 0$ for small positive $x$, so $f'' < 0$). Wait ŌĆö re-evaluate. For $x \in (-\varepsilon, 0)$, $\sin(x) < 0$, so $f'' = -\sin(x) > 0$. For $x \in (0, \varepsilon)$, $\sin(x) > 0$, so $f'' < 0$. Sign change from $+$ to $-$ $\Rightarrow$ inflection point.
- At $x = \pi$: For $x \in (\pi - \varepsilon, \pi)$, $\sin(x) > 0$, so $f'' < 0$. For $x \in (\pi, \pi + \varepsilon)$, $\sin(x) < 0$, so $f'' > 0$. Sign change from $-$ to $+$ $\Rightarrow$ inflection point.
- At $x = 2\pi$: For $x \in (2\pi - \varepsilon, 2\pi)$, $\sin(x) < 0$, so $f'' > 0$. For $x \in (2\pi, 2\pi + \varepsilon)$, $\sin(x) > 0$, so $f'' < 0$. Sign change from $+$ to $-$ $\Rightarrow$ inflection point.

**Step 5: Coordinates of inflection points in $[0, 2\pi]$.** Since $f(x) = \sin(x)$:

- At $x = 0$: $f(0) = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $(0, 0)$.
- At $x = \pi$: $f(\pi) = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $(\pi, 0)$.
- At $x = 2\pi$: $f(2\pi) = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $(2\pi, 0)$.

**Final Answer:** $f$ is concave down on $\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ and concave up on $\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}\right)$. The inflection points in $[0, 2\pi]$ are $(0, 0)$, $(\pi, 0)$, and $(2\pi, 0)$. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Concavity Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Concave Up" with "Increasing"** ŌĆö A function can be concave up while *decreasing* (e.g., $f(x) = -x^2$ is concave down, but $f(x) = e^{-x}$ is concave up *and* decreasing on $(0, \infty)$). Concavity is about **bending**, not about going up or down. **(Lose 1ŌĆō2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Sign Chart** ŌĆö Examiners award a separate mark (usually 1 mark) for explicitly drawing the sign chart. Even if your final answer is correct, omitting the chart costs a mark. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Declaring an Inflection Point Without Checking Sign Change** ŌĆö As shown in the $f(x) = x^4$ example, $f''(c) = 0$ does **not** automatically mean $c$ is an inflection point. You must verify that $f''$ actually changes sign. **(Lose 2ŌĆō3 marks)**
>
> 4. **Ignoring Undefined Points** ŌĆö For functions like $f(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$, the second derivative is undefined at $x = 0$, but $x = 0$ is still an inflection point. Always check for DNE candidates, not just zeros. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Forgetting the $y$-coordinate** ŌĆö An inflection point is a point $(c, f(c))$, not merely a value $x = c$. Always compute the $y$-coordinate. **(Lose 1 mark)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Read This the Night Before the KTU Exam**

- **Definition:** Concave up $\Leftrightarrow$ $f''(x) > 0$. Concave down $\Leftrightarrow$ $f''(x) < 0$.
- **Procedure:** Find $f'' \to$ Solve $f'' = 0$ and find DNE points $\to$ Build sign chart $\to$ Read concavity.
- **Inflection Point:** A point where $f''$ changes sign. Requires both (i) $f''(c) = 0$ or DNE, AND (ii) sign change of $f''$ at $c$, AND (iii) $f(c)$ must exist.
- **Counter-example to remember:** $f(x) = x^4$ has $f''(0) = 0$ but **no** inflection point at $0$.
- **Counter-example to remember:** $f(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$ has $f''$ undefined at $0$ but **does** have an inflection point at $0$.
- **Standard patterns:** $e^x$ is always concave up; $\ln x$ is always concave down on $(0, \infty)$; $\sin x$ alternates with period $2\pi$.
- **Taylor's theorem link:** Concavity is the second-order local behavior ŌĆö $f(x) - L(x) \approx \frac{1}{2} f''(c)(x-c)^2$, and the sign of $f''(c)$ controls which side of the tangent the curve lies on.
- **Engineering hook:** Convex loss functions = good for ML optimization; non-convex = hard.
- **Always draw the sign chart** in the exam ŌĆö it earns an easy mark and prevents silly sign errors.
- **State intervals in open interval notation** like $(a, b)$ ŌĆö KTU examiners are strict about notation.
- **Final answer format:** "$f$ is concave up on \_\_\_\_ and concave down on \_\_\_\_. Inflection point(s) at \_\_\_\_."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Domains and Ranges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE ŌĆō 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Domains and Ranges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Function? (Quick Recap)

A **function** $f$ from a set $A$ to a set $B$ is a rule that assigns to every element $x \in A$ exactly one element $y \in B$. We write $y = f(x)$ and say "$y$ is the image of $x$ under $f$."

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Before discussing domains and ranges, the student must firmly internalize that a function is a *mapping rule*. The **Domain** is the *input set* and the **Range** is the *output set* produced by the rule.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Domain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Domain):** The **Domain** of a function $f$ is the set of all permissible input values $x$ for which the expression $f(x)$ is **well-defined** (i.e., produces a finite, real-valued output). In set-builder notation:
> $$\text{Dom}(f) = \{\, x \in \mathbb{R} \mid f(x) \text{ is defined and real}\,\}$$

## 1.3 Formal Definition of Range

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Range):** The **Range** of a function $f$ is the set of all actual output values $y$ that the function produces as $x$ varies over the domain.
> $$\text{Ran}(f) = \{\, y \in \mathbb{R} \mid y = f(x) \text{ for some } x \in \text{Dom}(f)\,\}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **Range** with **Co-domain**. The *co-domain* is the *declared* set $B$ that the function maps *into* (often $\mathbb{R}$). The *range* is the *actual* set of values *hit* by $f$. Always report only the values that are **actually attained**.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Vending Machine

Imagine a **vending machine**:

- The **Domain** is the set of *buttons* you are allowed to press (the legal inputs). Some buttons may be disabled (excluded values), but every allowed button yields *some* drink.
- The **Range** is the *set of drinks that can actually drop out*. The machine is stocked with only certain beverages ŌĆö even if the machine *could* theoretically dispense 100 types, it actually only contains **Cola, Soda, and Water**.

So the *Domain* = the set of *valid button presses* (inputs), and the *Range* = the set of *drinks actually obtained* (outputs). The vending machine's "menu board" represents the **co-domain** ŌĆö the *promised* output space.

## 1.5 Why Domains and Ranges Matter in Information Science

In **Information Science**, domains and ranges govern:

- **Data type constraints** (an integer input field cannot accept $\sqrt{2}$ or undefined operations like division by zero).
- **Signal processing windows** (a sinc function's range tells you its amplitude bounds).
- **Algorithm complexity** (domain restrictions like $n \geq 1$ on factorial functions).
- **Database schema** (a column declared as `INT UNSIGNED` has domain $[0, 2^{32}-1]$ and range determined by stored values).

## 1.6 Standard Symbols Used by KTU Examiners

| Symbol | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| $D_f$ or $\text{Dom}(f)$ | Domain of $f$ |
| $R_f$ or $\text{Ran}(f)$ | Range of $f$ |
| $\mathbb{R}$ | Set of all real numbers |
| $\mathbb{R}^+$ | Set of all positive real numbers |
| $\mathbb{Z}$ | Set of all integers |
| $\cup$ | Union (combining sets) |
| $\in$ | "Is an element of" |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Cardinal Rules for Finding the Domain

When a function $f(x)$ is given as an algebraic expression, the **Domain** is $\mathbb{R}$ minus any $x$-values that cause the expression to be **undefined**. KTU examiners test exactly these three rules:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 1 ŌĆö No Division by Zero:** Any expression appearing in a denominator must be non-zero.
> $$x \neq 0, \quad \text{or} \quad \text{denominator}(x) \neq 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 2 ŌĆö Even Roots Demand Non-Negative Radicands:** For any even-indexed root (square root, fourth root, etc.), the quantity under the root must be non-negative.
> $$\sqrt{g(x)} \implies g(x) \geq 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 3 ŌĆö Logarithms Demand Positive Arguments:** For any logarithm $\log_a(g(x))$ (any base), the argument must be strictly positive.
> $$\log(g(x)) \implies g(x) > 0$$

## 2.2 The Logic Flow for Finding the Range

Finding the **Range** is intrinsically harder than finding the Domain because we must analyze the *output behavior*. KTU recognizes three primary strategies:

1. **Algebraic Inverse Method:** Solve $y = f(x)$ for $x$ in terms of $y$, then impose domain restrictions on $x$ to find admissible $y$.
2. **Graphical/Inspection Method:** Identify maximum, minimum, and asymptotic behavior of $f(x)$.
3. **Standard Form Recognition:** Recognize $f$ as a transformation of a parent function whose range is known.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Domains and Ranges of Parent Functions

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Memorize these standard results ŌĆö they appear in nearly every Part A question.

| Function $f(x)$ | Domain $\text{Dom}(f)$ | Range $\text{Ran}(f)$ |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Constant $f(x) = c$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $\{c\}$ |
| Identity $f(x) = x$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| Linear $f(x) = mx + b, \, m \neq 0$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| Quadratic $f(x) = x^2$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| Quadratic $f(x) = (x-h)^2 + k$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $[k, \infty)$ |
| Square Root $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| Cube Root $f(x) = \sqrt[3]{x}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| Reciprocal $f(x) = 1/x$ | $\mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}$ | $\mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}$ |
| Exponential $f(x) = e^x$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $(0, \infty)$ |
| Exponential $f(x) = a^x, a>0, a \neq 1$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $(0, \infty)$ |
| Logarithm $f(x) = \ln(x)$ | $(0, \infty)$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| Sine $f(x) = \sin(x)$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $[-1, 1]$ |
| Cosine $f(x) = \cos(x)$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $[-1, 1]$ |
| Tangent $f(x) = \tan(x)$ | $\mathbb{R} \setminus \{\pi/2 + n\pi\}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| Absolute Value $f(x) = \vert x \vert$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | $[0, \infty)$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Notation in LaTeX:** KTU examiners (and LaTeX typesetters) require the use of `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x - 3 \vert$) rather than raw vertical bars. This is the same reason we have used `\vert` in this sheet.

## 2.4 Transformation Rules for Domains and Ranges
