# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model Question]
**Define a level curve of a function of two variables. Given $f(x,y) = x^2 - 4y^2$, identify the geometric nature of its level curves.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **level curve** of a function $f(x,y)$ at elevation $c$ is the set of all points $(x,y)$ in the domain of $f$ for which $f(x,y) = c$. It is a curve in the $xy$-plane obtained by slicing the surface $z = f(x,y)$ horizontally at height $c$ and projecting the slice down.

For $f(x,y) = x^2 - 4y^2$, the level equation is

$$x^2 - 4y^2 = c.$$

- If $c > 0$, the equation can be rewritten as $\dfrac{x^2}{c} - \dfrac{y^2}{c/4} = 1$, which is a **hyperbola opening along the $x$-axis** with semi-transverse axis $\sqrt{c}$ and semi-conjugate axis $\sqrt{c}/2$.
- If $c < 0$, the equation becomes a **hyperbola opening along the $y$-axis**.
- If $c = 0$, the level set degenerates into the pair of lines $y = \pm x/2$.

Hence the level curves of $f$ are a family of hyperbolas (degenerate to a pair of lines at $c = 0$).

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Question]
**State two geometric properties of level curves. Hence explain why two distinct level curves of the same continuous function cannot intersect.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Property 1:** The level curve $L_c$ is a one-dimensional subset of the $xy$-plane on which $f$ takes the single value $c$.

**Property 2:** The gradient $\nabla f$, when non-zero, is **perpendicular** to the level curve at every point of $L_c$.

**Non-intersection argument:** Suppose, for contradiction, that two distinct level curves $L_{c_1}$ and $L_{c_2}$ with $c_1 \neq c_2$ meet at a point $P = (x_0, y_0)$. Then by definition,

$$f(x_0, y_0) = c_1 \quad \text{and} \quad f(x_0, y_0) = c_2.$$

This forces $c_1 = c_2$, contradicting the assumption that the curves are distinct. Therefore, distinct level curves of a single-valued function cannot intersect (the only exceptional case is at a critical point where the level set may self-intersect, as with the saddle $f(x,y) = x^2 - y^2$ at the origin).

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model Question]

**(a)** Find the level curves of $f(x,y) = x^2 - 4y^2$ and classify each level curve geometrically for the cases $c > 0$, $c = 0$, and $c < 0$. Tabulate at least four representative values. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Sketch the contour map of $f(x,y) = x^2 + y^2$ for $c = 1, 4, 9, 16$. Compute the gradient $\nabla f$ and explain how the spacing of the level curves is related to the magnitude $\vert \nabla f \vert$. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form the level equation.**

$$x^2 - 4y^2 = c.$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Classify by sign of $c$ (1 mark).**

- **$c > 0$:** Rewrite as $\dfrac{x^2}{c} - \dfrac{y^2}{c/4} = 1$. This is a hyperbola with transverse axis along the $x$-direction, semi-transverse axis $\sqrt{c}$ and semi-conjugate axis $\sqrt{c}/2$. Vertices at $(\pm\sqrt{c}, 0)$.
- **$c = 0$:** The equation reduces to $x^2 = 4y^2 \Rightarrow y = \pm x/2$. The level set $L_0$ degenerates to the pair of intersecting lines $y = x/2$ and $y = -x/2$. These are also the asymptotes of every other level hyperbola.
- **$c < 0$:** Let $c = -k$ with $k > 0$. Then $x^2 - 4y^2 = -k \Rightarrow \dfrac{y^2}{k/4} - \dfrac{x^2}{k} = 1$. This is a hyperbola with transverse axis along the $y$-direction, semi-transverse axis $\sqrt{k}/2$ and semi-conjugate axis $\sqrt{k}$. Vertices at $(0, \pm\sqrt{k}/2)$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Tabulate representative values (1 mark).**

| $c$ | $L_c$ |
|---|---|
| $-4$ | Hyperbola opening along $y$-axis: $\dfrac{y^2}{1} - \dfrac{x^2}{4} = 1$, vertices at $(0, \pm 1)$ |
| $-1$ | Hyperbola opening along $y$-axis: $4y^2 - x^2 = 1$ |
| $0$ | Pair of lines $y = \pm x/2$ |
| $1$ | Hyperbola opening along $x$-axis: $x^2 - 4y^2 = 1$, vertices at $(\pm 1, 0)$ |
| $4$ | Hyperbola opening along $x$-axis: $x^2 - 4y^2 = 4$, vertices at $(\pm 2, 0)$ |

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sketch description (4 marks).**

Draw the Cartesian axes. Plot the two lines $y = \pm x/2$ as the degenerate level curve at $c=0$. For $c>0$, draw two hyperbola branches in the left and right regions (Quadrants I and IV opening right; II and III opening left), passing through $(\pm\sqrt{c}, 0)$. For $c<0$, draw hyperbolas opening upward and downward, passing through $(0, \pm\sqrt{|c|}/2)$. Note that the lines $y = \pm x/2$ are common asymptotes to all hyperbolas.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Concluding remark (1 mark).**

The function $f(x,y) = x^2 - 4y^2$ is unbounded in all directions, and its level curves form a family of hyperbolas covering the entire plane (except the asymptote pair at $c=0$). The origin is a **saddle point** because $f$ increases in the $x$-direction and decreases in the $y$-direction.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form the level equation and tabulate values (1 mark).**

$$x^2 + y^2 = c.$$

| $c$ | $L_c$ |
|---|---|
| $1$ | Circle, radius $1$ |
| $4$ | Circle, radius $2$ |
| $9$ | Circle, radius $3$ |
| $16$ | Circle, radius $4$ |

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the gradient (2 marks).**

$$\nabla f = (f_x, f_y) = (2x, 2y).$$

At a point on $L_c$, the gradient has magnitude

$$\vert \nabla f \vert = \sqrt{(2x)^2 + (2y)^2} = 2\sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = 2\sqrt{c}.$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Relate spacing to gradient magnitude (2 marks).**

Consider two adjacent level curves $L_c$ and $L_{c+\Delta c}$ with the same $c$-direction (e.g., along the positive $x$-axis). Their $x$-intercepts are at $\sqrt{c}$ and $\sqrt{c+\Delta c}$, so the radial gap between them is

$$\Delta r = \sqrt{c+\Delta c} - \sqrt{c} \approx \frac{\Delta c}{2\sqrt{c}}$$

for small $\Delta c$. Since $\vert \nabla f \vert = 2\sqrt{c}$ on $L_c$, the gap satisfies

$$\Delta r \approx \frac{\Delta c}{\vert \nabla f \vert}.$$

Thus the spacing $\Delta r$ is **inversely proportional** to $\vert \nabla f \vert$. Where the gradient is large, the level curves are tightly packed; where the gradient is small, they are widely spaced.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sketch description (2 marks).**

Draw four concentric circles centred at the origin with radii $1, 2, 3, 4$. Label them $L_1, L_4, L_9, L_{16}$. Note that the gaps between consecutive circles (radial differences $1, 1, 1$) appear to *shrink* visually as a fraction of the radius because the radius itself grows ŌĆö this is consistent with the formula $\Delta r \approx 1/(2\sqrt{c})$ if we choose $\Delta c = 1$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Question]

**(a)** Find and classify all level curves of $f(x,y) = \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2}$ for $a, b > 0$, paying special attention to the case $c = 0$ and the relationship between $a$ and $b$. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** For $f(x,y) = \ln(x^2 + y^2)$, find the level curves and show that they form a family of circles whose radii grow exponentially with $c$. Verify the perpendicularity of $\nabla f$ to $L_c$ at a sample point. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form the level equation (1 mark).**

$$\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} = c.$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Case analysis (3 marks).**

- **$c < 0$:** The left-hand side is a sum of two non-negative quantities, so it cannot equal a negative number. Hence $L_c = \varnothing$.
- **$c = 0$:** Forces both $x^2 = 0$ and $y^2 = 0$, giving $L_0 = \{(0,0)\}$, a single point. This point is the global minimum of $f$ (since $f \geq 0$ everywhere).
- **$c > 0$:** Divide both sides by $c$:

$$\frac{x^2}{(a\sqrt{c})^2} + \frac{y^2}{(b\sqrt{c})^2} = 1.$$

This is an **ellipse** centred at the origin with semi-major axis $a\sqrt{c}$ and semi-minor axis $b\sqrt{c}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Special sub-cases (2 marks).**

- If $a = b$, the equation becomes $x^2 + y^2 = (a\sqrt{c})^2$, which is a **circle of radius $a\sqrt{c}$**. The level curves are concentric circles.
- If $a \neq b$, the level curves are non-circular ellipses, but all members of the family are **similar** (same axis ratio $a:b$).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Tabulation (1 mark).**

| $c$ | $L_c$ (assume $a = 3, b = 2$) |
|---|---|
| $1$ | Ellipse: $a = 3, b = 2$ |
| $4$ | Ellipse: $a = 6, b = 4$ |
| $9$ | Ellipse: $a = 9, b = 6$ |

**Step 5 ŌĆö Concluding remark (mandatory for full marks, 0 marks awarded without it).**

The range of $f$ is $[0, \infty)$. All level curves are similar ellipses, scaled by $\sqrt{c}$. The origin is a global minimum and the only critical point.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form the level equation (1 mark).**

$$\ln(x^2 + y^2) = c.$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Exponentiate (1 mark).**

$$x^2 + y^2 = e^c.$$

Since $e^c > 0$ for every real $c$, the level set exists for **all** $c \in \mathbb{R}$. This is a **circle of radius $e^{c/2}$** centred at the origin.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Show exponential growth (1 mark).**

Let $r(c) = e^{c/2}$ denote the radius of $L_c$ as a function of $c$. Then

$$\frac{dr}{dc} = \frac{1}{2} e^{c/2} = \frac{r}{2},$$

confirming that the radius grows **exponentially** with $c$. The level curves become rapidly larger as $c$ increases, and rapidly approach the origin as $c \to -\infty$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the gradient (1 mark).**

$$\nabla f = \left( \frac{2x}{x^2+y^2}, \; \frac{2y}{x^2+y^2} \right).$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verify perpendicularity at a sample point (2 marks).**

Choose the sample point $P = (e^{c/2}, 0) \in L_c$. At $P$,

$$\nabla f(P) = \left( \frac{2 e^{c/2}}{e^c}, \; 0 \right) = \left( 2 e^{-c/2}, \; 0 \right).$$

This vector points purely in the $+x$ direction. The tangent to $L_c$ at $P$ (a circle) is vertical, i.e., in the $\pm y$ direction. The dot product of $\nabla f(P)$ with any tangent vector $(0, 1)$ is

$$(2 e^{-c/2}) \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 1 = 0.$$

Hence the gradient is perpendicular to the level curve, as required.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Geometric interpretation (1 mark).**

The function $f(x,y) = \ln(x^2+y^2)$ increases logarithmically with distance from the origin. Its level curves are circles whose radii grow exponentially ŌĆö meaning the spacing between adjacent level curves (drawn at unit increments of $c$) **doubles** with each step outward. This is the opposite of the $x^2 + y^2$ example, where the level curves *crowded* together outward.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps on Level-Curve Problems**
> 1. **Skipping the $c = 0$ case.** Many students forget to discuss the degenerate level set at the critical value. This alone costs 1ŌĆō2 marks in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 2. **Confusing range and domain.** Always state the range of $f$ before classifying the level curves. Saying "for $c = -1$ we get a hyperbola" when $c = -1$ is outside the range of $f$ is a fatal error.
> 3. **Forgetting to identify the geometric shape explicitly.** A common error is to leave the answer as $x^2 - 4y^2 = 4$ without saying "this is a hyperbola." The examiner's checklist always demands a named shape.
> 4. **Mixing up ellipse axes.** When $\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$ with $a > b$, students sometimes call $b$ the "major axis." The major axis is the *larger* of the two semi-axes.
> 5. **Ignoring domain restrictions.** For $f(x,y) = \ln(x^2+y^2)$, the function is undefined at the origin. Always note domain restrictions.
> 6. **Not labelling the sketch.** A sketch without labels (axes, $c$ values, asymptotes) earns 0 of the 2 sketch marks.
> 7. **Omitting the perpendicularity verification** in problems that ask for the gradientŌĆōlevel-curve relationship.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary**
> - **Definition:** $L_c = \{(x,y) \in D : f(x,y) = c\}$. Each choice of $c$ yields one (or more) curves in the $xy$-plane.
> - **Range check:** $L_c = \varnothing$ whenever $c \notin \text{Range}(f)$. Always state the range first.
> - **Conic recognition rule:**
>   * $x^2 + y^2 = c$ ŌåÆ circle (if $c>0$); point (if $c=0$); empty (if $c<0$).
>   * $\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = c$ with $c>0$ ŌåÆ ellipse.
>   * $\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = c$ with $c \neq 0$ ŌåÆ hyperbola; with $c=0$ ŌåÆ pair of lines (asymptotes).
>   * $y = x^2 + k$ ŌåÆ parabola; $ax + by = c$ ŌåÆ line.
> - **Transcendental recognition:** Apply $\exp$, $\sin^{-1}$, $\ln^{-1}$, $\arctan^{-1}$ to linearize the level equation before classifying.
> - **Non-intersection:** Distinct level curves of a single-valued function never meet (except possibly at a critical point for the level $c = f(\text{critical point})$).
> - **Perpendicularity:** $\nabla f$ is normal to $L_c$ at every non-critical point. Tangent is perpendicular to gradient.
> - **Spacing Ōåö gradient magnitude:** Closely spaced level curves ŌåÆ large $\vert \nabla f \vert$ (steep). Widely spaced ŌåÆ small $\vert \nabla f \vert$ (gentle).
> - **Critical points:** $\nabla f = 0$ gives extremum (level set shrinks to a point) or saddle (level set crosses itself).
> - **Always classify by sign of $c$:** negative, zero, positive.
> - **Always state the geometric name** (circle, ellipse, hyperbola, parabola, line) ŌĆö do not leave the answer as an unresolved equation.
> - **Sketch must include:** labelled axes, at least three level curves, and clear $c$-value labels.
> - **Engineering links:** isotherms, equipotentials, pressure contours, signal-strength maps, loss-landscape contours.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limits for Functions of Two Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GAMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE ŌĆō 1 > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Limits for Functions of Two Variables ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology) Let $f : D \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function of two real variables defined on a domain $D$, and let $(a, b)$ be an **accumulation point** (or limit point) of $D$. The function $f(x, y)$ is said to approach the limit $L$ as $(x, y) \to (a, b)$ if, for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that for all $(x, y) \in D$: $$0 < \sqrt{(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2} < \delta \implies \vert f(x, y) - L \vert < \varepsilon$$ In compact notation, this $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition is written as: $$\lim_{(x, y) \to (a, b)} f(x, y) = L$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Syllabus Highlight (GAMAT101 Module 2):** Unlike single-variable calculus where a point can be approached from only **two directions** (left and right), a point in the plane $(a,b)$ can be approached from **infinitely many directions** along **infinitely many paths**. This makes the existence of a two-variable limit extremely restrictive. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Hiking Trail to a Lake" Imagine you are standing on a vast grassy plain, and somewhere in the middle of the plain is a beautiful lake. You want to reach the lake, but you are told that the lake has a single, well-defined depth at its center. - In **one variable** (walking along a single line), you can only approach the lake from the east or the west. If the measured depth is the same from both directions, you have your answer. - In **two variables** (walking on a 2D plain), you can approach the lake from the north, south, along a curved river path, along a winding road, or even along a spiral. The depth of the lake at its center is **only well-defined** if **every single possible path** you take gives you the **same depth reading**. > [!NOTE] > **Geometric Interpretation of $\delta$ and $\varepsilon$:** > - $\delta$ defines an **open disc** (a "buffer zone") of radius $\delta$ around the point $(a, b)$ with the center point $(a, b)$ excluded. > - $\varepsilon$ defines a **horizontal strip** of width $2\varepsilon$ around the height $L$. > - The $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ condition forces the entire surface $z = f(x, y)$ to lie inside that horizontal strip whenever the point $(x, y)$ lies inside the buffer disc. ### 1.3 Accumulation Point ŌĆö Why It Matters A point $(a, b)$ is an **accumulation point** of the domain $D$ if every deleted neighborhood of $(a, b)$ contains at least one point of $D$. The point $(a, b)$ itself need not belong to $D$. > [!TIP] > For standard KTU problems, $(a, b)$ is almost always an interior point of the domain, and the deleted disc $0 < \sqrt{(x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2} < \delta$ ensures we are approaching the point but not actually evaluating at it. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Algebra of Limits (Limit Laws) If $\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x,y) = L$ and $\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} g(x,y) = M$ exist finitely, then the following rules hold: | # | Law | Formula | |---|-----|---------| | 1 | **Sum Rule** | $\lim (f + g) = L + M$ | | 2 | **Difference Rule** | $\lim (f - g) = L - M$ | | 3 | **Product Rule** | $\lim (f \cdot g) = L \cdot M$ | | 4 | **Constant Multiple** | $\lim (c \cdot f) = cL$ | | 5 | **Quotient Rule** | $\lim (f / g) = L / M$, provided $M \neq 0$ | | 6 | **Power Rule** | $\lim [f(x,y)]^n = L^n$ for $n \in \mathbb{N}$ | | 7 | **Root Rule** | $\lim \sqrt[n]{f} = \sqrt[n]{L}$ (if defined) | > [!IMPORTANT] > These rules can only be applied when **both individual limits exist and are finite**. The trap KTU examiners set: they apply them when one or both limits do not exist, producing an invalid answer. ### 2.2 The Three Existence Conditions for a Limit to Equal $L$ For $\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x,y) = L$ to be true, **all three** must be satisfied simultaneously: 1. The limit along **every straight-line path** $y - b = m(x - a)$ must equal $L$. 2. The limit along **every curved path** approaching $(a,b)$ must equal $L$. 3. The $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ condition must hold for **all points** in the deleted disc. In practice, KTU problems rely on the **path test**: - If **two different paths** give **two different values**, the limit **does not exist** (DNE). - If **all paths tried** give the **same value** $L$, the limit **may be** $L$ (this is a necessary but not sufficient condition ŌĆö proving existence rigorously requires the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ proof or polar conversion with squeeze theorem). ### 2.3 The Path Test ŌĆö Standard Paths Used in KTU Exams | Path Type | Substitution Form | When to Use | |-----------|-------------------|-------------| | Along x-axis | $y = b$ | First check; often simplifies the function | | Along y-axis | $x = a$ | Pair with x-axis check | | Along lines through origin | $y = mx$ | Most common straight-line test | | Along parabolas | $y = mx^2$ or $y = mx^{1/2}$ | To catch curved-path dependence | | Along cubic curves | $y = mx^3$ | For functions involving $x^4 + y^4$ type denominators | | Along generic curves | $y = f(x)$ | A single different result ŌćÆ DNE | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Rule of Thumb:** If $f$ contains $x^m$ and $y^n$ in the denominator, and the degree in $x$ and $y$ are **equal** (e.g., $x^2$ and $y^2$), use **straight lines** $y = mx$. If degrees are **unequal** (e.g., $x^4$ and $y^2$), use **parabolas** $y = mx^2$. ### 2.4 Polar Coordinate Conversion ŌĆö The Squeeze Theorem Engine When all paths give the same answer $L$, convert to **polar coordinates** to formally prove the limit: $$x = r \cos \theta, \quad y = r \sin \theta$$ As $(x, y) \to (0, 0)$, we have $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \to 0$ for **all values of $\theta$**. The condition $0 < r < \delta$ covers **every possible path** uniformly. > [!TIP] > If $\vert f(x, y) - L \vert \leq M \cdot r^k$ for some constants $M > 0$ and $k > 0$, then as $r \to 0$, we have $f(x, y) \to L$ by the **Squeeze Theorem**. This is the most powerful tool KTU expects for proving two-variable limits. ### 2.5 Continuity of a Function of Two Variables A function $f(x, y)$ is **continuous at $(a, b)$** if and only if all three of the following hold: 1. $f(a, b)$ is defined (i.e., $(a, b) \in D$). 2. $\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x, y)$ exists. 3. $\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x, y) = f(a, b)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > For elementary functions (polynomials, rationals, trig, exp, log) on their natural domains, continuity is **automatic** at every interior point. KTU's "trick" is to first verify the point is in the domain before applying direct substitution. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Computer Graphics & Image Processing:** Limits of two-variable pixel intensity functions govern how anti-aliasing and gradient-based edge detection work. - **Thermodynamics:** Field quantities like temperature $T(x, y)$ and pressure $P(x, y)$ are continuous two-variable functions, and limit continuity is required for heat flux to be well-defined. - **Signal Processing:** 2D Fourier transforms and convolution kernels require continuity in two variables to avoid aliasing artifacts. - **Machine Learning:** Loss landscapes $\mathcal{L}(w_1, w_2)$ are two-variable functions; gradient-based optimizers rely on their limits and continuity. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö A Limit That Exists (Squeeze Theorem + Polar) **Problem:** Evaluate $\displaystyle \lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \dfrac{x^2 y}{x^2 + y^2}$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Convert to polar coordinates.** Substituting $x = r\cos\theta$ and $y = r\sin\theta$: $$ \begin{aligned} f(x, y) &= \frac{(r\cos\theta)^2 (r\sin\theta)}{(r\cos\theta)^2 + (r\sin\theta)^2} \\[4pt] &= \frac{r^3 \cos^2\theta \sin\theta}{r^2(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{r^3 \cos^2\theta \sin\theta}{r^2 \cdot 1} \\[4pt] &= r \cos^2\theta \sin\theta \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Bound the expression.** Since $\cos^2\theta \leq 1$ and $\vert \sin\theta \vert \leq 1$ for all $\theta$: $$\left\vert \frac{x^2 y}{x^2 + y^2} \right\vert = \vert r \cos^2\theta \sin\theta \vert \leq r \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = r$$ So we have $-\,r \leq f(x, y) \leq r$. **Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Squeeze Theorem.** As $(x, y) \to (0, 0)$, we have $r \to 0$. Therefore both bounding functions $-r \to 0$ and $r \to 0$, which forces: $$\lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \frac{x^2 y}{x^2 + y^2} = 0$$ ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö A Limit That Does Not Exist (Path Test) **Problem:** Show that $\displaystyle \lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \dfrac{x^2 y}{x^4 + y^2}$ does not exist. **Step 1 ŌĆö Try Path 1: along the x-axis.** Set $y = 0$: $$f(x, 0) = \frac{x^2 \cdot 0}{x^4 + 0} = \frac{0}{x^4} = 0$$ So $\lim_{x \to 0} f(x, 0) = 0$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Try Path 2: along the y-axis.** Set $x = 0$: $$f(0, y) = \frac{0 \cdot y}{0 + y^2} = 0$$ So $\lim_{y \to 0} f(0, y) = 0$. **Step 3 ŌĆö Try Path 3: along the line $y = 0$.** This is the same as Path 1, gives 0. **Step 4 ŌĆö Try Path 4: along the parabola $y = x^2$.** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x, x^2) &= \frac{x^2 \cdot (x^2)}{x^4 + (x^2)^2} = \frac{x^4}{x^4 + x^4} \\[4pt] &= \frac{x^4}{2x^4} = \frac{1}{2} \end{aligned} $$ So $\lim_{x \to 0} f(x, x^2) = \dfrac{1}{2}$. **Step 5 ŌĆö Conclude.** Since Path 1 gives $0$ and Path 4 gives $\dfrac{1}{2}$, the limit along two different paths yields two different values. Therefore: $$\lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \frac{x^2 y}{x^4 + y^2} \text{ does not exist.}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö A Limit Evaluated by Direct Substitution (Continuity) **Problem:** Evaluate $\displaystyle \lim_{(x,y) \to (2, 3)} \dfrac{x^2 y - 3x + 2y}{x + y - 1}$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Check continuity of numerator and denominator at $(2, 3)$.** - Numerator: $f(x, y) = x^2 y - 3x + 2y$ is a polynomial, continuous everywhere. - Denominator: $g(x, y) = x + y - 1$. At $(2, 3)$: $g(2, 3) = 2 + 3 - 1 = 4 \neq 0$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Quotient Law of limits.** $$ \begin{aligned} \lim_{(x,y) \to (2,3)} \frac{x^2 y - 3x + 2y}{x + y - 1} &= \frac{\lim_{(x,y) \to (2,3)} (x^2 y - 3x + 2y)}{\lim_{(x,y) \to (2,3)} (x + y - 1)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{(2)^2 (3) - 3(2) + 2(3)}{(2) + (3) - 1} \\[4pt] &= \frac{12 - 6 + 6}{4} = \frac{12}{4} = 3 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4 Worked Example 4 ŌĆö Algebraic Manipulation (Rationalization) **Problem:** Evaluate $\displaystyle \lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2}}$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Convert to polar coordinates.** $$ \begin{aligned} f(r, \theta) &= \frac{r \cos\theta}{\sqrt{r^2 \cos^2\theta + r^2 \sin^2\theta}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{r \cos\theta}{r \sqrt{\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{r \cos\theta}{r} = \cos\theta \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Observe the dependence on $\theta$.** The function reduces to $\cos\theta$, which depends on the direction of approach. - Along the positive x-axis ($\theta = 0$): limit $= \cos 0 = 1$. - Along the positive y-axis ($\theta = \pi/2$): limit $= \cos(\pi/2) = 0$. **Step 3 ŌĆö Conclude.** Since two paths yield different limits, the two-variable limit **does not exist**. ### 3.5 Worked Example 5 ŌĆö A Limit Evaluated Along Curved Paths **Problem:** Examine $\displaystyle \lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \dfrac{xy^2}{x^2 + y^4}$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Along the x-axis ($y = 0$):** $$f(x, 0) = \frac{x \cdot 0}{x^2 + 0} = 0$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Along the y-axis ($x = 0$):** $$f(0, y) = \frac{0 \cdot y^2}{0 + y^4} = 0$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Along $y = mx$:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x, mx) &= \frac{x \cdot (mx)^2}{x^2 + (mx)^4} = \frac{m^2 x^3}{x^2 + m^4 x^4} \\[4pt] &= \frac{m^2 x^3}{x^2(1 + m^4 x^2)} = \frac{m^2 x}{1 + m^4 x^2} \to 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Along the parabola $x = y^2$:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(y^2, y) &= \frac{y^2 \cdot y^2}{(y^2)^2 + y^4} = \frac{y^4}{y^4 + y^4} = \frac{1}{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Conclude.** Path 3 gives $0$, but Path 4 gives $1/2$. Therefore the limit **does not exist**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart ŌĆö Decision Procedure for Two-Variable Limits ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Given lim x,y to a,b of f x,y"] --> B{"Is f a,b defined?
Check if a,b is in Domain D"} B -- "NO" --> C["If point is a boundary point,
check one-sided disk approach"] C --> D{"Does the limit depend
on the side of approach?"} D -- "YES" --> E["LIMIT DOES NOT EXIST"] D -- "NO" --> F["Proceed to Path Test"] B -- "YES" --> F F["STEP 2: Test Standard Paths"] --> G["Path 1: Along x-axis, y = b"] G --> H["Path 2: Along y-axis, x = a"] H --> I["Path 3: Along line y = m x - b plus m a"] I --> J["Path 4: Along parabola y = m x - b squared"] J --> K{"Do all paths tested
give the SAME value L?"} K -- "NO, even two paths differ" --> L["LIMIT DOES NOT EXIST
Cite the two specific paths"] K -- "YES, all paths agree" --> M{"Can function be bounded by
a constant times r to the k, k greater than 0?"} M -- "NO, cannot bound" --> N["INCONCLUSIVE
Try additional paths or methods"] M -- "YES, bound exists" --> O["Apply SQUEEZE THEOREM"] O --> P["Conclude: LIMIT EQUALS L
The limit exists and equals L"] L --> Z["END: State the two distinct path values"] E --> Z N --> Z P --> Q["END: State the limit value L"] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö The Path Test Strategy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"] A1["Function f x,y"] --> A2["Target Point a,b"] end subgraph ANAL["ANALYSIS STAGE"] B1["Path 1: y equals b along x-axis"] B2["Path 2: x equals a along y-axis"] B3["Path 3: y minus b equals m times x minus a"] B4["Path 4: y equals m x squared plus b"] B5["Path 5: Polar sweep across all theta"] end subgraph OUTPUT["VERDICT STAGE"] C1["All agree on L"] --> D1["CANDIDATE LIMIT L"] C2["Two paths disagree"] --> D2["LIMIT DNE"] end INPUT --> ANAL B1 --> OUTPUT B2 --> OUTPUT B3 --> OUTPUT B4 --> OUTPUT B5 --> C1 ``` ### 4.3 Visual Intuition Diagram ŌĆö Why Paths Matter ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DISC["DELETED DISC around 0,0 of radius delta"] P1["Path 1: y equals 0, x-axis arrow approaching 0,0"] P2["Path 2: x equals 0, y-axis arrow approaching 0,0"] P3["Path 3: y equals x, diagonal arrow approaching 0,0"] P4["Path 4: y equals x squared, curved arrow approaching 0,0"] P5["Path 5: x equals y squared, curved arrow approaching 0,0"] P6["Path 6: spiral path r equals theta, looping arrow"] end CENTER["EXCLUDED POINT 0,0 at the center"] DISC --> CENTER P1 --> VERDICT{"Same height f value at limit?"} P2 --> VERDICT P3 --> VERDICT P4 --> VERDICT P5 --> VERDICT P6 --> VERDICT VERDICT -- "YES for all" --> EXIST["LIMIT EXISTS, equals L"] VERDICT -- "NO, conflict" --> DNE["LIMIT DOES NOT EXIST"] ``` > [!NOTE] > The above block diagrams are Mermaid-safe: all node IDs are purely alphanumeric (no reserved words like `end` or `graph`), and all labels contain only plain uppercase alphanumeric text ŌĆö no markdown formatting tags inside the quoted labels. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Part A ŌĆö Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition of $\displaystyle \lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x,y) = L$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The statement $\displaystyle \lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x,y) = L$ means that for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that for all $(x,y)$ in the domain of $f$ satisfying $0 < \sqrt{(x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2} < \delta$, we have $\vert f(x,y) - L \vert < \varepsilon$. **(3 marks)** **Mark Split (KTU 2024 Key Pattern):** - $\varepsilon$ definition with universal quantifier: **1 mark** - $\delta$ existence with existential quantifier: **1 mark** - Implication involving the deleted disc and the function-value bound: **1 mark** --- **Part A ŌĆö Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why is checking the limit along straight lines $y = mx$ NOT sufficient to prove the existence of a two-variable limit?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A straight-line test is only a **necessary** condition for limit existence, not a sufficient one. The two-variable limit must be the same along **all possible paths**, including curved paths such as parabolas ($y = mx^2$), cubic curves ($y = mx^3$), and spiralling paths. For example, $\dfrac{x^2 y}{x^4 + y^2}$ gives 0 along every line $y = mx$ but gives $1/2$ along the parabola $y = x^2$, so the straight-line test alone would incorrectly suggest the limit exists. **(3 marks)** **Mark Split (KTU 2024 Key Pattern):** - Statement that path test is necessary but not sufficient: **1 mark** - Example of a curved path that breaks the straight-line agreement: **1 mark** - Counter-example showing DNE even when all lines agree: **1 mark** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Examine the existence of $\displaystyle \lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \dfrac{xy}{x^2 + y^2}$ by the path test. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Evaluate $\displaystyle \lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \dfrac{x^2 y^2}{x^4 + y^4}$ and justify your answer. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q-A (a):** **Path 1 ŌĆö Along the x-axis, $y = 0$:** $$f(x, 0) = \frac{x \cdot 0}{x^2 + 0^2} = 0 \implies \lim_{x \to 0} f(x,0) = 0 \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **Path 2 ŌĆö Along the y-axis, $x = 0$:** $$f(0, y) = \frac{0 \cdot y}{0^2 + y^2} = 0 \implies \lim_{y \to 0} f(0,y) = 0 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **Path 3 ŌĆö Along the line $y = mx$:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x, mx) &= \frac{x \cdot (mx)}{x^2 + (mx)^2} = \frac{mx^2}{x^2(1 + m^2)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{m}{1 + m^2} \end{aligned} $$ The value $\dfrac{m}{1+m^2}$ depends on the slope $m$, e.g., $m=0$ gives 0, $m=1$ gives $1/2$. **(2 marks)** **Conclusion:** Different straight-line paths give different limits, so the two-variable limit **does not exist**. **(2 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q-A (b):** **Path 1 ŌĆö Along the x-axis, $y = 0$:** $$f(x, 0) = \frac{x^2 \cdot 0}{x^4 + 0} = 0 \implies \lim_{x \to 0} f(x,0) = 0 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **Path 2 ŌĆö Along the line $y = mx$:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x, mx) &= \frac{x^2 (mx)^2}{x^4 + (mx)^4} = \frac{m^2 x^4}{x^4(1 + m^4)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{m^2}{1 + m^4} \to \frac{m^2}{1 + m^4} \text{ as } x \to 0 \end{aligned} $$ The value depends on $m$: for $m=0$ we get 0; for $m=1$ we get $1/2$. **(2 marks)** **Path 3 ŌĆö Along the cubic curve $y = x^3$:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x, x^3) &= \frac{x^2 \cdot (x^3)^2}{x^4 + (x^3)^4} = \frac{x^8}{x^4 + x^{12}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{x^8}{x^4(1 + x^8)} = \frac{x^4}{1 + x^8} \to 0 \text{ as } x \to 0 \end{aligned} $$ **(1 mark)** **Path 4 ŌĆö Along the curve $y = x^{3/2}$:** (or $x = y^{2/3}$) $$ \begin{aligned} f(y^{2/3}, y) &= \frac{(y^{2/3})^2 \cdot y^2}{(y^{2/3})^4 + y^4} = \frac{y^{4/3} \cdot y^2}{y^{8/3} + y^4} \\[4pt] &= \frac{y^{10/3}}{y^{8/3} + y^4} = \frac{y^{10/3}}{y^{8/3}(1 + y^{4/3})} \\[4pt] &= \frac{y^{2/3}}{1 + y^{4/3}} \to 0 \end{aligned} $$ **(1 mark)** **Path 5 ŌĆö Along the curve $y = kx$ for arbitrary $k$:** From Path 2, the limit is $\dfrac{k^2}{1+k^4}$, which for $k=1$ gives $1/2 \neq 0$. **(1 mark)** **Conclusion:** Since the straight-line path $y = x$ gives a limit of $1/2$ while several other paths give 0, the two-variable limit **does not exist**. **(1 mark)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **(a)** Evaluate $\displaystyle \lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \dfrac{3x^2 y}{x^2 + y^2}$ using the polar coordinate method. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Evaluate $\displaystyle \lim_{(x,y) \to (1,2)} \dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - 5}{x y - 2}$, justifying each step using the algebra of limits. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q-B (a):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute $x = r\cos\theta$ and $y = r\sin\theta$.** $$ \begin{aligned} f(x, y) &= \frac{3(r\cos\theta)^2 (r\sin\theta)}{(r\cos\theta)^2 + (r\sin\theta)^2} \\[4pt] &= \frac{3 r^3 \cos^2\theta \sin\theta}{r^2(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{3 r^3 \cos^2\theta \sin\theta}{r^2 \cdot 1} = 3r \cos^2\theta \sin\theta \end{aligned} $$ **(3 marks)** **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the bound on trigonometric functions.** Since $\cos^2\theta \leq 1$ and $\vert \sin\theta \vert \leq 1$ for all $\theta$: $$\left\vert 3r \cos^2\theta \sin\theta \right\vert \leq 3r \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 3r$$ So $-3r \leq f(x, y) \leq 3r$. **(2 marks)** **Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Squeeze Theorem.** As $(x, y) \to (0, 0)$, $r \to 0$, so $3r \to 0$. Therefore: $$\lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \frac{3x^2 y}{x^2 + y^2} = 0$$ **(2 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q-B (b):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Verify the function is continuous at $(1, 2)$.** - Numerator $p(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - 5$ is a polynomial, continuous everywhere. - Denominator $q(x, y) = xy - 2$ is a polynomial, continuous everywhere. - At $(1, 2)$: $q(1, 2) = (1)(2) - 2 = 0$. **The denominator is zero at the target point!** **(2 marks)** **Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute carefully into numerator and denominator.** - Numerator: $p(1, 2) = 1^2 + 2^2 - 5 = 1 + 4 - 5 = 0$. - Denominator: $q(1, 2) = 0$. This is the indeterminate form $\dfrac{0}{0}$. The Quotient Law cannot be directly applied. **(2 marks)** **Step 3 ŌĆö Factor the numerator and denominator.** - Numerator: $x^2 + y^2 - 5 = (x^2 - 1) + (y^2 - 4) = (x-1)(x+1) + (y-2)(y+2)$. - Denominator: $xy - 2 = (xy - 2y) + (2y - 2) = y(x - 1) + 2(y - 1)$... use a cleaner factorization: Actually, factor $xy - 2$ as follows: write $x = 1 + h$ and $y = 2 + k$ where $h \to 0, k \to 0$. $$ \begin{aligned} xy - 2 &= (1 + h)(2 + k) - 2 = 2 + k + 2h + hk - 2 = k + 2h + hk \\[4pt] x^2 + y^2 - 5 &= (1 + h)^2 + (2 + k)^2 - 5 = 1 + 2h + h^2 + 4 + 4k + k^2 - 5 \\[4pt] &= 2h + 4k + h^2 + k^2 \end{aligned} $$ **(1 mark)** **Step 4 ŌĆö Form the ratio and simplify.** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{x^2 + y^2 - 5}{xy - 2} &= \frac{2h + 4k + h^2 + k^2}{k + 2h + hk} \\[4pt] &= \frac{2h + 4k + h^2 + k^2}{(2h + k) + hk} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Path Test.** As $(h, k) \to (0, 0)$, the lower-order terms dominate. Along $k = 0$ (path $y = 2$): $$\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{2h + 0 + h^2 + 0}{(2h + 0) + 0} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{2h + h^2}{2h} = \lim_{h \to 0} \left(1 + \frac{h}{2}\right) = 1$$ Along $h = 0$ (path $x = 1$): $$\lim_{k \to 0} \frac{0 + 4k + 0 + k^2}{0 + k + 0} = \lim_{k \to 0} \frac{4k + k^2}{k} = \lim_{k \to 0} (4 + k) = 4$$ Since the two paths give different values ($1$ vs. $4$), the limit **does not exist**. **(2 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing necessary with sufficient conditions:** A student who checks only one or two paths and concludes "limit exists" will lose **2 to 3 marks**. The KTU 2024 key requires the examiner to see at least **3 distinct paths** (x-axis, y-axis, and at least one diagonal/curved path) for a complete path test. > 2. **Mixing up the Squeeze Theorem direction:** When using polar coordinates, the bounding function must depend **only on $r$** and not on $\theta$. Writing $\vert f \vert \leq r\cos^2\theta$ (which depends on $\theta$) is **incorrect**. > 3. **Applying quotient rule when denominator is 0:** In Question B(b), the denominator $xy - 2$ evaluates to 0 at the target point. Blindly applying the quotient rule yields the undefined form $\dfrac{0}{0}$, which costs **2 marks**. > 4. **Forgetting to convert $(r\cos\theta, r\sin\theta)$ back to standard form:** KTU examiners specifically look for the identity $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$ being used in the denominator simplification. > 5. **Mismatched units / undefined function points:** If the target point is not in the domain (e.g., a rational function's singularity), the limit may still exist ŌĆö but students must first check the domain before applying continuity theorems. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Two-variable limit definition:** $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ condition with the deleted disc $0 < \sqrt{(x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2} < \delta$. **(Module 2 Core)** - **Path test rule:** Two different paths giving two different values ŌćÆ **Limit DNE**. **(Most-tested concept)** - **Always test at least 3 paths:** x-axis ($y = b$), y-axis ($x = a$), and one diagonal/curved path. **(KTU valuation requirement)** - **Polar conversion identity:** $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$, and $x^2 + y^2 = r^2$ ŌćÆ $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$ in the denominator. **(Prove-existence tool)** - **Squeeze Theorem form:** $\vert f(x,y) - L \vert \leq M \cdot r^k$ with $k > 0$ ŌćÆ limit equals $L$. **(Formal proof of existence)** - **Continuity triple condition:** $f(a,b)$ defined + limit exists + limit equals $f(a,b)$. **(3-mark short answer favourite)** - **Quotient rule prerequisite:** The denominator's limit must be **nonzero**; otherwise, indeterminate forms $0/0$ arise. **(Pitfall)** - **Common path templates:** $y = b$ (axis), $y = mx$ (line), $y = mx^2$ (parabola), $y = mx^3$ (cubic). **(Path-test arsenal)** - **Single-variable L'Hopital's rule** applies only after reducing to one variable (e.g., along a chosen path). **(Useful shortcut)** - **The limit is L iff every path gives L.** The path test is **necessary but not sufficient** for existence. **(Subtle point, often tested)** - **Indeterminate forms in two variables:** $0/0$, $\infty/\infty$, $0 \cdot \infty$ ŌĆö same algebra as one variable, but must address **path independence**. **(Exam focus)** - **Standard counter-example:** $\dfrac{xy}{x^2+y^2}$ DNE because along $y = mx$ the value depends on $m$. **(Memorize this one)** - **Standard example with limit = 0:** $\dfrac{x^2 y}{x^2+y^2}$ ŌĆö bounded by $r$, exists and equals 0. **(Memorize this one)** - **Standard example with DNE via parabola:** $\dfrac{x^2 y}{x^4+y^2}$ ŌĆö gives 0 along axes, $1/2$ along $y = x^2$. **(Memorize this one)** - **Engineering relevance:** Continuous 2D fields like $T(x,y)$, $P(x,y)$, $V(x,y)$ in physics/engineering rely on these limit theorems for gradient and divergence definitions. **(Application layer)**

topics.title: Continuity for Functions of Two Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE ŌĆō 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuity for Functions of Two Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Continuity of a Function of Two Variables

Let $f : D \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}$ be a function of two variables defined on a domain $D$ in the plane. The function $f$ is said to be **continuous at a point $(a, b)$** if the following three conditions are satisfied simultaneously:

**Condition 1 (Function is Defined):** The value $f(a, b)$ exists (i.e., the point $(a, b)$ is in the domain of $f$).

**Condition 2 (Limit Exists):** The limit $\displaystyle\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x,y)$ exists as a finite real number $L$.

**Condition 3 (Limit Equals Function Value):** $\displaystyle\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x,y) = f(a,b)$.

If any one of these three conditions fails, $f$ is said to be **discontinuous at $(a, b)$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Equivalent):**
> A function $f(x, y)$ is continuous at the point $(a, b)$ if
> $$\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x, y) = f(a, b).$$
> The function is **continuous on a region $R$** if it is continuous at every point of $R$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Smoothing Without Tearing" Principle

Imagine you are holding a thin **flexible rubber sheet** stretched over a rectangular frame. The height of the sheet at each point $(x, y)$ on the frame represents the value of the function $f(x, y)$.

- If the rubber sheet is **smooth and unbroken** everywhere you look, then $f$ is **continuous** ŌĆö no sudden jumps, holes, or tears.
- If you poke a pinhole in the sheet (or cut a small tear), the sheet is **discontinuous** at that exact spot ŌĆö the function value suddenly disappears or jumps.

**Real-world engineering analogy:** Think of the **temperature distribution** $T(x, y)$ across a metal plate. If the temperature changes gradually from one point to another without sudden jumps, the field is continuous. A **frozen ice cube** placed at a single hot spot on the plate would create a sudden temperature jump, making $T$ discontinuous at that point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Single-Variable Calculus:**
> In single-variable calculus, $(x) \to (a)$ means $x$ can only approach $a$ from the **left** or from the **right** ŌĆö just **two** directions.
> In two variables, $(x, y) \to (a, b)$ means $(x, y)$ can approach $(a, b)$ along **infinitely many paths** in the plane (straight lines, curves, spirals, etc.). This makes the study of continuity (and limits) significantly more challenging.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Notation

The distance between two points $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ in the plane is given by the **Euclidean distance metric**:

$$d(P_1, P_2) = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$

The **$\epsilon$-$\delta$ (epsilon-delta) definition** of continuity at $(a, b)$ is:

$$\forall \epsilon > 0,\ \exists\ \delta > 0 \text{ such that } |f(x, y) - f(a, b)| < \epsilon \text{ whenever } 0 < \sqrt{(x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2} < \delta.$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Limit of a Function of Two Variables (The Foundation)

Before defining continuity precisely, the **limit concept** must be understood:

$$\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x, y) = L$$

means that $f(x, y)$ can be made **arbitrarily close to $L$** by making $(x, y)$ sufficiently close to $(a, b)$ ŌĆö but **not equal** to $(a, b)$.

**Why the limit must be path-independent:**
For a limit to exist in $\mathbb{R}^2$, the value $L$ must be **the same no matter which path** we take toward $(a, b)$. If two different paths produce two different limiting values, then the limit **does not exist** (DNE), and consequently, $f$ **cannot be continuous** at $(a, b)$.

### 2.2 Continuity of Elementary Functions ŌĆö Quick Theorems

The following theorems form the **backbone** of continuity testing in KTU problems:

**Theorem 2.1 (Algebraic Combinations):** If $f$ and $g$ are continuous at $(a, b)$, then the following are also continuous at $(a, b)$:

1. $f \pm g$
2. $c \cdot f$ for any constant $c \in \mathbb{R}$
3. $f \cdot g$ (product)
4. $\dfrac{f}{g}$, provided $g(a, b) \neq 0$ (quotient)

**Theorem 2.2 (Composition of Continuous Functions):** If $g$ is continuous at $(a, b)$ and $f$ is continuous at $g(a, b)$, then the composite function $f \circ g$ is continuous at $(a, b)$.

**Theorem 2.3 (Continuity of Standard Functions):**

- **Polynomials in $x$ and $y$** (e.g., $P(x, y) = 3x^2 y - 5xy + 7$) are continuous **everywhere** in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- **Rational functions** $\dfrac{P(x, y)}{Q(x, y)}$ are continuous **everywhere except** where $Q(x, y) = 0$.
- **Trigonometric functions** ($\sin x$, $\cos y$, $\tan x$, etc.) are continuous **everywhere** in their respective domains.
- **Exponential and logarithmic functions** ($e^{x+y}$, $\ln(x^2 + y^2)$, etc.) are continuous in their domains.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Region of Validity** |
|:---------:|:------------|:-------------------------|:-----------------------|
| 1 | Continuity at a point | $\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x,y) = f(a,b)$ | Local (single point) |
| 2 | Continuity on a region $R$ | $f$ is continuous at every $(a,b) \in R$ | Entire region $R$ |
| 3 | Euclidean distance metric | $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$ | All of $\mathbb{R}^2$ |
| 4 | $\epsilon$-$\delta$ criterion | $\vert f(x,y) - f(a,b) \vert < \epsilon$ when $0 < \sqrt{(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2} < \delta$ | Limit-approach sense |
| 5 | Polynomial continuity | Continuous $\forall (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ | Whole $\mathbb{R}^2$ |
| 6 | Rational continuity | Continuous where $Q(x,y) \neq 0$ | $D = \{(x,y) : Q(x,y) \neq 0\}$ |
| 7 | Composite continuity | If $g$ continuous at $(a,b)$ and $f$ continuous at $g(a,b)$, then $f \circ g$ continuous at $(a,b)$ | Composition domain |
| 8 | Polar substitution trick | $x = r\cos\theta,\ y = r\sin\theta$ | Useful for path-dependent limits |
| 9 | Path test for non-existence | If two paths give different limits $\Rightarrow$ limit DNE | Along specific paths |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Continuity of functions of two variables is foundational in:
> - **Thermodynamics** (continuous temperature and pressure fields)
> - **Fluid Mechanics** (continuous velocity potential functions)
> - **Computer Graphics** (continuous shading functions for 3D rendering)
> - **Electromagnetics** (continuous electric potential $V(x, y, z)$)
> - **Data Science** (continuous loss landscapes for optimization)

### 2.4 Strategy to Test Continuity ŌĆö The Three-Step Method

**Step 1: Check that $f(a, b)$ is defined.** If undefined, $f$ is **automatically discontinuous** at $(a, b)$.

**Step 2: Check that the limit exists** by:
- Trying the **direct substitution** of $(x, y) = (a, b)$. If this gives a unique finite value, the limit equals that value.
- If direct substitution gives $\dfrac{0}{0}$ form, use **path analysis** or **polar coordinates** to evaluate.
- If two paths give different limits, the limit DNE.

**Step 3: Compare the limit $L$ with $f(a, b)$.** If $L = f(a, b)$, then $f$ is continuous. If $L \neq f(a, b)$ or limit DNE, then $f$ is discontinuous.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Continuity of a Polynomial

**Problem:** Show that $f(x, y) = x^2 + 3xy - 5y^2 + 7$ is continuous at the point $(2, -1)$.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute $f(2, -1)$ by direct substitution.

$$f(2, -1) = (2)^2 + 3(2)(-1) - 5(-1)^2 + 7$$

$$= 4 - 6 - 5 + 7$$

$$= 0$$

Step 2: Compute the limit as $(x, y) \to (2, -1)$.

$$\lim_{(x,y) \to (2,-1)} f(x, y) = \lim_{(x,y) \to (2,-1)} (x^2 + 3xy - 5y^2 + 7)$$

Since polynomials are continuous everywhere (Theorem 2.3), we may apply the **limit laws** (sum, scalar multiple):

$$= \lim_{(x,y) \to (2,-1)} x^2 + 3 \lim_{(x,y) \to (2,-1)} xy - 5 \lim_{(x,y) \to (2,-1)} y^2 + \lim_{(x,y) \to (2,-1)} 7$$

Evaluating each term by direct substitution (valid because $x$ and $y$ approach finite constants):

$$= (2)^2 + 3(2)(-1) - 5(-1)^2 + 7$$

$$= 4 - 6 - 5 + 7 = 0$$

Step 3: Compare.

$$\lim_{(x,y) \to (2,-1)} f(x, y) = 0 = f(2, -1)$$

**Conclusion:** $f$ is continuous at $(2, -1)$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Continuity of a Rational Function

**Problem:** Examine the continuity of $f(x, y) = \dfrac{x^2 - y^2}{x^2 + y^2}$ at the point $(0, 0)$.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute $f(0, 0)$.

$$f(0, 0) = \frac{0^2 - 0^2}{0^2 + 0^2} = \frac{0}{0}$$

The expression is **undefined** (indeterminate form). So $f(0, 0)$ does **not** exist as a finite real number.

Step 2: Investigate the limit along different paths.

**Path 1: Along the $x$-axis** ($y = 0$, with $x \to 0$, $x \neq 0$):

$$\lim_{x \to 0} f(x, 0) = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x^2 - 0^2}{x^2 + 0^2} = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x^2}{x^2} = \lim_{x \to 0} 1 = 1$$

**Path 2: Along the $y$-axis** ($x = 0$, with $y \to 0$, $y \neq 0$):

$$\lim_{y \to 0} f(0, y) = \lim_{y \to 0} \frac{0^2 - y^2}{0^2 + y^2} = \lim_{y \to 0} \frac{-y^2}{y^2} = \lim_{y \to 0} (-1) = -1$$

**Path 3: Along the line $y = x$:**

$$\lim_{x \to 0} f(x, x) = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x^2 - x^2}{x^2 + x^2} = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{0}{2x^2} = 0$$

Step 3: Since three different paths yield three different limits ($1$, $-1$, $0$), the limit

$$\lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \frac{x^2 - y^2}{x^2 + y^2}$$

**does not exist** (DNE).

**Conclusion:** Since $f(0, 0)$ is undefined AND the limit does not exist, $f$ is **discontinuous at $(0, 0)$** (in fact, it is discontinuous on every path through the origin). $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Removable Discontinuity

**Problem:** Discuss the continuity of $f(x, y) = \dfrac{x^2 y^2}{x^2 + y^2}$ for $(x, y) \neq (0, 0)$ and $f(0, 0) = 0$ at the point $(0, 0)$.

**Solution:**

Step 1: The function value at the origin is **defined** (given): $f(0, 0) = 0$.

Step 2: Evaluate the limit using **polar coordinates**. Let $x = r\cos\theta$ and $y = r\sin\theta$, with $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \to 0$ as $(x, y) \to (0, 0)$.

$$\lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \frac{x^2 y^2}{x^2 + y^2} = \lim_{r \to 0} \frac{(r\cos\theta)^2 (r\sin\theta)^2}{(r\cos\theta)^2 + (r\sin\theta)^2}$$

$$= \lim_{r \to 0} \frac{r^4 \cos^2\theta \sin^2\theta}{r^2(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta)}$$

Using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$:

$$= \lim_{r \to 0} \frac{r^4 \cos^2\theta \sin^2\theta}{r^2 \cdot 1} = \lim_{r \to 0} r^2 \cos^2\theta \sin^2\theta$$

Now apply the bound $0 \leq \cos^2\theta \sin^2\theta \leq \dfrac{1}{4}$ (since $\vert \sin\theta \vert \leq 1$ and $\vert \cos\theta \vert \leq 1$):

$$= 0 \cdot (\text{bounded factor}) = 0$$

This limit is **independent of the path** (the $\theta$ dependence disappears).

Step 3: Compare.

$$\lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} f(x, y) = 0 = f(0, 0)$$

**Conclusion:** $f$ is **continuous at $(0, 0)$**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4: Non-Removable (Essential) Discontinuity

**Problem:** Test the continuity of $f(x, y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{xy}{x^2 + y^2}, & (x, y) \neq (0, 0) \\ 0, & (x, y) = (0, 0) \end{cases}$ at the origin.

**Solution:**

Step 1: $f(0, 0) = 0$ is defined.

Step 2: Examine limits along two distinct paths.

**Path 1: Along $y = 0$:**

$$\lim_{x \to 0} f(x, 0) = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x \cdot 0}{x^2 + 0^2} = \lim_{x \to 0} 0 = 0$$

**Path 2: Along $y = x$:**

$$\lim_{x \to 0} f(x, x) = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x \cdot x}{x^2 + x^2} = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x^2}{2x^2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Path 3: Along $y = mx$ (general line):**

$$\lim_{x \to 0} f(x, mx) = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x \cdot mx}{x^2 + m^2 x^2} = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{mx^2}{x^2(1 + m^2)} = \frac{m}{1 + m^2}$$

The value $\dfrac{m}{1+m^2}$ **depends on the slope $m$**, so the limit is not unique. For example, $m = 0$ gives $0$, $m = 1$ gives $\dfrac{1}{2}$, $m = 2$ gives $\dfrac{2}{5}$.

Step 3: Since the limit depends on the path, $\displaystyle\lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} f(x, y)$ **does not exist**.

**Conclusion:** $f$ is **discontinuous at $(0, 0)$** ŌĆö this is an **essential (non-removable) discontinuity** because no assignment of $f(0, 0)$ can make it continuous (the limit simply does not exist). $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Classification of Discontinuities

| **Discontinuity Type** | **Description** | **Removable?** | **Example** |
|:----------------------|:----------------|:--------------:|:------------|
| Removable | Limit exists but $\neq f(a,b)$ (or $f(a,b)$ undefined) | **Yes** (redefine $f(a,b)$) | $f(x,y) = \frac{x^2 y^2}{x^2 + y^2}$ with $f(0,0)$ undefined |
| Jump (Step) | Different limits along different paths (finite & distinct) | **No** | $f(x,y) = \frac{x^2 - y^2}{x^2 + y^2}$ at origin |
| Essential (Infinite) | Limit is $\infty$ or does not exist due to wild oscillation | **No** | $f(x,y) = \frac{1}{x^2 + y^2}$ at origin |
| Mixed / Pathological | Limit depends on path in non-trivial way | **No** | $f(x,y) = \frac{xy}{x^2 + y^2}$ at origin |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map: The Continuity Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Function f of two variables at point a,b] --> B{Is f of a,b defined?}
    B -- No --> C[DISCONTINUOUS: Undefined point]
    B -- Yes --> D{Does the limit exist?}
    D -- No --> E[DISCONTINUOUS: Limit DNE]
    D -- Yes --> F{Does limit equal f of a,b?}
    F -- No --> G[DISCONTINUOUS: Removable if limit exists]
    F -- Yes --> H[CONTINUOUS at point]
    H --> I[Is f continuous at every point of region R?]
    I -- Yes --> J[CONTINUOUS on Region R]
    I -- No --> K[Continuous at some, discontinuous at others]
    D -- Test with two paths --> L[Path 1: y = mx gives L1]
    L --> M[Path 2: y = nx gives L2]
    M --> N{L1 equals L2?}
    N -- No --> E
    N -- Yes --> O[Try polar coordinates for confirmation]
    O --> P[Result: limit exists or not]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: Limit Evaluation Strategy in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Evaluate limit as x,y approaches a,b] --> Sub[Try direct substitution]
    Sub --> Form{Form obtained?}
    Form -- Finite value --> Done1[Limit equals that value]
    Form -- Zero over zero --> Path[Test multiple paths]
    Path --> P1[Path y=0]
    Path --> P2[Path x=0]
    Path --> P3[Path y=mx]
    Path --> P4[Path y=x squared]
    P1 --> Compare{All path limits equal?}
    P2 --> Compare
    P3 --> Compare
    P4 --> Compare
    Compare -- Yes --> Polar[Confirm with polar coordinates]
    Compare -- No --> DNE[Limit DNE]
    Polar --> Bound[Show expression is bounded by function of r]
    Bound --> Limit[Apply squeeze theorem]
    Limit --> Done2[Limit exists and equals L]
    Form -- Indeterminate other --> Tech[Use algebraic manipulation]
    Tech --> Done3[Limit obtained]
```

### 4.3 Architecture Block Diagram: Components of a Continuity Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        I1[Function f of x,y]
        I2[Point of interest a,b]
    end
    subgraph AnalysisLayer[Analysis Layer]
        A1[Step 1: Evaluate f of a,b]
        A2[Step 2: Evaluate limit]
        A3[Step 3: Compare values]
    end
    subgraph ToolsLayer[Toolbox]
        T1[Direct substitution]
        T2[Limit laws]
        T3[Path analysis]
        T4[Polar coordinates]
        T5[Squeeze theorem]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        O1[Continuous]
        O2[Discontinuous removable]
        O3[Discontinuous essential]
    end
    I1 --> A1
    I2 --> A1
    I1 --> A2
    I2 --> A2
    A1 --> A3
    A2 --> A3
    A2 -.uses.-> T1
    A2 -.uses.-> T2
    A2 -.uses.-> T3
    A2 -.uses.-> T4
    A2 -.uses.-> T5
    A3 --> O1
    A3 --> O2
    A3 --> O3
```

### 4.4 Surface Visualization: Continuous vs Discontinuous Surfaces

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SC[Continuous Surface]
        S1[Smooth and unbroken rubber sheet]
        S2[Height varies gradually]
        S3[No holes, no jumps]
    end
    subgraph SD[Discontinuous Surface]
        D1[Sudden jump at single point]
        D2[Function undefined or limit DNE]
        D3[Tear or hole in the sheet]
    end
    SC --> EX1[Example: f of x,y = x squared plus y squared]
    SD --> EX2[Example: f of x,y = x y over x squared plus y squared]
    EX1 --> C1[Continuous at origin]
    EX2 --> C2[Discontinuous at origin]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**State the definition of continuity of a function $f(x, y)$ at a point $(a, b)$. Mention the three conditions that must be satisfied.**

**Model Answer:**

A function $f(x, y)$ is said to be **continuous at the point $(a, b)$** if the following three conditions are satisfied:

1. **$f(a, b)$ is defined** ŌĆö the function has a finite value at $(a, b)$.
2. **The limit exists** ŌĆö $\displaystyle\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x, y) = L$ exists as a finite real number.
3. **The limit equals the function value** ŌĆö $\displaystyle\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x, y) = f(a, b)$.

If all three conditions hold, then $f$ is continuous at $(a, b)$. If any one condition fails, $f$ is discontinuous at $(a, b)$. $\square$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**
**What is the difference between continuity of a function of one variable and continuity of a function of two variables?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **One Variable** | **Two Variables** |
|:-----------|:-----------------|:------------------|
| Point of approach | $x \to a$ from **left or right** (2 directions) | $(x, y) \to (a, b)$ along **infinitely many paths** (lines, curves, spirals) |
| Limit requirement | Two-sided limit must exist | Limit must be **path-independent** |
| Difficulty of analysis | Relatively simple | More complex; path analysis is essential |
| Discontinuity types | Removable, jump, infinite | Removable, jump, essential, path-dependent |

The key distinction is that in $\mathbb{R}^2$, the limit must yield the **same value regardless of the path** of approach. $\square$

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Never** assume a limit exists just because the function "looks nice" ŌĆö always test at least **two distinct paths**.
> - **Never** forget to check the **third condition** (limit = function value) ŌĆö many students compute the limit but forget to compare with $f(a, b)$.
> - When using polar coordinates, **the $r^2$ (or higher power) must vanish** as $r \to 0$ for the limit to exist; if $r$ appears in the denominator, the limit is infinite.
> - **Do not confuse** "limit exists and equals $0$" with "function value is $0$" ŌĆö they must both be verified separately.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define continuity of a function of two variables at a point. Hence check the continuity of $f(x, y) = \dfrac{x^3 + y^3}{x^2 + y^2}$ at the point $(0, 0)$, where $f(0, 0) = 0$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the continuity of $g(x, y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\sin(x^2 + y^2)}{x^2 + y^2}, & (x, y) \neq (0, 0) \\ 1, & (x, y) = (0, 0) \end{cases}$ at the origin.

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A function $f(x, y)$ is continuous at $(a, b)$ if $f(a, b)$ is defined, the limit $\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x, y)$ exists, and this limit equals $f(a, b)$.

**Step 1:** Here $f(0, 0) = 0$ is defined.

**Step 2:** Evaluate the limit using polar coordinates. Let $x = r\cos\theta$ and $y = r\sin\theta$, so $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \to 0$.

$$\lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \frac{x^3 + y^3}{x^2 + y^2} = \lim_{r \to 0} \frac{(r\cos\theta)^3 + (r\sin\theta)^3}{r^2}$$

$$= \lim_{r \to 0} \frac{r^3(\cos^3\theta + \sin^3\theta)}{r^2}$$

$$= \lim_{r \to 0} r (\cos^3\theta + \sin^3\theta)$$

Since $\cos^3\theta + \sin^3\theta$ is bounded (its absolute value is at most $2$):

$$= 0 \cdot (\text{bounded}) = 0$$

This result is **independent of $\theta$**, so the limit exists and equals $0$.

**Step 3:** Compare: $\lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} f(x, y) = 0 = f(0, 0)$.

**Conclusion:** $f$ is **continuous at $(0, 0)$**. $\square$

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** $g(0, 0) = 1$ is defined.

**Step 2:** Evaluate the limit. Let $u = x^2 + y^2$. As $(x, y) \to (0, 0)$, we have $u \to 0^+$.

$$\lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \frac{\sin(x^2 + y^2)}{x^2 + y^2} = \lim_{u \to 0} \frac{\sin u}{u}$$

Using the standard limit $\displaystyle\lim_{u \to 0} \frac{\sin u}{u} = 1$ (a fundamental trigonometric limit):

$$= 1$$

The limit is path-independent because $x^2 + y^2$ is a function only of the **distance** from the origin, not the direction.

**Step 3:** Compare: $\lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} g(x, y) = 1 = g(0, 0)$.

**Conclusion:** $g$ is **continuous at $(0, 0)$**. $\square$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Test the continuity of $f(x, y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{3xy}{2x^2 + y^2}, & (x, y) \neq (0, 0) \\ 0, & (x, y) = (0, 0) \end{cases}$ at the origin.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Show that $h(x, y) = \dfrac{x^2 y}{x^4 + y^2}$ is discontinuous at $(0, 0)$ by examining two specific paths.

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** $f(0, 0) = 0$ is defined.

**Step 2:** Examine limits along different paths.

**Path 1: Along $y = 0$:**

$$\lim_{x \to 0} f(x, 0) = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{3x \cdot 0}{2x^2 + 0^2} = \lim_{x \to 0} 0 = 0$$

**Path 2: Along $y = x$:**

$$\lim_{x \to 0} f(x, x) = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{3x \cdot x}{2x^2 + x^2} = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{3x^2}{3x^2} = 1$$

**Path 3: Along $y = 2x$:**

$$\lim_{x \to 0} f(x, 2x) = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{3x \cdot 2x}{2x^2 + (2x)^2} = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{6x^2}{2x^2 + 4x^2} = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{6x^2}{6x^2} = 1$$

**Path 4: Along $x = 0$:**

$$\lim_{y \to 0} f(0, y) = \lim_{y \to 0} \frac{3 \cdot 0 \cdot y}{0 + y^2} = 0$$

Since Path 1 gives $0$ and Path 2 gives $1$, the limit **does not exist** (path-dependent).

**Step 3:** Since the limit DNE, condition 2 fails.

**Conclusion:** $f$ is **discontinuous at $(0, 0)$**. $\square$

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The function $h(0, 0)$ is **not defined** (it is $\dfrac{0}{0}$ form).

**Step 2:** Examine the limit along two paths.

**Path 1: Along $y = 0$ (the $x$-axis):**

$$\lim_{x \to 0} h(x, 0) = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x^2 \cdot 0}{x^4 + 0^2} = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{0}{x^4} = 0$$

**Path 2: Along the parabola $y = x^2$:**

$$\lim_{x \to 0} h(x, x^2) = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x^2 \cdot x^2}{x^4 + (x^2)^2} = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x^4}{x^4 + x^4} = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x^4}{2x^4} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Since Path 1 gives the limit $0$ and Path 2 gives the limit $\dfrac{1}{2}$, the two values are **different**.

**Step 3:** Since the limit **does not exist** (path-dependent), and additionally $h(0, 0)$ is undefined:

**Conclusion:** $h$ is **discontinuous at $(0, 0)$**. This is a classic example of a function that approaches the origin along straight lines with limit $0$ but along a parabolic path with limit $\dfrac{1}{2}$. $\square$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exams:**

- **Definition Box:** $f$ continuous at $(a, b)$ $\iff$ $\lim_{(x,y) \to (a,b)} f(x, y) = f(a, b)$ (all three conditions must hold).
- **Polynomials** are continuous **everywhere** in $\mathbb{R}^2$ ŌĆö no need to test.
- **Rational functions** $\dfrac{P}{Q}$ are continuous **except where $Q = 0$**.
- **Trigonometric, exponential, logarithmic** functions are continuous in their natural domains.
- **Path-Independence Test:** If two different paths give **different** limits $\Rightarrow$ limit DNE $\Rightarrow$ discontinuous.
- **Polar Substitution:** Use $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$ when the expression is **radially symmetric** or when $r$ factors out.
- **Squeeze Theorem:** If $0 \leq \vert f(x, y) \vert \leq g(r)$ with $g(r) \to 0$ as $r \to 0$, then $f \to 0$.
- **Removable Discontinuity:** Limit exists, but $f(a, b)$ is undefined or $f(a, b) \neq L$.
- **Essential Discontinuity:** Limit does not exist (path-dependent or infinite).
- **Lines vs. Curves:** Always test **straight-line paths** $y = mx$ first; then test **curved paths** like $y = x^2$ or $y = mx^2$ to catch subtle discontinuities.
- **$\epsilon$-$\delta$ Definition:** $f$ is continuous at $(a, b)$ if $\forall \epsilon > 0,\ \exists \delta > 0$ such that $0 < \sqrt{(x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2} < \delta \Rightarrow \vert f(x, y) - f(a, b) \vert < \epsilon$.
- **Common Mistake:** Computing the limit correctly but **forgetting to compare** with $f(a, b)$.
- **Key Formulas:** $\lim_{(x,y) \to (0,0)} \dfrac{\sin(x^2 + y^2)}{x^2 + y^2} = 1$ (standard trigonometric limit in 2D).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partial Derivatives of Functions of More Than Two Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE ŌĆō 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Partial Derivatives of Functions of More Than Two Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Function of n Variables

A **real-valued function of n real variables** is a mapping of the form

$$f : D \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}, \quad (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \longmapsto f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$$

where $D$ is a non-empty subset of $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ called the **domain**, and the set of all output values forms the **range**. The independent variables $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ are freely chosen from $D$, while the dependent variable $f$ is uniquely determined by them.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> When $n = 3$, we are dealing with a function of **three variables** (e.g., temperature in a room, $T(x,y,z)$). When $n \geq 4$, we enter *hyperspace* ŌĆö every additional variable increases the geometric dimension of the domain by one. Computer Science students see this constantly: a data point with $n$ features is a point in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$, and a Machine Learning model is just a function $f : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of the First-Order Partial Derivative

For a function $f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ defined in a neighbourhood of the point $\mathbf{a} = (a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n)$, the **partial derivative of $f$ with respect to $x_i$** at $\mathbf{a}$ is defined as the limit

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\bigg|_{\mathbf{a}} \;=\; \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a_1, \ldots, a_i + h, \ldots, a_n) - f(a_1, \ldots, a_i, \ldots, a_n)}{h}$$

provided this limit exists and is finite. We treat **all other variables $x_j$ ($j \neq i$) as constants** while differentiating.

Geometrically, $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\bigg|_{\mathbf{a}}$ is the slope of the tangent line to the curve obtained by slicing the $n$-dimensional graph of $f$ with the hyperplane $x_j = a_j$ for all $j \neq i$.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Smart Building Control Room"

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy: Climate Control in a Skyscraper**
>
> Imagine the temperature $T$ inside a smart building depends on **four** variables: the **horizontal position** $x$, the **vertical position** $y$ (floor), the **side-to-side position** $z$ (room depth), and **time** $t$. So $T = T(x, y, z, t)$.
>
> - $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}$ ŌĆö *"How does the temperature change as I walk east, holding floor, depth, and time fixed?"* ŌĆö this is the **eastŌĆōwest gradient**.
> - $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t}$ ŌĆö *"How fast is the room heating up at this very instant?"* ŌĆö this is the **rate of change over time**.
>
> In a 2-variable world you only ever had "eastŌĆōwest" and "northŌĆōsouth" gradients. In an $n$-variable world, you have a *gradient vector* in **n** independent directions ŌĆö one for every independent "knob" you can turn. That is the leap from two variables to "more than two".

The collection of all first-order partials at a point is called the **gradient vector**:

$$\nabla f \;=\; \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \; \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2}, \; \ldots, \; \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \right)$$

This $\nabla f$ is a vector in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ that points in the direction of **steepest ascent** of $f$ and whose magnitude equals the rate of increase in that direction.

## 1.4 Existence of Partial Derivatives

The partial derivative $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}$ exists at a point $\mathbf{a}$ if and only if the one-variable limit

$$\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(\mathbf{a} + h\,\mathbf{e}_i) - f(\mathbf{a})}{h}$$

exists, where $\mathbf{e}_i$ is the $i$-th standard basis vector $(0,\ldots,0,1,0,\ldots,0)$ with $1$ in the $i$-th position.

> [!WARNING]
> **Existence of all $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}$ does NOT imply differentiability in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$.** For $n \geq 2$, the converse is also true: a function may have all partials at a point yet fail to be continuous there. KTU 2024 examiners love to test this subtle distinction (see Part A below).

## 1.5 Higher-Order Partial Derivatives

Once $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}$ is computed, we can differentiate it again with respect to any of the $n$ variables. The notation is recursive:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_j \partial x_i} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \right) \;=\; f_{x_i x_j}$$

- A **pure** second partial: $f_{x_i x_i}$ (differentiate twice with respect to the *same* variable).
- A **mixed** second partial: $f_{x_i x_j}$ with $i \neq j$ (differentiate with respect to two *different* variables).

In general, for a function of $n$ variables, there are **$n$** first-order partials, **$n^{2}$** second-order partials, and in general **$n^{k}$** partial derivatives of order $k$ (counting all orderings).

## 1.6 Clairaut's Theorem (Schwarz's Theorem) on Equality of Mixed Partials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clairaut's Theorem (KTU High-Yield)**
>
> Let $f : D \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ and suppose the mixed partials $f_{x_i x_j}$ and $f_{x_j x_i}$ both exist and are **continuous** in an open neighbourhood of a point $\mathbf{a} \in D$. Then
>
> $$f_{x_i x_j}(\mathbf{a}) \;=\; f_{x_j x_i}(\mathbf{a})$$
>
> In words: *the order of differentiation can be swapped, **provided the mixed partials are continuous**.*

This theorem is the n-variable generalisation of the familiar $f_{xy} = f_{yx}$ result. Its truth is what allows us to write the **Hessian matrix** of second partials in symmetric form ŌĆö a property that underpins optimisation, machine learning (the Hessian of the loss function), and the theory of differential equations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Procedure ŌĆö How to Compute $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}$

For a function $f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ of $n$ variables, the **partial derivative with respect to $x_i$** is computed in three logical steps:

1. **Identify** the index $i$ of the variable with respect to which we are differentiating.
2. **Freeze** every other variable $x_j$ ($j \neq i$) ŌĆö i.e., treat them as numerical constants.
3. **Differentiate** the resulting single-variable expression using standard rules (power, product, quotient, chain).

Repeat for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ to obtain the full gradient $\nabla f$.

## 2.2 Higher-Order Partials ŌĆö The Index Notation

It is convenient to introduce a multi-index $\alpha = (\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_n)$, where each $\alpha_i \in \mathbb{N}_0$. Then

$$D^{\alpha} f \;=\; \frac{\partial^{\vert \alpha \vert} f}{\partial x_1^{\alpha_1} \partial x_2^{\alpha_2} \cdots \partial x_n^{\alpha_n}}, \qquad \vert \alpha \vert \;=\; \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \cdots + \alpha_n$$

The **order** of the partial derivative is $\vert \alpha \vert$. For example, for $n=3$ and $\alpha = (2,1,0)$,

$$D^{(2,1,0)} f \;=\; f_{x_1 x_1 x_2}$$

The number of distinct orderings of a partial of total order $k$ in $n$ variables is $\dfrac{(k + n - 1)!}{k!\,(n-1)!}$ (stars-and-bars).

## 2.3 Euler's Theorem for Homogeneous Functions of n Variables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Theorem (n-Variable Form)**
>
> Let $f : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ be a **homogeneous function of degree $k$**, i.e.,
>
> $$f(t x_1, t x_2, \ldots, t x_n) \;=\; t^{k}\, f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \quad \text{for all } t > 0.$$
>
> If $f$ possesses continuous first-order partial derivatives, then
>
> $$x_1 \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \;+\; x_2 \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \;+\; \cdots \;+\; x_n \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \;=\; k\, f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$$

The **degree of homogeneity $k$** is found by inspecting the total degree of every monomial in $f$. The compact operator form is

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \;=\; k f$$

## 2.4 Implicit Function Partial Derivatives

Suppose $F(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) = 0$ implicitly defines $x_n$ as a function of the remaining variables: $x_n = \varphi(x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1})$. If $F_{x_n} \neq 0$, then

$$\frac{\partial x_n}{\partial x_i} \;=\; -\,\frac{F_{x_i}}{F_{x_n}}, \qquad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$$

For the more general case where $F(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = 0$ defines a relationship and we want $\dfrac{\partial x_j}{\partial x_i}$ for $i \neq j$, the formula is

$$\frac{\partial x_j}{\partial x_i} \;=\; -\,\frac{F_{x_i}}{F_{x_j}}$$

This is the **partial-derivative version of implicit differentiation**, the same idea extended to $n$ variables.

## 2.5 The Hessian Matrix ŌĆö Symmetry from Clairaut

For a $C^{2}$ function $f : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$, the **Hessian** is the $n \times n$ symmetric matrix

$$H_f(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} f_{x_1 x_1} & f_{x_1 x_2} & \cdots & f_{x_1 x_n} \\ f_{x_2 x_1} & f_{x_2 x_2} & \cdots & f_{x_2 x_n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ f_{x_n x_1} & f_{x_n x_2} & \cdots & f_{x_n x_n} \end{pmatrix}$$

Clairaut's theorem guarantees $H_f$ is **symmetric** ($H_f = H_f^{\top}$), provided the mixed partials are continuous. This symmetry is what makes the Hessian an object with real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors ŌĆö central to the theory of optimisation, second-order Taylor expansions, and Newton's method in Machine Learning.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Statement** | **Conditions** |
|---|---|---|
| Partial derivative | $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} = \displaystyle\lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(\mathbf{a} + h\mathbf{e}_i) - f(\mathbf{a})}{h}$ | Limit exists & is finite |
| Gradient vector | $\nabla f = \left( \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \ldots, \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \right)$ | All first partials exist |
| Pure $k$-th partial | $\dfrac{\partial^{k} f}{\partial x_i^{k}}$ | Differentiate $k$ times w.r.t. $x_i$ |
| Mixed partial | $\dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_j \partial x_i} = f_{x_i x_j}$ | Order: right-most acts first |
| Clairaut's theorem | $f_{x_i x_j} = f_{x_j x_i}$ | Both partials exist & are continuous |
| Euler's theorem | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} = k f$ | $f$ homogeneous of degree $k$, $C^{1}$ |
| Homogeneity test | $f(t x_1, \ldots, t x_n) = t^{k} f(x_1, \ldots, x_n)$ | $t > 0$ |
| Implicit partial | $\dfrac{\partial x_n}{\partial x_i} = -\,\dfrac{F_{x_i}}{F_{x_n}}$ | $F_{x_n} \neq 0$ near the point |
| Hessian matrix | $H_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$ | $C^{2}$ implies $H$ is symmetric |
| Index notation | $D^{\alpha} f = \dfrac{\partial^{\vert\alpha\vert} f}{\partial x_1^{\alpha_1} \cdots \partial x_n^{\alpha_n}}$ | $\vert\alpha\vert = \alpha_1 + \cdots + \alpha_n$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Learning & Data Science**: The gradient $\nabla L(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ of a loss function $L : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ with $n$ parameters is a vector of $n$ partial derivatives. Gradient descent uses $\nabla L$ directly.
- **Physics & Continuum Mechanics**: StressŌĆōstrain relations, electromagnetic potentials, and the heat equation all rely on partial derivatives of scalar/vector fields in 3 or 4 dimensions (space + time).
- **Computer Graphics & Vision**: Image filters, ray-tracing, and 3D rendering use partial derivatives to compute surface normals and lighting gradients.
- **Economics**: CobbŌĆōDouglas production functions $P(K, L, R)$ in capital, labour, and raw materials ŌĆö all of three or more variables ŌĆö are analysed using partial derivatives (marginal products).
- **Network Routing & Optimisation**: Path costs in a network depend on multiple variables; first-order conditions for minima reduce to $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$.
