# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example A ŌĆö All First and Second Partials of a 3-Variable Function

**Problem.** Find all first and second order partial derivatives of

$$f(x, y, z) \;=\; x^{2}\,y \;+\; y\,z^{2} \;+\; z\,\ln(xy)$$

Then verify that $f_{xy} = f_{yx}$ and $f_{xz} = f_{zx}$.

### 3.1.1 First-Order Partials

Differentiate with respect to $x$, treating $y$ and $z$ as constants.

$$f_x \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[ x^{2} y \right] + \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[ y z^{2} \right] + \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[ z \ln(xy) \right]$$

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[ x^{2} y \right] &= 2 x y \\ \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[ y z^{2} \right] &= 0 \quad \text{(no } x\text{)} \\ \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[ z \ln(xy) \right] &= z \cdot \frac{1}{xy} \cdot y \;=\; \frac{z}{x} \end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_{x} \;=\; 2 x y \;+\; \frac{z}{x}\,}$$

Differentiate with respect to $y$, treating $x$ and $z$ as constants.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left[ x^{2} y \right] &= x^{2} \\ \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left[ y z^{2} \right] &= z^{2} \\ \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left[ z \ln(xy) \right] &= z \cdot \frac{1}{xy} \cdot x \;=\; \frac{z}{y} \end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_{y} \;=\; x^{2} \;+\; z^{2} \;+\; \frac{z}{y}\,}$$

Differentiate with respect to $z$, treating $x$ and $y$ as constants.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left[ x^{2} y \right] &= 0 \\ \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left[ y z^{2} \right] &= 2 y z \\ \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left[ z \ln(xy) \right] &= \ln(xy) \end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_{z} \;=\; 2 y z \;+\; \ln(xy)\,}$$

### 3.1.2 Second-Order Partials

**Pure partials** (differentiate twice with respect to the *same* variable):

$$f_{xx} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[ 2xy + \frac{z}{x} \right] \;=\; 2 y \;-\; \frac{z}{x^{2}}$$

$$f_{yy} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left[ x^{2} + z^{2} + \frac{z}{y} \right] \;=\; -\,\frac{z}{y^{2}}$$

$$f_{zz} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left[ 2 y z + \ln(xy) \right] \;=\; 2 y$$

**Mixed partials** (differentiate with respect to *two different* variables):

$$f_{xy} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left[ 2 x y + \frac{z}{x} \right] \;=\; 2 x$$

$$f_{yx} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[ x^{2} + z^{2} + \frac{z}{y} \right] \;=\; 2 x$$

$$\therefore \;\; f_{xy} \;=\; f_{yx} \;=\; 2 x \quad \checkmark$$

$$f_{xz} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left[ 2 x y + \frac{z}{x} \right] \;=\; \frac{1}{x}$$

$$f_{zx} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[ 2 y z + \ln(xy) \right] \;=\; \frac{1}{x}$$

$$\therefore \;\; f_{xz} \;=\; f_{zx} \;=\; \frac{1}{x} \quad \checkmark$$

$$f_{yz} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left[ x^{2} + z^{2} + \frac{z}{y} \right] \;=\; 2 z + \frac{1}{y}$$

$$f_{zy} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left[ 2 y z + \ln(xy) \right] \;=\; 2 z + \frac{1}{y}$$

$$\therefore \;\; f_{yz} \;=\; f_{zy} \;=\; 2 z + \frac{1}{y} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.1.3 Summary Table of All 9 Partials

| **Partial** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| $f_x$ | $2xy + \dfrac{z}{x}$ |
| $f_y$ | $x^{2} + z^{2} + \dfrac{z}{y}$ |
| $f_z$ | $2yz + \ln(xy)$ |
| $f_{xx}$ | $2y - \dfrac{z}{x^{2}}$ |
| $f_{yy}$ | $-\,\dfrac{z}{y^{2}}$ |
| $f_{zz}$ | $2y$ |
| $f_{xy}=f_{yx}$ | $2x$ |
| $f_{xz}=f_{zx}$ | $\dfrac{1}{x}$ |
| $f_{yz}=f_{zy}$ | $2z + \dfrac{1}{y}$ |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example B ŌĆö Verification of Euler's Theorem (3 Variables)

**Problem.** Verify Euler's theorem for $u(x, y, z) = x^{3} y^{2} z$, and hence compute $x u_x + y u_y + z u_z$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the degree of homogeneity.**

Replace $(x, y, z)$ by $(t x, t y, t z)$:

$$u(t x, t y, t z) \;=\; (t x)^{3} (t y)^{2} (t z) \;=\; t^{3+2+1}\, x^{3} y^{2} z \;=\; t^{6}\, u(x, y, z)$$

The total degree is $3 + 2 + 1 = 6$. So $u$ is **homogeneous of degree $k = 6$**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the first-order partials.**

$$u_x \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[ x^{3} y^{2} z \right] \;=\; 3 x^{2} y^{2} z$$

$$u_y \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left[ x^{3} y^{2} z \right] \;=\; 2 x^{3} y\, z$$

$$u_z \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left[ x^{3} y^{2} z \right] \;=\; x^{3} y^{2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Form the Euler sum $x u_x + y u_y + z u_z$.**

$$\begin{aligned} x u_x + y u_y + z u_z &= x \cdot 3 x^{2} y^{2} z \;+\; y \cdot 2 x^{3} y z \;+\; z \cdot x^{3} y^{2} \\ &= 3 x^{3} y^{2} z \;+\; 2 x^{3} y^{2} z \;+\; x^{3} y^{2} z \\ &= (3 + 2 + 1)\, x^{3} y^{2} z \\ &= 6\, x^{3} y^{2} z \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compare with $k u$.**

$$k u \;=\; 6 \cdot x^{3} y^{2} z \;=\; 6 x^{3} y^{2} z$$

$$\therefore \;\; x u_x + y u_y + z u_z \;=\; k u \;=\; 6 u \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the result:** The exponents 3, 2, 1 in $u$ appear *exactly* as the coefficients 3, 2, 1 in the Euler sum. This is the punchline of the theorem: **for any monomial $x_1^{a_1} x_2^{a_2} \cdots x_n^{a_n}$, the Euler sum is always $(a_1 + a_2 + \cdots + a_n)$ times the monomial**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example C ŌĆö Implicit Partial Derivatives (3 Variables)

**Problem.** If $z$ is an implicit function of $x$ and $y$ defined by

$$F(x, y, z) \;=\; x^{3} \;+\; y^{3} \;+\; z^{3} \;+\; 3 x y z \;-\; 1 \;=\; 0$$

find $\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial x}$ and $\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial y}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the three partials of $F$.**

$$F_x \;=\; \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} \;=\; 3 x^{2} \;+\; 3 y z$$

$$F_y \;=\; \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} \;=\; 3 y^{2} \;+\; 3 x z$$

$$F_z \;=\; \frac{\partial F}{\partial z} \;=\; 3 z^{2} \;+\; 3 x y \;=\; 3\!\left( z^{2} + x y \right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the implicit-function formula.**

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial x} \;=\; -\,\frac{F_x}{F_z} \;=\; -\,\frac{3 x^{2} + 3 y z}{3(z^{2} + x y)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} &= -\,\frac{x^{2} + y z}{z^{2} + x y} \end{aligned}$$

$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial y} \;=\; -\,\frac{F_y}{F_z} \;=\; -\,\frac{3 y^{2} + 3 x z}{3(z^{2} + x y)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} &= -\,\frac{y^{2} + x z}{z^{2} + x y} \end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check at a symmetric point.** Try $x = y = z$. Then $F = 3 x^{3} + 3 x^{3} - 1 = 6 x^{3} - 1 = 0$, giving $x = y = z = (1/6)^{1/3}$. At that point, $\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial x} = -\dfrac{x^{2} + x^{2}}{x^{2} + x^{2}} = -1$. This makes intuitive sense: the implicit surface is symmetric, so a unit increase in $x$ at the symmetric point must be offset by a unit *decrease* in $z$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example D ŌĆö 4-Variable Function (Truly "More Than Two")

**Problem.** For $F(x, y, z, w) = x^{2} y z^{3} + w^{2} \sin(xy) - z w$, compute $\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial x}$, $\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial w}$, and $\dfrac{\partial^{2} F}{\partial x \partial w}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö First partial with respect to $x$ (treat $y, z, w$ as constants).**

$$\begin{aligned} F_x &= \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[ x^{2} y z^{3} \right] + \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[ w^{2} \sin(xy) \right] - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[ z w \right] \\ &= 2 x y z^{3} + w^{2} \cos(xy) \cdot y - 0 \\ &= 2 x y z^{3} + y w^{2} \cos(xy) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö First partial with respect to $w$ (treat $x, y, z$ as constants).**

$$\begin{aligned} F_w &= \frac{\partial}{\partial w}\!\left[ x^{2} y z^{3} \right] + \frac{\partial}{\partial w}\!\left[ w^{2} \sin(xy) \right] - \frac{\partial}{\partial w}\!\left[ z w \right] \\ &= 0 + 2 w \sin(xy) - z \\ &= 2 w \sin(xy) - z \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Mixed partial $\dfrac{\partial^{2} F}{\partial x \partial w} = F_{w x}$.**

Differentiate $F_x = 2 x y z^{3} + y w^{2} \cos(xy)$ with respect to $w$ (treating $x, y, z$ as constants):

$$F_{x w} \;=\; 0 \;+\; y \cdot 2 w \cos(xy) \;=\; 2 y w \cos(xy)$$

Cross-check: differentiate $F_w = 2 w \sin(xy) - z$ with respect to $x$ (treating $y, z, w$ as constants):

$$F_{w x} \;=\; 2 w \cos(xy) \cdot y \;=\; 2 y w \cos(xy)$$

$$\therefore \;\; F_{x w} \;=\; F_{w x} \;=\; 2 y w \cos(xy) \quad \checkmark$$

This example demonstrates that all the machinery ŌĆö partial differentiation, mixed partials, equality of mixed partials ŌĆö generalises seamlessly from two to three to **four or more** variables.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Computing Partials in Practice

The diagram below maps the **computational pipeline** an engineer or data scientist follows when working with a function of $n$ variables. It is intentionally drawn at the *block-architecture* level to abstract the mathematics into executable stages.
