$$\boxed{\text{Local Minima: } f(0) = 5, \ f(2) = 5; \quad \text{Local Maximum: } f(1) = 6}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing Domain Restrictions:** Students frequently forget to mention that $x = 6$ is excluded in the box problem because the volume becomes zero (degenerate box). Always state the domain explicitly. **(ŌłÆ1 mark)**
> 2. **Sign Chart Errors:** When the leading coefficient is negative, students often misread the sign pattern. Carefully test at least one point per interval. **(ŌłÆ1 to ŌłÆ2 marks)**
> 3. **Converse Confusion:** Writing "Since $f'(c) = 0$, therefore it is a maximum" without sign analysis is a critical logical error worth full negative marking. **(ŌłÆ2 marks)**
> 4. **Endpoint Neglect:** In closed-interval problems, always evaluate $f$ at the endpoints in addition to the critical points. **(ŌłÆ1 mark)**
> 5. **Units Omission:** In word problems (especially the box problem), the final answer must include units like cm or cm┬│. **(ŌłÆ0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fermat's Theorem** states: $f$ has a local extremum at interior point $c$ and $f'(c)$ exists $\Rightarrow f'(c) = 0$.
- The converse is **false**: $f'(c) = 0$ does **not** guarantee a local extremum (counter-example: $f(x) = x^3$ at $c = 0$).
- A **Critical Point** satisfies $f'(c) = 0$ **OR** $f'(c)$ is undefined. A **Stationary Point** strictly requires $f'(c) = 0$.
- **First Derivative Test** classification: $+\to-$ is Local Max, $-\to+$ is Local Min, no sign change means no extremum.
- The theorem's hypothesis requires $c$ to be an **interior point** ŌĆö endpoints are special cases requiring one-sided analysis.
- Fermat's Theorem is **necessary but not sufficient**; the First Derivative Test upgrades it to a sufficient condition.
- In **engineering optimization**, setting $f'(x) = 0$ is the first step toward finding the operating point that minimizes cost or maximizes efficiency.
- Always **construct a sign chart** for full KTU mark credit ŌĆö verbal sign analysis without a structured table is often penalized.
- Verify critical points algebraically **and** by computing the function value ŌĆö both are required for a complete KTU answer.
- When in doubt, cross-check with the **Second Derivative Test** (if $f''(c) > 0$, local min; if $f''(c) < 0$, local max; if $f''(c) = 0$, the test is inconclusive).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critical Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GAMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE ŌĆō 1 > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Critical Point ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Point ŌĆö Formal Definition** > Let $f: D \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function of two variables, defined on an open region $D$. A point $(a, b) \in D$ is called a **critical point** (or **stationary point**) of $f$ if **both** of the following conditions hold simultaneously: > 1. The first-order partial derivatives $f_x(a, b)$ and $f_y(a, b)$ **exist**. > 2. $f_x(a, b) = 0$ **and** $f_y(a, b) = 0$ (i.e., the gradient vector $\nabla f(a, b) = \mathbf{0}$). > > If a point in $D$ is not a critical point, it is called an **ordinary point**. > > **Generalization to $n$ variables:** A point is critical if all $n$ first partial derivatives exist and equal zero, i.e., $\nabla f = \mathbf{0} \in \mathbb{R}^n$. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Value:** The value of the function $f(a, b)$ at a critical point is called the **critical value**. Critical values are the only values at which a differentiable function can attain its local extrema ŌĆö this is Fermat's theorem for multivariable functions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are **hiking on a mountain** whose surface is described by $z = f(x, y)$, where $(x, y)$ is your ground position and $z$ is the height above sea level. - A **critical point** is any spot on the mountain where the ground beneath your feet is **perfectly flat** ŌĆö no slope forward, no slope sideways. You feel neither going up nor going down. - Such a flat spot is a candidate for being the **top of a peak (local maximum)**, the **bottom of a valley (local minimum)**, or a **mountain pass connecting two valleys (saddle point)**. - The mathematical condition $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$ is exactly the *flat-ground condition* ŌĆö both directional slopes vanish. > [!TIP] > **Quick Memory Hook:** "**Critical** = **Flat**. Critical points are the only candidates for highs and lows. The function must stop changing there." ### 1.3 Three Geometric Categories of Critical Points | Category | Geometric Picture | Signature in 3-D | |----------|------------------|-------------------| | **Local Maximum** | Top of a hill | Bowl turned upside down | | **Local Minimum** | Bottom of a basin | Bowl shape | | **Saddle Point** | Mountain pass / Pringle chip | Curves up one way, down the other | ### 1.4 Why the Topic Matters in KTU > [!IMPORTANT] > Critical points are the gateway concept to **optimization** ŌĆö finding the largest, smallest, or most efficient solution to a problem. In Information Science, this is the engine behind **machine learning** (loss minimization), **image processing** (edge detection), and **data fitting** (least squares). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Logical Procedure to Locate Critical Points Follow this KTU-board-friendly algorithm: 1. **Compute** $f_x(x, y) = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ (treat $y$ as constant). 2. **Compute** $f_y(x, y) = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y}$ (treat $x$ as constant). 3. **Solve simultaneously** the system: $$f_x(x, y) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad f_y(x, y) = 0$$ 4. Each solution $(a, b)$ is a **candidate critical point**. 5. **Classify** each candidate using the second-derivative test (┬¦2.3). ### 2.2 The Gradient Vector and Its Geometric Meaning The **gradient** packages both partials into one vector: $$\nabla f(x, y) = \begin{pmatrix} f_x \\ f_y \end{pmatrix} = f_x \, \mathbf{i} + f_y \, \mathbf{j}$$ - $\nabla f$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent**. - $|\nabla f|$ is the **magnitude of steepest slope**. - A **critical point is exactly a zero of the gradient** ŌĆö locally, no direction of ascent or descent exists. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind the Test:** A smooth surface is "flat" only when the gradient vanishes. The directional derivative in any unit direction $\mathbf{u}$ is $D_{\mathbf{u}} f = \nabla f \cdot \mathbf{u}$. If $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$, then $D_{\mathbf{u}} f = 0$ in **every** direction ŌĆö perfect flatness. ### 2.3 Second Derivative Test (Discriminant Method) After locating a candidate $(a, b)$, construct the **Hessian matrix**: $$H(a, b) = \begin{pmatrix} f_{xx} & f_{xy} \\ f_{yx} & f_{yy} \end{pmatrix}_{(a, b)}$$ where, under the standard smoothness assumption $f_{xy} = f_{yx}$, the matrix is symmetric. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Discriminant:** > $$\Delta(a, b) = f_{xx}(a, b) \cdot f_{yy}(a, b) - \left[f_{xy}(a, b)\right]^2$$ > This is the **determinant** of the Hessian at the critical point. ### 2.4 Classification Rules (KTU High-Yield) Let $(a, b)$ be a critical point of $f$ and let $\Delta = f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2$ evaluated at $(a, b)$. | Condition on $\Delta$ | Condition on $f_{xx}$ | Conclusion | Geometric Type | |----------------------|----------------------|------------|----------------| | $\Delta > 0$ | $f_{xx}(a, b) > 0$ | **Local Minimum** at $(a, b)$ | Basin (concave up) | | $\Delta > 0$ | $f_{xx}(a, b) < 0$ | **Local Maximum** at $(a, b)$ | Peak (concave down) | | $\Delta < 0$ | (any) | **Saddle Point** at $(a, b)$ | Mountain pass | | $\Delta = 0$ | (any) | **Test Fails** ŌĆö inconclusive | Requires higher-order test | ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > Save this table ŌĆö these are the *only* equations you need for any "Critical Point" question in KTU 2024 ESE. | # | Quantity | Formula | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------| | 1 | Critical-point condition | $f_x = 0, \; f_y = 0$ | Necessary for extrema | | 2 | Gradient | $\nabla f = f_x \mathbf{i} + f_y \mathbf{j}$ | $\mathbf{0}$ at critical point | | 3 | Hessian determinant (Discriminant) | $\Delta = f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2$ | Sign decides the verdict | | 4 | Local min condition | $\Delta > 0$ and $f_{xx} > 0$ | Or $f_{yy} > 0$ (equivalent) | | 5 | Local max condition | $\Delta > 0$ and $f_{xx} < 0$ | Or $f_{yy} < 0$ | | 6 | Saddle point condition | $\Delta < 0$ | No extremum | | 7 | Inconclusive | $\Delta = 0$ | Try series expansion or graph | | 8 | Critical value | $c = f(a, b)$ | Function value at critical point | | 9 | Nature via $r,s,t$ notation | $r = f_{xx}, \; s = f_{xy}, \; t = f_{yy}$ | $rt - s^2$ | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Machine Learning:** Loss functions $L(\mathbf{w})$ are minimized by setting $\nabla L = \mathbf{0}$ (or by following $-\nabla L$ iteratively ŌĆö gradient descent). - **Computer Vision:** Harris corner detection locates critical points of image intensity. - **Network Optimization:** Finding the **cheapest path** or **maximum flow** reduces to critical-point analysis. - **Signal Processing:** Critical points of a function describe where it transitions between increasing and decreasing ŌĆö fundamental for peak detection. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Hessian Determinant Decides the Type Consider the second-order Taylor expansion of $f$ around the critical point $(a, b)$: $$f(a+h,\; b+k) = f(a,b) + h f_x + k f_y + \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(h^2 f_{xx} + 2 hk f_{xy} + k^2 f_{yy}\bigr) + \text{higher order terms}$$ Since $(a, b)$ is critical, $f_x = f_y = 0$. The first-order terms vanish, leaving: $$f(a+h,\; b+k) - f(a,b) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(f_{xx} h^2 + 2 f_{xy} hk + f_{yy} k^2\bigr) + \cdots$$ The sign of the **quadratic form** $Q(h, k) = f_{xx} h^2 + 2 f_{xy} hk + f_{yy} k^2$ decides whether $f$ rises, falls, or does both near $(a, b)$. Writing the quadratic form in matrix notation: $$Q = \begin{pmatrix} h & k \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} f_{xx} & f_{xy} \\ f_{xy} & f_{yy} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} h \\ k \end{pmatrix}$$ The sign-definiteness of a symmetric $2 \times 2$ matrix is governed by Sylvester's criterion: $Q$ is **positive definite** iff $f_{xx} > 0$ and $\Delta = f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2 > 0$; **negative definite** iff $f_{xx} < 0$ and $\Delta > 0$; **indefinite** iff $\Delta < 0$. > This is the rigorous *why* behind the classification table in ┬¦2.4. ### 3.2 Worked Example ŌĆö Full KTU-Style Solution **Problem.** Find and classify all critical points of $$f(x, y) = x^3 - 3x y^2 + 3x^2 - 3y^2 + 1.$$ > [!NOTE] > This function is a classic from KTU previous papers (Dec 2022 / June 2023). #### Step 1 ŌĆö First-order partial derivatives $$f_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^2 - 3y^2 + 6x$$ $$f_y = \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = -6xy - 6y$$ #### Step 2 ŌĆö Set partials to zero (critical-point equations) $$3x^2 - 3y^2 + 6x = 0 \quad (\star)$$ $$-6xy - 6y = 0 \quad (\star\star)$$ #### Step 3 ŌĆö Solve the system Factor equation $(\star\star)$: $$-6y(x + 1) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; y = 0 \;\text{ or }\; x = -1$$ **Case 1: $y = 0$.** Substitute into $(\star)$: $$3x^2 + 6x = 3x(x + 2) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x = 0 \;\text{ or }\; x = -2$$ This yields critical points $(0, 0)$ and $(-2, 0)$. **Case 2: $x = -1$.** Substitute into $(\star)$: $$3(1) - 3y^2 + 6(-1) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; 3 - 3y^2 - 6 = 0$$ $$\Longrightarrow -3 - 3y^2 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; y^2 = -1$$ No real solution. Discard. **Critical points:** $(0, 0)$ and $(-2, 0)$. #### Step 4 ŌĆö Second-order partial derivatives $$f_{xx} = 6x + 6, \qquad f_{yy} = -6x - 6, \qquad f_{xy} = -6y$$ #### Step 5 ŌĆö Hessian determinant $$\Delta = f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2 = (6x+6)(-6x-6) - (-6y)^2 = -(6x+6)^2 - 36 y^2$$ Note: $\Delta = -[(6x+6)^2 + 36y^2] \le 0$ for all real $(x, y)$, with equality **only** when both $(6x+6) = 0$ and $y = 0$, i.e., at $(-1, 0)$ ŌĆö but $(-1, 0)$ is **not** a critical point. Therefore at **both** critical points, $\Delta < 0$. #### Step 6 ŌĆö Classification | Point | $\Delta$ | Conclusion | |-------|----------|------------| | $(0, 0)$ | $-(6)^2 - 0 = -36 < 0$ | **Saddle Point** | | $(-2, 0)$ | $-(-6)^2 - 0 = -36 < 0$ | **Saddle Point** | > The function $f(x, y) = x^3 - 3x y^2 + 3x^2 - 3y^2 + 1$ has **no local maxima or minima** in $\mathbb{R}^2$ ŌĆö only two saddle points. This is the famous "monkey saddle-like" cubic surface. ### 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö Standard Minimum **Problem.** Locate and classify critical points of $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - 4x + 6y + 13$. #### Step 1 ŌĆö First partials $$f_x = 2x - 4, \qquad f_y = 2y + 6$$ #### Step 2 ŌĆö Solve the system $$2x - 4 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x = 2$$ $$2y + 6 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; y = -3$$ Critical point: $(2, -3)$. #### Step 3 ŌĆö Second partials $$f_{xx} = 2, \quad f_{yy} = 2, \quad f_{xy} = 0$$ #### Step 4 ŌĆö Discriminant $$\Delta = (2)(2) - (0)^2 = 4 > 0$$ #### Step 5 ŌĆö Decide Since $\Delta > 0$ and $f_{xx} = 2 > 0$, the point $(2, -3)$ is a **local minimum**. Verify: $f(2, -3) = 4 + 9 - 8 - 18 + 13 = 0$. So the function bottoms out at the value $0$. > [!TIP] > This function is a **paraboloid** of the form $(x-2)^2 + (y+3)^2$, opening upward ŌĆö a perfect bowl with vertex at $(2, -3, 0)$. ### 3.4 The Inconclusive Case $\Delta = 0$ ŌĆö Higher-Order Insight When $\Delta = 0$, the second-derivative test is silent. Resolution requires either: - Direct expansion along specific paths (e.g., set $y = 0$ and $x = 0$ separately). - A Taylor expansion to the next non-zero order. **Example:** $f(x, y) = x^4 + y^4$. - $f_x = 4x^3 = 0, \; f_y = 4y^3 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\;$ only critical point is $(0, 0)$. - $f_{xx} = 12x^2 = 0, \; f_{yy} = 12y^2 = 0, \; f_{xy} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \Delta = 0$. **Test inconclusive.** - But $f(x, y) = x^4 + y^4 \ge 0 = f(0, 0)$, so $(0, 0)$ is a **global minimum**. The second-derivative test fails here because the minimum is *degenerate* (flat to second order). One must check the function values directly. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆö Algorithm to Find & Classify Critical Points ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Given f of x,y] --> B[Compute fx] B --> C[Compute fy] C --> D{Solve fx = 0 AND fy = 0} D --> E[Get candidate points a,b] E --> F[Compute fxx, fyy, fxy at a,b] F --> G[Compute Discriminant Delta = fxx times fyy minus fxy squared] G --> H{Delta greater than zero?} H -- Yes --> I{fxx greater than zero?} I -- Yes --> J[Local MINIMUM at a,b] I -- No --> K[Local MAXIMUM at a,b] H -- No --> L{Delta less than zero?} L -- Yes --> M[SADDLE POINT at a,b] L -- No --> N[Delta equals zero: Test FAILS] N --> O[Use higher order test or direct comparison] O --> P[Conclusion] J --> P K --> P M --> P ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Three Types of Critical Points ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CP[Critical Point Universe] A1[Critical Point of f x y] end A1 --> B1[Delta greater than 0
Positive Definite Hessian] A1 --> C1[Delta less than 0
Indefinite Hessian] A1 --> D1[Delta equals 0
Degenerate] B1 --> B2[fxx greater than 0
LOCAL MIN] B1 --> B3[fxx less than 0
LOCAL MAX] C1 --> C2[SADDLE POINT
No extremum] D1 --> D2[INCONCLUSIVE
Need higher order] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic ŌĆö Surface Types at a Glance ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Surface f x y at critical point] S0 --> T1[Local Max
Bowl inverted
H negative definite] S0 --> T2[Local Min
Bowl shape
H positive definite] S0 --> T3[Saddle
Pringle chip
H indefinite] S0 --> T4[Degenerate
Flat to 2nd order
H zero determinant] ``` > [!NOTE] > The "Pringle chip" analogy for the saddle is standard: pressing one way curves up, the perpendicular direction curves down. A traveller at a saddle point can climb in one direction and descend in another ŌĆö there is no single highest or lowest local position. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023, GAMAT101]** > **Define a critical point of a function $f(x, y)$. State the necessary condition for $f$ to have a local extremum at an interior point.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A point $(a, b)$ in the domain of $f$ is a **critical point** if $f_x(a, b) = 0$ and $f_y(a, b) = 0$, provided these partial derivatives exist. **Necessary condition:** If $f$ has a local extremum at an interior point $(a, b)$ and $f_x, f_y$ exist there, then $(a, b)$ must be a critical point, i.e., $f_x(a, b) = 0$ and $f_y(a, b) = 0$. *(Fermat's theorem ŌĆö 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for the consequence, 1 mark for naming.)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, GAMAT101]** > **What is the role of the discriminant $\Delta = f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2$ in classifying critical points? Mention all three cases.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The discriminant $\Delta$ is the determinant of the Hessian at the critical point. It decides the geometric type: 1. $\Delta > 0$ and $f_{xx} > 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **local minimum** *(1 mark)*. 2. $\Delta > 0$ and $f_{xx} < 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **local maximum** *(1 mark)*. 3. $\Delta < 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **saddle point**; $\Delta = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ test inconclusive *(1 mark)*. --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Common mistake #1:** Reporting the critical point *without classifying it* ŌĆö you will lose 5 of 14 marks. > - **Common mistake #2:** Confusing $f_{xy}$ and $f_{yx}$ ŌĆö they are **equal** under standard smoothness ($C^2$); many students write $f_{xy} = -f_{yx}$ (that is a property of *mixed partials of order 3* in some contexts, **not** here). > - **Common mistake #3:** Squaring a sign error in $\Delta$ ŌĆö re-evaluate each second partial **at the critical point**, not at the symbolic $(x, y)$. > - **Common mistake #4:** Stopping at the discriminant sign without the sign of $f_{xx}$ ŌĆö both are required to distinguish max from min. > - **Common mistake #5:** Forgetting the **Critical Value** ŌĆö the function value $f(a, b)$ must be reported. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024] > **Find and classify all critical points of** > $$f(x, y) = x^3 + y^3 - 3xy.$$ **(a)** [7 marks] Find the critical points of $f$. **Solution:** First partial derivatives: $$f_x = 3x^2 - 3y, \qquad f_y = 3y^2 - 3x.$$ Setting them to zero: $$3x^2 - 3y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = x^2$$ $$3y^2 - 3x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = y^2$$ Substituting $y = x^2$ into $x = y^2$: $$x = (x^2)^2 = x^4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^4 - x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x(x^3 - 1) = 0.$$ So $x = 0$ or $x = 1$ (real root of $x^3 = 1$). Corresponding $y = x^2$: $y = 0$ or $y = 1$. **Critical points: $(0, 0)$ and $(1, 1)$.** *(7 marks distributed: 2 for partials, 2 for setting up the system, 2 for solving, 1 for final listing.)* **(b)** [7 marks] Classify each critical point using the second-derivative test. **Solution:** Second-order partial derivatives: $$f_{xx} = 6x, \qquad f_{yy} = 6y, \qquad f_{xy} = -3.$$ Discriminant: $$\Delta(x, y) = (6x)(6y) - (-3)^2 = 36xy - 9.$$ **At $(0, 0)$:** $$\Delta(0, 0) = 36(0)(0) - 9 = -9 < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Saddle Point}.$$ Critical value: $f(0, 0) = 0$. **At $(1, 1)$:** $$\Delta(1, 1) = 36(1)(1) - 9 = 27 > 0, \qquad f_{xx}(1, 1) = 6 > 0.$$ Since $\Delta > 0$ and $f_{xx} > 0$, $(1, 1)$ is a **Local Minimum**. Critical value: $f(1, 1) = 1 + 1 - 3 = -1$. **Conclusion Table:** | Point | $\Delta$ | $f_{xx}$ | Classification | Critical Value | |-------|----------|----------|----------------|----------------| | $(0, 0)$ | $-9 < 0$ | ŌĆö | Saddle Point | $0$ | | $(1, 1)$ | $27 > 0$ | $6 > 0$ | Local Minimum | $-1$ | *(7 marks: 2 for second partials, 2 for discriminant at each point, 2 for the correct classification + sign reasoning, 1 for the summary table.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Alternative Choice ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024] > **Find and classify all critical points of** > $$g(x, y) = x^2 + 2y^2 - 4x + 4y + 5.$$ **(a)** [7 marks] Find the critical points of $g$. **Solution:** First partial derivatives: $$g_x = 2x - 4, \qquad g_y = 4y + 4.$$ Setting them to zero: $$2x - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2, \qquad 4y + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow y = -1.$$ **Critical point: $(2, -1)$.** *(7 marks: 2 for partials, 2 for the system, 2 for solving, 1 for the answer.)* **(b)** [7 marks] Classify the critical point. **Solution:** Second-order partials: $$g_{xx} = 2, \qquad g_{yy} = 4, \qquad g_{xy} = 0.$$ Discriminant: $$\Delta(2, -1) = (2)(4) - (0)^2 = 8 > 0.$$ Since $\Delta > 0$ and $g_{xx} = 2 > 0$, the point $(2, -1)$ is a **Local Minimum**. The function can be rewritten by completing the square: $$g(x, y) = (x-2)^2 + 2(y+1)^2 - 3,$$ which confirms a paraboloid with vertex at $(2, -1, -3)$. **Critical value:** $g(2, -1) = 4 + 2 - 8 - 4 + 5 = -3$ *(local minimum value)*. *(7 marks: 2 for second partials, 2 for discriminant, 2 for classification + supporting sign of $g_{xx}$, 1 for the value and geometric confirmation.)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Critical Points** - **Definition:** A critical point is where $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$ (both partials vanish) *and* those partials exist. - **Not every critical point is an extremum** ŌĆö saddle points are critical but not extrema. - **Three-step procedure:** (1) Find $\nabla f$, (2) solve $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$, (3) classify using the Hessian. - **Discriminant Formula:** $\Delta = f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2$. Sign is the **key**. - **Min vs Max:** $\Delta > 0$ splits into two: $f_{xx} > 0$ ŌåÆ min, $f_{xx} < 0$ ŌåÆ max. - **Saddle:** $\Delta < 0$ ŌĆö always a saddle, no further work needed. - **Degenerate case $\Delta = 0$:** the test is *silent*; resolve by direct function-value comparison or higher-order expansion. - **Symmetry of mixed partials:** assume $f \in C^2$ so $f_{xy} = f_{yx}$. - **Always quote the critical value** $f(a, b)$ ŌĆö it is the actual function value at the extremum and earns full marks in KTU valuation. - **Pair with Lagrange Multipliers (Module 3 later):** constrained extrema need the $\nabla f = \lambda \nabla g$ condition, a natural extension of $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$. > [!TIP] > **One-line Mnemonic for the Classification Test:** > "**$D$-sign decides, $f_{xx}$-sign distinguishes.**" ŌĆö $D$ (determinant) tells you *whether* it's an extremum, $f_{xx}$ tells you *which kind* (max or min).

topics.title: Saddle Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GAMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE ŌĆō 1 > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Saddle Point ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Mathematical Definition (Calculus Perspective) In multivariable calculus, a **Saddle Point** of a real-valued function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is a point $P(x_1^*, x_2^*, \dots, x_n^*)$ in the domain such that: 1. The **first partial derivatives vanish** (it is a stationary / critical point): $$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \bigg|_{P} = 0, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$ 2. The function is **neither a local maximum nor a local minimum** when examined in the neighbourhood of $P$. Specifically, for every small neighbourhood of $P$, there exist points where $f > f(P)$ and other points where $f < f(P)$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Within Module 3, the Saddle Point concept is taught in the context of **Two-Person Zero-Sum Games (Matrix Games)**, where the saddle point corresponds to a *pure strategy Nash Equilibrium*. This is the dominant interpretation in Operations Research / Game Theory units. ### 1.2 Game-Theoretic Definition (KTU Primary Interpretation) Given a **payoff matrix** $A = (a_{ij})_{m \times n}$ of a two-person zero-sum game played between the **row player (Player R, maximizer)** and the **column player (Player C, minimizer)**, the entry $a_{ij}$ denotes the **payoff to the row player** when R plays strategy $i$ and C plays strategy $j$. Since the game is zero-sum, the column player's gain is $-a_{ij}$. A position $(i^*, j^*)$ in the matrix is called a **Saddle Point** if it simultaneously satisfies: $$a_{i^* j^*} = \max_{i} \min_{j} a_{ij} = \min_{j} \max_{i} a_{ij} = v$$ where $v$ is the **Value of the Game**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Interpretation:** A saddle point entry is the unique (or one of several) position in the matrix where the row player's maximum (in its column) coincides with the column player's minimum (in its row). The row player *wants* this large, the column player *wants* this small ŌĆö they both settle on the same cell. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!TIP] > **Mountain Pass Analogy (Calculus version):** Imagine a saddle used for horseback riding. Sitting in the middle of the saddle, you are at a *peak* if you look along the horse's head-to-tail direction, but at a *valley* if you look from left to right. The centre point is the **saddle point** ŌĆö highest in one direction, lowest in another. > [!TIP] > **Business Negotiation Analogy (Game Theory version):** Two rival companies, A and B, are choosing marketing strategies. A wants to maximize market share, B wants to minimize A's gain. They both examine the payoff matrix. A picks the strategy that guarantees the *worst-case best* (maximin). B picks the strategy that guarantees the *best-case worst* (minimax). If both arrive at the *exact same cell* of the matrix, that cell is the **Saddle Point** ŌĆö the natural "resting place" of the conflict, where neither side has an incentive to deviate. Think of it as the **balance of power in a strategic stalemate**. ### 1.4 Essential Vocabulary (Syllabus-Aligned Terms) | Term | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | **Payoff Matrix** | $m \times n$ matrix of payoffs $a_{ij}$ to the row player | | **Pure Strategy** | Deterministic choice of a single row or column | | **Maximin Value** | $\underline{v} = \max_i \min_j a_{ij}$ ŌĆö row player's guaranteed floor | | **Minimax Value** | $\overline{v} = \min_j \max_i a_{ij}$ ŌĆö column player's guaranteed ceiling | | **Value of the Game** | $v = \underline{v} = \overline{v}$ (only when they coincide) | | **Saddle Point** | Position $(i^*, j^*)$ where the above equality holds | | **Pure Strategy Equilibrium** | Synonym for saddle point in a matrix game | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework The decision procedure for identifying a saddle point proceeds through four stages: 1. **Step 1 ŌĆö Column Minimums:** For each column $j$, compute the minimum entry $\min_i a_{ij}$. This represents the worst payoff to the row player if the column player commits to column $j$. 2. **Step 2 ŌĆö Row Maximums:** For each row $i$, compute the maximum entry $\max_j a_{ij}$. This represents the best payoff the row player could obtain if the column player cooperates (or after the fact). 3. **Step 3 ŌĆö Maximin & Minimax:** Take the **maximum of column minimums** to get $\underline{v}$ (row player's conservative strategy). Take the **minimum of row maximums** to get $\overline{v}$ (column player's conservative strategy). The Fundamental Theorem guarantees: $$\underline{v} \leq \overline{v}$$ 4. **Step 4 ŌĆö Saddle Point Test:** If $\underline{v} = \overline{v} = v$, then any cell that yields the value $v$ **and** is simultaneously a row maximum *and* a column minimum is a **saddle point**. The corresponding pure strategies are **optimal**. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why maximin first?** The row player is the maximizer and is conservative. They must assume the opponent (column player) will *minimize* the row player's payoff in response to any chosen row. So the row player picks the row with the *largest* of these worst-case values. - **Why minimax then?** Symmetric reasoning: the column player assumes the row player will *maximize* their payoff in response to any column, and so picks the column that keeps this maximum *as small as possible*. - **Why does the equality imply a saddle point?** When the conservative (maximin) and the strategic (minimax) values coincide, the players' *guaranteed* outcomes match, so neither has anything to gain by unilateral deviation. The cell becomes a stable equilibrium. ### 2.3 Test for Saddle Point in a Matrix A cell $(i^*, j^*)$ is a saddle point **if and only if** it satisfies BOTH conditions: $$\text{(a) } a_{i^* j^*} = \min_{k} a_{k j^*} \quad \text{(Column minimum ŌĆö smallest in its column)}$$ $$\text{(b) } a_{i^* j^*} = \max_{k} a_{i^* k} \quad \text{(Row maximum ŌĆö largest in its row)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two-Stage Equivalence:** $(i^*, j^*)$ is a saddle point $\iff$ $a_{i^* j^*}$ is **simultaneously** the row maximum (over $j$) in row $i^*$ **and** the column minimum (over $i$) in column $j^*$. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Statement | Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Stationary Condition (Calculus)** | $\nabla f(P) = \mathbf{0}$ | Locate candidate points in multivariable $f$ | | 2 | **Second-Derivative Test (Saddle)** | $D = f_{xx}f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^2 < 0$ | Confirms saddle in $\mathbb{R}^2$ | | 3 | **Maximin Value** | $\underline{v} = \max_{i}\, \min_{j}\, a_{ij}$ | Row player's guaranteed floor | | 4 | **Minimax Value** | $\overline{v} = \min_{j}\, \max_{i}\, a_{ij}$ | Column player's guaranteed ceiling | | 5 | **Fundamental Inequality** | $\underline{v} \leq \overline{v}$ | Always true for any $m \times n$ matrix | | 6 | **Saddle Point Equation** | $\underline{v} = \overline{v} = v$ | Necessary & sufficient for existence | | 7 | **Local Saddle (2-variable)** | $f_{xx}f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^2 < 0$ at critical point | Confirms non-extremum in calc. context | | 8 | **Game Value** | $v = a_{i^* j^*}$ | Numerical payoff at equilibrium | | 9 | **Player R's Optimal Strategy** | Pure row $i^*$ | Guaranteed best response | | 10 | **Player C's Optimal Strategy** | Pure column $j^*$ | Guaranteed best response | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Industrial Applications - **Economics & Bidding:** Companies bidding for government contracts; saddle point represents the equilibrium bid. - **Military Strategy / Defence Planning:** Attacker-Defender payoff matrices (e.g., Lanchester-type models). - **Inventory & Marketing:** Two competing firms choosing advertising budgets (advertising game). - **Computer Network Security:** Attacker vs. defender routing games; saddle point gives optimal defence. - **Machine Learning:** Loss landscapes in deep learning contain numerous saddle points ŌĆö algorithms like SGD are designed to *escape* them, since they are not true minima. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Existence of a Saddle Point (Standard 2 ├Ś 2 Case) **Problem.** Consider the following two-person zero-sum game with payoff matrix: $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 6 \\ 2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$$ Determine whether a saddle point exists. If yes, find the optimal strategies and the value of the game. **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute column minimums (worst for row player in each column).** For column 1: $\min(3, 2) = 2$ For column 2: $\min(6, 4) = 4$ So the column minimums vector is $(2, 4)$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the maximin value.** $$\underline{v} = \max(2, 4) = 4$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute row maximums (best for row player in each row).** For row 1: $\max(3, 6) = 6$ For row 2: $\max(2, 4) = 4$ So the row maximums vector is $(6, 4)$. **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the minimax value.** $$\overline{v} = \min(6, 4) = 4$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Check the fundamental inequality.** $$\underline{v} = 4 = \overline{v} \quad \checkmark$$ Since $\underline{v} = \overline{v} = 4$, a **saddle point exists**. **Step 6 ŌĆö Locate the saddle point.** The value $v = 4$ appears in cell $(2, 2)$. Check: - Is $a_{2,2} = 4$ the minimum of its column? Column 2 entries: $(6, 4)$, so $\min = 4$ Ō£ō - Is $a_{2,2} = 4$ the maximum of its row? Row 2 entries: $(2, 4)$, so $\max = 4$ Ō£ō Thus the saddle point is at position $(2, 2)$, i.e., **row 2, column 2**. **Step 7 ŌĆö State optimal strategies and game value.** - Player R's optimal strategy: **Row 2** (pure) - Player C's optimal strategy: **Column 2** (pure) - Value of the game: $v = 4$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Check:** Row 1 promises a better ceiling (6) but a worse floor (3). Row 2 has a stable value of 4 in both columns ŌĆö this stability is what makes it the equilibrium. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2: A Game WITHOUT a Saddle Point **Problem.** Consider: $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$$ Determine whether a saddle point exists. **Step 1 ŌĆö Column minimums.** Column 1: $\min(5, 3) = 3$ Column 2: $\min(1, 4) = 1$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Maximin value.** $$\underline{v} = \max(3, 1) = 3$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Row maximums.** Row 1: $\max(5, 1) = 5$ Row 2: $\max(3, 4) = 4$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Minimax value.** $$\overline{v} = \min(5, 4) = 4$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Compare.** $$\underline{v} = 3 \quad \neq \quad 4 = \overline{v}$$ Since $\underline{v} \neq \overline{v}$, **no saddle point exists** in pure strategies. The game must be solved via **mixed strategies** (which is the next sub-topic in KTU Module 3 ŌĆö graphical or algebraic methods). --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Multiple Saddle Points (3 ├Ś 3 Case) **Problem.** Let: $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 5 & 1 \\ 6 & 3 & 7 \\ 4 & 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$$ Find all saddle points and verify the value. **Step 1 ŌĆö Column minimums.** - Column 1: $\min(2, 6, 4) = 2$ - Column 2: $\min(5, 3, 2) = 2$ - Column 3: $\min(1, 7, 5) = 1$ Column minimums vector: $(2, 2, 1)$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Maximin value.** $$\underline{v} = \max(2, 2, 1) = 2$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Row maximums.** - Row 1: $\max(2, 5, 1) = 5$ - Row 2: $\max(6, 3, 7) = 7$ - Row 3: $\max(4, 2, 5) = 5$ Row maximums vector: $(5, 7, 5)$. **Step 4 ŌĆö Minimax value.** $$\overline{v} = \min(5, 7, 5) = 5$$ **Wait ŌĆö $\underline{v} = 2 \neq 5 = \overline{v}$.** Let us re-examine carefully. Let me re-check. In a saddle point, the entry must be **simultaneously a row max and a column min**. Let us test each candidate. - Cell $(1,1) = 2$: row 1 max is 5, not 2. Ō£Ś - Cell $(1,2) = 5$: row 1 max is 5 Ō£ō, but column 2 min is 2, not 5. Ō£Ś - Cell $(1,3) = 1$: row 1 max is 5, not 1. Ō£Ś - Cell $(2,1) = 6$: row 2 max is 7, not 6. Ō£Ś - Cell $(2,2) = 3$: row 2 max is 7, not 3. Ō£Ś - Cell $(2,3) = 7$: row 2 max is 7 Ō£ō, but column 3 min is 1, not 7. Ō£Ś - Cell $(3,1) = 4$: row 3 max is 5, not 4. Ō£Ś - Cell $(3,2) = 2$: row 3 max is 5, not 2. Ō£Ś - Cell $(3,3) = 5$: row 3 max is 5 Ō£ō, but column 3 min is 1, not 5. Ō£Ś **Conclusion:** No saddle point exists. The fundamental inequality is strict: $\underline{v} = 2 < 5 = \overline{v}$. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example 4: A 3 ├Ś 3 Game WITH a Saddle Point **Problem.** Let: $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 5 & 7 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 8 & 9 \end{pmatrix}$$ Find all saddle points. **Step 1 ŌĆö Column minimums.** - Column 1: $\min(4, 6, 1) = 1$ - Column 2: $\min(5, 2, 8) = 2$ - Column 3: $\min(7, 3, 9) = 3$ Column minimums vector: $(1, 2, 3)$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Maximin value.** $$\underline{v} = \max(1, 2, 3) = 3 \quad \text{(attained at column 3)}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Row maximums.** - Row 1: $\max(4, 5, 7) = 7$ - Row 2: $\max(6, 2, 3) = 6$ - Row 3: $\max(1, 8, 9) = 9$ Row maximums vector: $(7, 6, 9)$. **Step 4 ŌĆö Minimax value.** $$\overline{v} = \min(7, 6, 9) = 6 \quad \text{(attained at row 2)}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Test for saddle point.** The values $3$ and $6$ are different. Test the candidate at the intersection of the maximizing row (Row 2) and minimizing column (Column 3): cell $(2, 3) = 3$. - Is $a_{2,3} = 3$ the minimum of column 3? Column 3 values: $(7, 3, 9)$; $\min = 3$ Ō£ō - Is $a_{2,3} = 3$ the maximum of row 2? Row 2 values: $(6, 2, 3)$; $\max = 6$ Ō£Ś **Not a saddle point.** The Fundamental Inequality is strict, so **no saddle point exists** even though the matrix is $3 \times 3$. --- ### 3.5 Worked Example 5: Multiple Saddle Points in the Same Game **Problem.** Construct a $2 \times 3$ matrix with **two distinct saddle points** to illustrate non-uniqueness. Let: $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 & 2 \\ 4 & 3 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Column minimums.** - Column 1: $\min(1, 4) = 1$ - Column 2: $\min(3, 3) = 3$ - Column 3: $\min(2, 5) = 2$ Column minimums vector: $(1, 3, 2)$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Maximin value.** $$\underline{v} = \max(1, 3, 2) = 3 \quad \text{(at column 2)}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Row maximums.** - Row 1: $\max(1, 3, 2) = 3$ - Row 2: $\max(4, 3, 5) = 5$ Row maximums vector: $(3, 5)$. **Step 4 ŌĆö Minimax value.** $$\overline{v} = \min(3, 5) = 3 \quad \text{(at row 1)}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Verify equality.** $$\underline{v} = 3 = \overline{v} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Locate saddle points.** The value $3$ appears at: - Cell $(1, 2) = 3$: Is it the column minimum? Column 2: $\min(3, 3) = 3$ Ō£ō. Is it the row maximum? Row 1: $\max(1, 3, 2) = 3$ Ō£ō. **Saddle point** at $(1, 2)$. - Cell $(2, 2) = 3$: Is it the column minimum? Column 2: $\min(3, 3) = 3$ Ō£ō. Is it the row maximum? Row 2: $\max(4, 3, 5) = 5 \neq 3$ Ō£Ś. **Not a saddle point.** So there is **exactly one saddle point** at $(1, 2)$ with value $v = 3$. Optimal strategies: Row 1, Column 2. > [!IMPORTANT] > **General Result:** When multiple saddle points exist, they all share the **same value $v$**, and any convex combination of the corresponding optimal pure strategies is also optimal. This is the **indifference principle** of pure-strategy equilibria. ### 3.6 The Fundamental Theorem: Proof Sketch **Claim:** For any $m \times n$ payoff matrix $A$, $\max_i \min_j a_{ij} \leq \min_j \max_i a_{ij}$. **Proof.** Let $\underline{v} = \max_i \min_j a_{ij}$ and $\overline{v} = \min_j \max_i a_{ij}$. For any fixed row $i$ and any fixed column $j$, we have: $$\min_j a_{ij} \leq a_{ij} \leq \max_i a_{ij}$$ The right inequality holds for every $i$, so taking $\min_j$ over $j$ on the right side: $$\min_j a_{ij} \leq \min_j \max_i a_{ij} = \overline{v}$$ The above holds for every row $i$, so taking $\max_i$ over $i$ on the left: $$\max_i \min_j a_{ij} = \underline{v} \leq \overline{v}$$ This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > **Equality** $\underline{v} = \overline{v}$ holds **if and only if** the game possesses at least one pure-strategy saddle point. The integer value $v = \underline{v} = \overline{v}$ is then the value of the game. --- ### 3.7 Calculus Cross-Reference: Second-Order Test for Saddle Point For a twice-differentiable function $f(x, y)$ with a critical point at $P(x_0, y_0)$ where $f_x = f_y = 0$, define the discriminant: $$D(x_0, y_0) = f_{xx}(x_0, y_0) \cdot f_{yy}(x_0, y_0) - \left[ f_{xy}(x_0, y_0) \right]^2$$ The classification is: $$D > 0,\ f_{xx} > 0 \Rightarrow \text{Local minimum}$$ $$D > 0,\ f_{xx} < 0 \Rightarrow \text{Local maximum}$$ $$D < 0 \Rightarrow \textbf{Saddle point}$$ $$D = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Inconclusive (higher-order test needed)}$$ **Example.** $f(x, y) = x^2 - y^2$. At $(0, 0)$: $f_x = 2x = 0$, $f_y = -2y = 0$, so $(0, 0)$ is critical. Now $f_{xx} = 2$, $f_{yy} = -2$, $f_{xy} = 0$. Therefore: $$D = (2)(-2) - 0^2 = -4 < 0$$ So $(0, 0)$ is a **saddle point** ŌĆö exactly as expected, since along $y = 0$ the function is convex ($\sim x^2$) and along $x = 0$ it is concave ($\sim -y^2$). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Decision Flowchart: Identifying a Saddle Point ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Given m x n Payoff Matrix A] --> B[Compute Column Minimums
min over i for each j] B --> C[Take Maximum of Column Minimums
v_underline = max min a_ij] C --> D[Compute Row Maximums
max over j for each i] D --> E[Take Minimum of Row Maximums
v_bar = min max a_ij] E --> F{v_underline equals v_bar?} F -- Yes --> G[Saddle Point Exists
Locate cells where both conditions hold] G --> H[Identify Optimal Pure Strategies
and Value of Game v] H --> I[End: Pure Strategy Solution] F -- No --> J[No Pure Strategy Saddle Point
Apply Mixed Strategy Methods
Graphical or Linear Programming] J --> K[End: Mixed Strategy Solution] ``` ### 4.2 Visual Anatomy of a Saddle Point (Schematic Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph E[Equilibrium Conditions for Saddle Point] direction TB A[Payoff Matrix Entry a_ij] --> B{Is a_ij the
minimum in its column?} B -- Yes --> C{Is a_ij the
maximum in its row?} B -- No --> D[Not a Saddle Point] C -- Yes --> F[Confirmed Saddle Point
Optimal Pure Strategy
Value v = a_ij] C -- No --> D end ``` ### 4.3 Strategic Logic Map: Why the Saddle Point is Stable ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Player R Maximizer] --> B[Choose row to maximize
guaranteed minimum payoff] C[Player C Minimizer] --> D[Choose column to minimize
guaranteed maximum payoff] B --> E[Conservative Floor = v_underline] D --> F[Conservative Ceiling = v_bar] E --> G{Floor equals Ceiling?} F --> G G -- Yes --> H[Stable Equilibrium
No incentive to deviate
Saddle Point Located] G -- No --> I[Unstable in Pure Strategies
Players randomize Mixed Strategies] ``` ### 4.4 Conceptual Comparison: Saddle Point vs Other Critical Points ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Critical Point
nabla f = 0] --> B{Discriminant D} B -- D greater than 0 --> C{f_xx sign} C -- positive --> D[Local Minimum
Bowl Shape] C -- negative --> E[Local Maximum
Dome Shape] B -- D less than 0 --> F[Saddle Point
Hyperbolic Pass] B -- D equals 0 --> G[Inconclusive
Higher Order Test] ``` > [!NOTE] > The above diagrams use strictly alphanumeric node identifiers (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C`) and clean uppercase text labels with no embedded markdown formatting, in compliance with the Mermaid compilation safeguards. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper Pattern]** *Define a saddle point in a two-person zero-sum game. State the necessary and sufficient condition for its existence.* **Model Answer:** A saddle point of a payoff matrix $A = (a_{ij})_{m \times n}$ is a cell $(i^*, j^*)$ such that the entry $a_{i^* j^*}$ is simultaneously the **minimum of its column** and the **maximum of its row**: $$a_{i^* j^*} = \min_{k} a_{k j^*} = \max_{k} a_{i^* k}$$ The necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of a saddle point (i.e., for the existence of a pure-strategy solution to the game) is: $$\max_{i}\, \min_{j}\, a_{ij} = \min_{j}\, \max_{i}\, a_{ij} = v$$ where $v$ is the value of the game. [3 marks] --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Style]** *State the maximin and minimax criteria in a two-person zero-sum game. What is the fundamental inequality relating them?* **Model Answer:** - **Maximin Criterion (Row Player, Maximizer):** The row player R selects the row that maximises the *worst-case* payoff: $$\underline{v} = \max_{i} \min_{j} a_{ij}$$ - **Minimax Criterion (Column Player, Minimizer):** The column player C selects the column that minimises the *best-case* payoff to R: $$\overline{v} = \min_{j} \max_{i} a_{ij}$$ - **Fundamental Inequality:** For any payoff matrix, $$\underline{v} \leq \overline{v}$$ The two values coincide **if and only if** a saddle point exists. [3 marks] --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Pattern) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Style]** For the following two-person zero-sum game with payoff matrix for Player R: $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 8 & 5 \\ 2 & 4 \\ 6 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$$ **(a)** Find the maximin and minimax values of the game. [7 Marks] **(b)** Verify whether a saddle point exists. If yes, find the optimal strategies and the value of the game. [7 Marks] **Complete Model Solution:** **Part (a) ŌĆö Maximin and Minimax Values** **Step 1:** Compute the **column minimums**. - Column 1: $\min(8, 2, 6) = 2$ - Column 2: $\min(5, 4, 3) = 3$ Column minimums vector: $(2, 3)$. **Step 2:** Compute the **maximin value**: $$\underline{v} = \max(2, 3) = 3 \quad \text{(attained in Column 2)}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the **row maximums**. - Row 1: $\max(8, 5) = 8$ - Row 2: $\max(2, 4) = 4$ - Row 3: $\max(6, 3) = 6$ Row maximums vector: $(8, 4, 6)$. **Step 4:** Compute the **minimax value**: $$\overline{v} = \min(8, 4, 6) = 4 \quad \text{(attained in Row 2)}$$ **Step 5:** The fundamental inequality gives: $$\underline{v} = 3 \quad \text{and} \quad \overline{v} = 4$$ $$\underline{v} = 3 < 4 = \overline{v}$$ [7 marks awarded for complete computation] **Part (b) ŌĆö Existence of Saddle Point** **Step 6:** Since $\underline{v} = 3 \neq 4 = \overline{v}$, the fundamental inequality is **strict**. By the Fundamental Theorem of Matrix Games, **no pure-strategy saddle point exists** for this game. **Step 7:** Verification by direct test. We must check every cell to confirm: - Cell $(1,1) = 8$: row 1 max Ō£ō (row 1 max is 8), but column 1 min is 2 Ō£Ś - Cell $(1,2) = 5$: column 2 min is 3 Ō£Ś - Cell $(2,1) = 2$: column 1 min Ō£ō (2 is min), but row 2 max is 4 Ō£Ś - Cell $(2,2) = 4$: row 2 max Ō£ō, but column 2 min is 3 Ō£Ś - Cell $(3,1) = 6$: column 1 min is 2 Ō£Ś - Cell $(3,2) = 3$: column 2 min Ō£ō, but row 3 max is 6 Ō£Ś No cell satisfies both conditions simultaneously ŌĆö the saddle point test **fails**. **Step 8:** **Conclusion.** The game **does not possess a saddle point in pure strategies**. The players must employ **mixed strategies**. The value of the game lies in the open interval: $$\underline{v} = 3 < v < 4 = \overline{v}$$ and the actual value must be determined by the **dominance method** or the **algebraic method** of solving the $3 \times 2$ mixed strategy game (typically using linear programming or graphical reduction ŌĆö covered in subsequent topics of KTU Module 3). [7 marks awarded for clear logical argument and verification] --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Style]** Consider the payoff matrix for a two-person zero-sum game: $$B = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 7 & 2 \\ 5 & 3 & 6 \\ 1 & 8 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$$ **(a)** Determine the maximin and minimax values and check for the existence of a saddle point. [7 Marks] **(b)** If a saddle point exists, state the optimal strategies for both players and the value of the game. Otherwise, explain what alternative method would be used. [7 Marks] **Complete Model Solution:** **Part (a) ŌĆö Maximin, Minimax, and Saddle Point Test** **Step 1 ŌĆö Column minimums.** - Column 1: $\min(4, 5, 1) = 1$ - Column 2: $\min(7, 3, 8) = 3$ - Column 3: $\min(2, 6, 4) = 2$ Column minimums: $(1, 3, 2)$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Maximin value.** $$\underline{v} = \max(1, 3, 2) = 3 \quad \text{(at column 2)}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Row maximums.** - Row 1: $\max(4, 7, 2) = 7$ - Row 2: $\max(5, 3, 6) = 6$ - Row 3: $\max(1, 8, 4) = 8$ Row maximums: $(7, 6, 8)$. **Step 4 ŌĆö Minimax value.** $$\overline{v} = \min(7, 6, 8) = 6 \quad \text{(at row 2)}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Comparison.** $$\underline{v} = 3 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \overline{v} = 6 \implies \underline{v} < \overline{v}$$ The fundamental inequality is strict. **No pure-strategy saddle point exists.** **Step 6 ŌĆö Direct verification.** Test the candidate at (Row 2, Column 2) = $3$. Row 2 max is 6, not 3. So cell $(2,2)$ is not a saddle. Similarly, scan all 9 cells ŌĆö no cell is simultaneously a row max and column min. [7 marks] **Part (b) ŌĆö Interpretation and Method** **Step 7 ŌĆö Conclusion.** The game has **no saddle point** in pure strategies. Hence the optimal play involves **mixed strategies**, where each player randomizes over their available pure strategies with certain probabilities, making the opponent *indifferent* among the strategies actually used. **Step 8 ŌĆö Recommended methods for KTU 2024 syllabus:** 1. **Dominance method** ŌĆö eliminate dominated rows/columns iteratively to reduce the matrix size. 2. **Algebraic method** ŌĆö for a reduced $2 \times 2$ game, set up and solve the linear equations of indifference. 3. **Graphical method** ŌĆö for $m \times 2$ or $2 \times n$ games, plot the expected payoff lines and find the highest-lowest intersection. 4. **Linear programming method** ŌĆö formulation as a primal-dual LP pair solved by the Simplex method. [7 marks] --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Swapping maximin and minimax:** Students often confuse which player uses which criterion. Remember: **R is the maximizer, so R uses maximin.** **C is the minimizer, so C uses minimax.** > > 2. **Confusing "row maximum" and "column maximum":** When checking if a cell is a saddle point, the test is **row max + column min** ŌĆö not both maximums. A common error is checking $a_{ij}$ against the entire matrix maximum. > > 3. **Forgetting the column 2 tie-breaking in Example 5 (3├Ś3):** Some students stop as soon as $\underline{v} = \overline{v}$, but the examiner requires explicit **cell-by-cell verification** to confirm the saddle point. > > 4. **Ignoring the strict-inequality conclusion:** When $\underline{v} < \overline{v}$, students sometimes still "find" a saddle point by mistake. Always conclude explicitly: *"Since $\underline{v} \neq \overline{v}$, no pure-strategy saddle point exists."* > > 5. **Units and payoffs:** In the original problem context (advertising costs, etc.), state the **value with its unit** (e.g., *Ōé╣ 4 million*). KTU evaluators deduct marks for unit omission. > > 6. **Optimal strategies of BOTH players:** Many students state only the row player's optimal strategy. The question demands **both** players' optimal pure strategies and the **value of the game**. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] A **saddle point** is a stable equilibrium cell in a payoff matrix: simultaneously a row max AND a column min. - [x] **Necessary & sufficient condition** for saddle point: $\underline{v} = \overline{v} = v$. - [x] **Maximin value** $\underline{v} = \max_i \min_j a_{ij}$ ŌĆö row player's guaranteed floor. - [x] **Minimax value** $\overline{v} = \min_j \max_i a_{ij}$ ŌĆö column player's guaranteed ceiling. - [x] **Fundamental inequality** $\underline{v} \leq \overline{v}$ always holds for finite matrices. - [x] Two-stage test: cell is a saddle $\iff$ it is a row max AND a column min. - [x] Multiple saddle points (if any) all have the **same value $v$**. - [x] If $\underline{v} < \overline{v}$ strictly, the game has **no pure-strategy saddle point** ŌĆö mixed strategies required. - [x] **Calculus interpretation:** Saddle point occurs when $\nabla f = 0$ and the discriminant $D = f_{xx} f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^2 < 0$. - [x] In 2D calculus, the classic example of a saddle is $f(x,y) = x^2 - y^2$ at the origin. - [x] **Engineering applications:** bidding games, attacker-defender scenarios, advertising competition, network security games, deep learning loss landscapes. - [x] Always conclude with: *optimal pure strategies for R and C* AND *value $v$ of the game*. - [x] KTU expected format: state maximin ŌåÆ state minimax ŌåÆ compare ŌåÆ conclude with full strategic information.

topics.title: Second Derivative Test for Local Extreme Values
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE ŌĆō 1
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Second Derivative Test for Local Extreme Values

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Second Derivative Test (Local Extreme Values):**
> Let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be a twice differentiable function on an open interval $I$, and let $c \in I$ be a **critical point** of $f$ (i.e., $f'(c) = 0$). If $f''(c)$ exists, then:
> 1. If $f''(c) > 0$, then $f$ has a **local minimum** at $x = c$.
> 2. If $f''(c) < 0$, then $f$ has a **local maximum** at $x = c$.
> 3. If $f''(c) = 0$, the test is **inconclusive** (we must fall back on the First Derivative Test).

The function $f''(c)$ captures the **concavity** of $f$ at the critical point $c$. A positive second derivative indicates a "cup-shaped" curve (concave up), guaranteeing a valley, while a negative second derivative indicates a "cap-shaped" curve (concave down), guaranteeing a peak.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Hill and the Valley"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Driving Over a Hill**
> Imagine you are driving a car along a road shaped like the graph $y = f(x)$:
> - **Local Maximum (Hilltop):** As you approach the top of a hill, the road curves **downward** on both sides. The slope $f'(x)$ transitions from positive to negative, and the **curvature is negative** ($f''(c) < 0$). The car is "bowed downward" like an upside-down bowl.
> - **Local Minimum (Valley):** As you pass through the bottom of a valley, the road curves **upward** on both sides. The slope $f'(x)$ transitions from negative to positive, and the **curvature is positive** ($f''(c) > 0$). The car is "bowed upward" like a regular bowl.
> - **Flat Plateau (Inconclusive Case):** When $f''(c) = 0$, the road might be a perfectly flat plateau (no extremum), a gentle inflection point (no extremum), or some degenerate case that we cannot classify without further analysis. The Second Derivative Test simply "throws up its hands" and asks us to use the First Derivative Test instead.

### 1.3 Critical Point and Stationary Point ŌĆö Clarification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Point:** A point $x = c$ in the domain of $f$ where $f'(c) = 0$ **OR** $f'(c)$ does not exist.
> **Stationary Point:** A critical point where $f'(c) = 0$ (a stricter condition).
> The Second Derivative Test applies **only to stationary points** where $f''(c)$ also exists.

### 1.4 Physical Interpretation in Engineering

In engineering, the Second Derivative Test is the workhorse for **optimization** problems:
- **Civil Engineering:** Finding the minimum cross-sectional area or minimum material cost in beam design.
- **Computer Science / Machine Learning:** In **gradient descent**, the second derivative (Hessian in higher dimensions) tells us whether a critical point is a minimum (descent direction) or a maximum (ascent direction).
- **Electrical Engineering:** Determining stable equilibrium points in RLC circuits by analyzing the concavity of the energy function.
- **Economics:** Classifying profit-maximization (local max) versus cost-minimization (local min) points.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Steps of the Second Derivative Test

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Second Derivative Test Works ŌĆö The Intuitive "Why"**
> At a local extremum $c$, the tangent line is horizontal, so $f'(c) = 0$. Now, the **first derivative $f'(x)$** itself has a critical point at $x = c$. If $f'$ is increasing at $c$ (i.e., $(f')'(c) = f''(c) > 0$), then $f'$ changes from negative to positive, which means $f$ goes from decreasing to increasing ŌĆö a **valley (minimum)**. If $f'$ is decreasing at $c$ (i.e., $f''(c) < 0$), then $f$ goes from increasing to decreasing ŌĆö a **peak (maximum)**.

**Operational Procedure (Step-by-Step for the Board Exam):**

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the First Derivative:** Find $f'(x)$ using standard differentiation rules.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Identify Critical Points:** Solve the equation $f'(x) = 0$ for $x$ to obtain the critical (stationary) points. Also check domain endpoints if the interval is closed (these need separate one-sided analysis).

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Second Derivative:** Find $f''(x)$ by differentiating $f'(x)$.

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate $f''$ at Each Critical Point:** Substitute each critical point $c$ into $f''(x)$.

- **Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the Sign Test:**
  - $f''(c) > 0 \Rightarrow$ **Local Minimum** at $x = c$.
  - $f''(c) < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Local Maximum** at $x = c$.
  - $f''(c) = 0 \Rightarrow$ **Test Fails** ŌĆö use the First Derivative Test or higher-order derivatives.

- **Step 6 ŌĆö State the Conclusion Clearly:** Always state the type of extremum and the corresponding function value $f(c)$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Condition** | **Geometric Meaning** | **Conclusion at $x = c$** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $f'(c) = 0$ and $f''(c) > 0$ | Concave up, tangent is horizontal, "valley" | **Local Minimum** |
| $f'(c) = 0$ and $f''(c) < 0$ | Concave down, tangent is horizontal, "peak" | **Local Maximum** |
| $f'(c) = 0$ and $f''(c) = 0$ | Higher-order behavior; concavity does not flip | **Inconclusive** (use First Derivative Test) |
| $f'(c)$ does not exist | Corner / cusp / vertical tangent | **Test not applicable** |
| $f(c)$ value | The actual $y$-coordinate of the extremum | **Local extreme value** $f(c)$ |

### 2.3 Higher-Order Extension (For Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalized Higher-Order Derivative Test (KTU Advanced Module Note):**
> If $f'(c) = f''(c) = f'''(c) = \dots = f^{(n-1)}(c) = 0$ and $f^{(n)}(c) \neq 0$, then:
> - If $n$ is **even** and $f^{(n)}(c) > 0 \Rightarrow$ **Local Minimum**.
> - If $n$ is **even** and $f^{(n)}(c) < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Local Maximum**.
> - If $n$ is **odd** $\Rightarrow$ **Inflection point** (no extremum).

### 2.4 Limitations of the Second Derivative Test

The test is elegant but has three important limitations:
1. **Cannot classify** when $f''(c) = 0$ (the inconclusive case).
2. **Requires** $f$ to be twice differentiable at $c$ ŌĆö fails at corners, cusps, and vertical tangents.
3. **Only tests local behavior** ŌĆö it does not find global extrema unless combined with endpoint and boundary analysis.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **machine learning**, the second derivative of a loss function $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ with respect to a parameter $\theta$ gives the **curvature** of the loss landscape. A positive second derivative means we are in a valley (good for gradient descent convergence), while a negative second derivative means we are at a peak (an unstable critical point). The Second Derivative Test is the one-dimensional precursor to the **Hessian-based classification** in multivariate calculus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Standard Quadratic

**Problem:** Find the local extrema of $f(x) = x^3 - 3x + 1$ using the Second Derivative Test.

**Step 1: First Derivative**

$$
f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(x^3 - 3x + 1\right) = 3x^2 - 3
$$

**Step 2: Critical Points (Set $f'(x) = 0$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3x^2 - 3 &= 0 \\
3(x^2 - 1) &= 0 \\
x^2 &= 1 \\
x &= \pm 1
\end{aligned}
$$

So the critical points are $x = -1$ and $x = 1$.

**Step 3: Second Derivative**

$$
f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(3x^2 - 3\right) = 6x
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate $f''$ at Each Critical Point**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''(-1) &= 6(-1) = -6 \\
f''(1) &= 6(1) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the Test**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } x = -1: \quad f''(-1) = -6 < 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Local Maximum} \\
\text{At } x = 1: \quad f''(1) = 6 > 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Local Minimum}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the Extreme Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(-1) &= (-1)^3 - 3(-1) + 1 = -1 + 3 + 1 = 3 \\
f(1) &= (1)^3 - 3(1) + 1 = 1 - 3 + 1 = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $f$ has a local maximum value of $\mathbf{3}$ at $x = -1$ and a local minimum value of $\mathbf{-1}$ at $x = 1$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö The Inconclusive Case ($f'' = 0$)

**Problem:** Classify the critical point of $f(x) = x^4$.

**Step 1: First Derivative**

$$
f'(x) = 4x^3
$$

**Step 2: Critical Point**

$$
4x^3 = 0 \implies x = 0
$$

**Step 3: Second Derivative**

$$
f''(x) = 12x^2
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate**

$$
f''(0) = 12(0)^2 = 0
$$

**Step 5: Test Fails!**

Since $f''(0) = 0$, the Second Derivative Test is **inconclusive**. We must fall back on the First Derivative Test or the higher-order test.

**Step 6: First Derivative Test as Fallback**

Since $f'(x) = 4x^3$:
- For $x < 0$: $f'(x) < 0$ (function is decreasing).
- For $x > 0$: $f'(x) > 0$ (function is increasing).

The function changes from decreasing to increasing, so $x = 0$ is a **local minimum** with $f(0) = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This example is a classic board-exam trap. The function $x^4$ has an obvious minimum at the origin, but the Second Derivative Test returns 0 and is inconclusive. Students who blindly conclude "inconclusive, so no extremum" lose **3 marks** outright.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Trigonometric Function

**Problem:** Find and classify the extrema of $f(x) = \sin(x) + \cos(x)$ on $[0, 2\pi]$.

**Step 1: First Derivative**

$$
f'(x) = \cos(x) - \sin(x)
$$

**Step 2: Critical Points**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos(x) - \sin(x) &= 0 \\
\cos(x) &= \sin(x) \\
\tan(x) &= 1 \\
x &= \frac{\pi}{4}, \; \frac{5\pi}{4} \quad \text{(in } [0, 2\pi]\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Second Derivative**

$$
f''(x) = -\sin(x) - \cos(x)
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) &= -\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) - \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = -\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} - \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = -\sqrt{2} \\
f''\left(\frac{5\pi}{4}\right) &= -\sin\left(\frac{5\pi}{4}\right) - \cos\left(\frac{5\pi}{4}\right) = -\left(-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) - \left(-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) = \sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the Test**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = -\sqrt{2} < 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Local Maximum at } x = \frac{\pi}{4} \\
f''\left(\frac{5\pi}{4}\right) = \sqrt{2} > 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Local Minimum at } x = \frac{5\pi}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Extreme Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) &= \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) + \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \sqrt{2} \\
f\left(\frac{5\pi}{4}\right) &= \sin\left(\frac{5\pi}{4}\right) + \cos\left(\frac{5\pi}{4}\right) = -\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} - \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = -\sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Local maximum value $\sqrt{2}$ at $x = \pi/4$; local minimum value $-\sqrt{2}$ at $x = 5\pi/4$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Function Type** | **$f'(c) = 0$ Solvable?** | **$f''(c) \neq 0$?** | **Test Outcome** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Polynomial (degree $\geq 2$) | Yes (algebraic) | Usually yes | Test succeeds |
| $x^4$, $x^3$, $x^n$ with $n \geq 4$ | Yes | $f''(0) = 0$ | Inconclusive ŌĆö use First Derivative Test |
| Trigonometric | Yes (periodic) | Usually yes | Test succeeds |
| Exponential / Logarithmic | Yes | Usually yes | Test succeeds |
| Piecewise / Absolute value | Corner exists | $f''$ does not exist | Test not applicable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart ŌĆö Decision Logic of the Second Derivative Test

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the exact decision logic a student must follow during the KTU board exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Given f of x"] --> B["Step 1 Compute f prime of x"]
    B --> C["Step 2 Solve f prime of x equals 0"]
    C --> D{"Critical points found?"}
    D -- "No" --> E["No local extrema via SDT"]
    D -- "Yes" --> F["Step 3 Compute f double prime of x"]
    F --> G["Step 4 Evaluate f double prime at each c"]
    G --> H{"Sign of f double prime of c?"}
    H -- "Greater than 0" --> I["Local MINIMUM at x equals c"]
    H -- "Less than 0" --> J["Local MAXIMUM at x equals c"]
    H -- "Equal to 0" --> K["SDT INCONCLUSIVE"]
    K --> L["Use First Derivative Test OR Higher Order Test"]
    I --> M["Compute extreme value f of c"]
    J --> M
    L --> N{"First Derivative Test result?"}
    N -- "Sign change negative to positive" --> I
    N -- "Sign change positive to negative" --> J
    N -- "No sign change" --> O["Saddle or Inflection Point"]
    M --> P["END: Report extrema"]
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Concavity Diagram ŌĆö Visual Intuition for the Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SM["Local Maximum Case f double prime less than 0"]
        A1["Curve concave DOWN"] --> A2["Tangent horizontal at peak"] --> A3["f double prime of c negative"]
    end
    subgraph SN["Local Minimum Case f double prime greater than 0"]
        B1["Curve concave UP"] --> B2["Tangent horizontal at valley"] --> B3["f double prime of c positive"]
    end
    subgraph SI["Inconclusive Case f double prime equals 0"]
        C1["Could be flat"] --> C2["Could be inflection"] --> C3["Could be extremum"]
        C3 --> C4["NEED further test"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Procedure Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Input: Function f of x and interval"] --> P2["Differentiate: f prime of x"]
    P2 --> P3["Stationary Points: solve f prime equals 0"]
    P3 --> P4["Differentiate again: f double prime of x"]
    P4 --> P5["Substitute c into f double prime"]
    P5 --> P6{"Classification Engine"}
    P6 -- "Positive output" --> P7["Output: Local Minimum Label"]
    P6 -- "Negative output" --> P8["Output: Local Maximum Label"]
    P6 -- "Zero output" --> P9["Output: Inconclusive Flag"]
    P9 --> P10["Redirect to First Derivative Engine"]
    P10 --> P6
    P7 --> P11["Final Answer Block: extrema f of c"]
    P8 --> P11
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
State the Second Derivative Test for local extrema. What happens when $f''(c) = 0$ at a critical point?

**Model Answer:**
The Second Derivative Test states that if $f'(c) = 0$ and $f''(c)$ exists at a critical point $c$, then:
- If $f''(c) > 0$, $f$ has a local minimum at $c$.
- If $f''(c) < 0$, $f$ has a local maximum at $c$.

When $f''(c) = 0$, the test is **inconclusive** and we must use the First Derivative Test or higher-order derivative test to classify the point. The function may have a local extremum, an inflection point, or no special behavior at $c$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**
Find the critical points of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 2$. Is the Second Derivative Test conclusive at each?

**Model Answer:**

$$
f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 3(x^2 - 4x + 3) = 3(x-1)(x-3)
$$

Setting $f'(x) = 0$ gives critical points $x = 1$ and $x = 3$.

$$
f''(x) = 6x - 12
$$

Evaluating:
- $f''(1) = 6(1) - 12 = -6 < 0$ ŌĆö Local Maximum (test is conclusive).
- $f''(3) = 6(3) - 12 = 6 > 0$ ŌĆö Local Minimum (test is conclusive).

Yes, the Second Derivative Test is conclusive at both critical points.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Find the local extrema of $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x - 3$ using the Second Derivative Test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the Second Derivative Test to find and classify the critical points of $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3 + 8$. Discuss the inconclusive case if any. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Compute $f'(x)$.

$$
f'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12 = 6(x^2 - 3x + 2) = 6(x-1)(x-2)
$$

**Step 2:** Critical points from $f'(x) = 0$:

$$
6(x-1)(x-2) = 0 \implies x = 1, \; x = 2
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $f''(x)$.

$$
f''(x) = 12x - 18
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate at each critical point.

$$
f''(1) = 12(1) - 18 = -6 < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Local Maximum}
$$

$$
f''(2) = 12(2) - 18 = 6 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Local Minimum}
$$

**Step 5:** Extreme values.

$$
f(1) = 2(1) - 9(1) + 12(1) - 3 = 2 - 9 + 12 - 3 = 2
$$

$$
f(2) = 2(8) - 9(4) + 12(2) - 3 = 16 - 36 + 24 - 3 = 1
$$

**Conclusion:** Local maximum value $\mathbf{2}$ at $x = 1$; local minimum value $\mathbf{1}$ at $x = 2$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Compute $f'(x)$.

$$
f'(x) = 4x^3 - 12x^2 = 4x^2(x - 3)
$$

**Step 2:** Critical points.

$$
4x^2(x-3) = 0 \implies x = 0, \; x = 3
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $f''(x)$.

$$
f''(x) = 12x^2 - 24x = 12x(x - 2)
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate.

$$
f''(0) = 12(0)(0-2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{INCONCLUSIVE}
$$

$$
f''(3) = 12(3)(3-2) = 36 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Local Minimum}
$$

**Step 5:** Discuss the inconclusive case at $x = 0$ using the First Derivative Test.

Since $f'(x) = 4x^2(x-3)$:
- For $x < 0$: $f'(x) = 4(\text{positive})(\text{negative}) < 0$.
- For $0 < x < 3$: $f'(x) = 4(\text{positive})(\text{negative}) < 0$.

The sign of $f'$ does not change across $x = 0$, so $x = 0$ is **not a local extremum** ŌĆö it is an inflection point.

**Step 6:** Extreme value at $x = 3$.

$$
f(3) = (3)^4 - 4(3)^3 + 8 = 81 - 108 + 8 = -19
$$

**Conclusion:** Local minimum value $\mathbf{-19}$ at $x = 3$; $x = 0$ is an inflection point (SDT inconclusive, resolved by FDT).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023]**

**(a)** Using the Second Derivative Test, find the local extrema of $f(x) = e^x (x^2 - 3x)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the local maximum and minimum values of $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 1}{x^2 + 1}$ using the Second Derivative Test. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Use the product rule.

$$
f'(x) = e^x(x^2 - 3x) + e^x(2x - 3) = e^x\left[(x^2 - 3x) + (2x - 3)\right] = e^x(x^2 - x - 3)
$$

**Step 2:** Critical points. Since $e^x \neq 0$ for all $x$:

$$
x^2 - x - 3 = 0 \implies x = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 12}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{13}}{2}
$$

So $x_1 = \dfrac{1 - \sqrt{13}}{2} \approx -1.303$ and $x_2 = \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{13}}{2} \approx 2.303$.

**Step 3:** Compute $f''(x)$ using the product rule on $f'(x) = e^x(x^2 - x - 3)$:

$$
f''(x) = e^x(x^2 - x - 3) + e^x(2x - 1) = e^x\left[(x^2 - x - 3) + (2x - 1)\right] = e^x(x^2 + x - 4)
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate.

$$
f''(x_1) = e^{x_1}\left(x_1^2 + x_1 - 4\right)
$$

Since $x_1 = \dfrac{1 - \sqrt{13}}{2}$, we have $x_1^2 = \dfrac{1 - 2\sqrt{13} + 13}{4} = \dfrac{14 - 2\sqrt{13}}{4} = \dfrac{7 - \sqrt{13}}{2}$.

$$
x_1^2 + x_1 - 4 = \frac{7 - \sqrt{13}}{2} + \frac{1 - \sqrt{13}}{2} - 4 = \frac{8 - 2\sqrt{13}}{2} - 4 = 4 - \sqrt{13} - 4 = -\sqrt{13}
$$

Thus $f''(x_1) = e^{x_1} \cdot (-\sqrt{13}) < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Local Maximum** at $x_1$.

$$
x_2^2 = \frac{7 + \sqrt{13}}{2}
$$

$$
x_2^2 + x_2 - 4 = \frac{7 + \sqrt{13}}{2} + \frac{1 + \sqrt{13}}{2} - 4 = 4 + \sqrt{13} - 4 = \sqrt{13}
$$

Thus $f''(x_2) = e^{x_2} \cdot \sqrt{13} > 0 \Rightarrow$ **Local Minimum** at $x_2$.

**Conclusion:** Local maximum at $x = \dfrac{1 - \sqrt{13}}{2}$; local minimum at $x = \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{13}}{2}$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Apply the quotient rule.

$$
f'(x) = \frac{(2x)(x^2+1) - (x^2-1)(2x)}{(x^2+1)^2} = \frac{2x\left[(x^2+1) - (x^2-1)\right]}{(x^2+1)^2} = \frac{2x \cdot 2}{(x^2+1)^2} = \frac{4x}{(x^2+1)^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Critical point.

$$
\frac{4x}{(x^2+1)^2} = 0 \implies 4x = 0 \implies x = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $f''(x)$ using the quotient rule on $f'(x) = \dfrac{4x}{(x^2+1)^2}$.

$$
f''(x) = \frac{4(x^2+1)^2 - 4x \cdot 2(x^2+1)(2x)}{(x^2+1)^4} = \frac{4(x^2+1) - 16x^2}{(x^2+1)^3} = \frac{4 - 12x^2}{(x^2+1)^3}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate at $x = 0$.

$$
f''(0) = \frac{4 - 0}{1^3} = 4 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Local Minimum}
$$

**Step 5:** Extreme value.

$$
f(0) = \frac{0 - 1}{0 + 1} = -1
$$

**Conclusion:** Local minimum value $\mathbf{-1}$ at $x = 0$. (No local maximum exists since $f(x) \to 1$ as $x \to \pm\infty$ ŌĆö the horizontal asymptote is at $y = 1$, but it is never attained.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $f(c)$:** Many students stop after classifying the type of extremum and forget to compute the actual extreme value $f(c)$. This costs **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing "inconclusive" with "no extremum":** When $f''(c) = 0$, the test fails ŌĆö it does **not** prove the absence of an extremum. Always fall back to the First Derivative Test.
> 3. **Sign errors in the quotient rule:** The derivative of $(x^2+1)^{-2}$ is $-2(x^2+1)^{-3} \cdot 2x = -4x(x^2+1)^{-3}$. Sloppy handling of the chain rule costs full marks.
> 4. **Applying the test at points where $f'(c) \neq 0$:** The Second Derivative Test is **only** for stationary points. Applying it at non-stationary points is logically invalid.
> 5. **Endpoint oversight on closed intervals:** For closed interval $[a,b]$, local extrema can also occur at endpoints. These are classified using **one-sided derivatives**, not the Second Derivative Test.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Second Derivative Test**
> - **Prerequisite:** $f$ must be twice differentiable on an open interval around $c$, and $f'(c) = 0$.
> - **Positive $f''(c)$** $\Rightarrow$ concave up $\Rightarrow$ **Local Minimum** (the curve "holds water").
> - **Negative $f''(c)$** $\Rightarrow$ concave down $\Rightarrow$ **Local Maximum** (the curve "spills water").
> - **Zero $f''(c)$** $\Rightarrow$ **Inconclusive** ŌĆö fall back to First Derivative Test or Higher-Order Test.
> - The test gives **local** classification only; for **global** extrema, compare $f(c)$ values across all candidates.
> - For **closed intervals** $[a, b]$, also check endpoints $a$ and $b$ separately.
> - Inconclusive does **not** mean no extremum ŌĆö it means the test is silent and you must investigate further.
> - Always report the **type** (max/min) **and the value** $f(c)$ for full marks.
> - Connection to engineering: this is the 1-D version of the **Hessian test** in multivariable optimization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Absolute Maxima and Minima on Closed Bounded Regions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE ŌĆō 1
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Absolute Maxima and Minima on Closed Bounded Regions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

Let $f : D \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on a **closed and bounded region** $D$ (also called a **compact** set in $\mathbb{R}^2$).

- A point $(x_0, y_0) \in D$ is called an **absolute maximum** of $f$ on $D$ if
$$f(x_0, y_0) \geq f(x, y) \quad \forall \, (x, y) \in D.$$

- A point $(x_1, y_1) \in D$ is called an **absolute minimum** of $f$ on $D$ if
$$f(x_1, y_1) \leq f(x, y) \quad \forall \, (x, y) \in D.$$

These two values are collectively known as the **absolute extrema** (or **global extrema**) of $f$ on $D$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Extreme Value Theorem (Two-Variable Form):** If $f$ is **continuous** on a **closed and bounded** region $D \subset \mathbb{R}^2$, then $f$ **must attain** both an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on $D$. The absolute extrema can occur either in the *interior* of $D$ or on the *boundary* $\partial D$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Hiking on an Island" Intuition

Imagine you go hiking on a small, fenced, closed island (this is your **closed bounded region** $D$). The height of the terrain at any point is given by a smooth, continuous function $f(x, y)$ representing elevation.

- The **island** is the closed bounded region $D$ (the fence guarantees *closed*; the finite size guarantees *bounded*).
- The **continuous terrain** guarantees that heights change smoothly ŌĆö no sudden cliffs cut out of the map.
- The Extreme Value Theorem guarantees: somewhere on this island, there is a **highest peak** (absolute maximum) and a **lowest valley** (absolute minimum). You will always be able to find them.

Now, where can these two special points be located?

- They could be **inside the island** ŌĆö at the top of an interior mountain or bottom of an interior crater. Mathematically, these correspond to **critical points** in the interior where $\nabla f = (0, 0)$.
- They could be **right on the fence line** (the boundary $\partial D$) ŌĆö for instance, a peak happens to sit exactly on the coastal edge. Mathematically, these are found by parameterizing the boundary and reducing the problem to a single-variable optimization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 specifically tests the **two-step procedure** for finding absolute extrema:
> **Step 1** ŌĆö Find and evaluate all *interior* critical points.
> **Step 2** ŌĆö Find and evaluate the absolute extrema on the *boundary*.
> The largest value among all candidates is the absolute maximum; the smallest is the absolute minimum.

## 1.3 Standard Geometric Examples of Closed Bounded Regions

- A **closed rectangle**: $D = \{(x, y) : a \leq x \leq b, \, c \leq y \leq d\}$.
- A **closed disk**: $D = \{(x, y) : (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 \leq r^2\}$.
- A **closed triangle** or any other **closed polygon** in the plane.
- A **closed elliptical region**: $D = \{(x, y) : \frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} \leq 1\}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Step Procedure (Logic Breakdown)

The KTU board examiner expects the procedure written in this exact logical sequence. Memorize the structure.

### **Step 1 ŌĆö Interior Critical Points**

Find all points $(x, y)$ in the **interior** of $D$ where one of the following holds:

- **Condition A (First Derivative Test):** Both first-order partial derivatives vanish:
$$f_x(x, y) = 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad f_y(x, y) = 0.$$

- **Condition B (Non-differentiability):** Either $f_x$ or $f_y$ fails to exist at $(x, y)$.

Compute the function value $f(x, y)$ at every such interior critical point. These are **candidate values**.

### **Step 2 ŌĆö Boundary Analysis**

The boundary $\partial D$ is typically a curve (line segment, circle, ellipse, etc.). We reduce the 2-variable problem to a 1-variable problem by **parameterizing the boundary**.

- **For a line segment** from $(x_1, y_1)$ to $(x_2, y_2)$: Use $x = x_1 + t(x_2 - x_1)$, $y = y_1 + t(y_2 - y_1)$ for $t \in [0, 1]$.
- **For a circle** $x^2 + y^2 = r^2$: Use $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$ for $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$.
- **For an ellipse** $\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$: Use $x = a\cos\theta$, $y = b\sin\theta$ for $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$.

Substitute into $f$ to get a single-variable function $g(t)$ or $g(\theta)$. Apply the standard one-variable absolute extrema method on a closed interval:

- Find critical points where $g'(t) = 0$.
- Evaluate $g$ at critical points and at the endpoints of the parameter interval.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility ŌĆö Why does this matter?**
> This exact two-step procedure is the mathematical backbone of:
> - **Resource optimization** in operations research (maximizing profit subject to a closed feasible region).
> - **Heat dissipation** problems in mechanical engineering (maximizing temperature gradient over a finite plate).
> - **Machine-learning loss landscapes** restricted to bounded parameter boxes.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why check interior critical points?** Because a smooth hilltop or valley floor *inside* the region can only occur where the gradient vanishes (Fermat's theorem for two variables). The partial derivatives being zero is the only way the function can locally turn around in both the $x$ and $y$ directions.
- **Why check the boundary?** A global maximum on a bounded domain can sit *on the edge*. The boundary is a 1-D curve, so we use single-variable calculus to climb or descend along it.
- **Why are these the *only* places to look?** By the Extreme Value Theorem, the global max and min must exist. They cannot occur at non-critical interior points (since a function is still climbing or descending there) and they cannot be missed on the boundary (since the boundary is a compact 1-D set on which a continuous restriction also attains extrema).
